EDCS-06000529.text_edition: Proscaenium  columnis et marmoribus  ex HS CCducentibus milibus a Marcio Vitale itemque ex HS CCCtrecentibus milibus a Iunio Galba in eam rem  datis item tetrastylis lacunarum  pecunia publica exornatum dedicatum est Lucio Hedio Rufo Lolliano Avito  proconsule Caio Vibio Gallione Claudio Severo legato
EDCS-06000529.translation: The proscaenium, adorned with columns and marble, costing 200,000 sesterces donated by Marcius Vitalis, and likewise 300,000 sesterces given for the same purpose by Junius Galba, and the tetrastyle lacunaria, were embellished with public money and dedicated when Lucius Hedius Rufus Lollianus Avitus was proconsul and Gaius Vibius Gallio Claudius Severus was legatus.
EDCS-06000529.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a theater's proscaenium and tetrastyle lacunaria, funded by private donations and public money, during the magistracies of Lucius Hedius Rufus Lollianus Avitus (proconsul) and Gaius Vibius Gallio Claudius Severus (legate).

EDCS-06000532.text_edition: Proculus legatus pro praetore dedicavit Caius Maecius Cnaei filius Falerna Flaccus sua pecunia fecit
EDCS-06000532.translation: Proculus, legatus pro praetore, dedicated this. Gaius Maecius, son of Gnaeus, of the Falerna tribe, Flaccus, made it with his own money.
EDCS-06000532.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Proculus, a legate, which was built at the expense of Gaius Maecius Flaccus.

EDCS-06000534.text_edition: Caius Rubellius Blandus proconsul  Marcus Etrilius Lupercus legatus pro praetore
EDCS-06000534.translation: Gaius Rubellius Blandus, proconsul. Marcus Etrilius Lupercus, legatus pro praetore.
EDCS-06000534.summary: This inscription lists the names of Gaius Rubellius Blandus, a proconsul, and Marcus Etrilius Lupercus, a legatus pro praetore, likely indicating their tenure in the province.

EDCS-06000537.text_edition: Curni  Lucio Volusio Basso Caereali clarissimo viro et consulari curatori rei publicae suae quae sic inter cetera opera quae industria et labore maximo renovavit Ulpiam basilicam cum ORVS in meliorem faciem publico sumptu restituit gratiam afferentes Lepcitani publice  Λέπκις ἡ Μεγάλη φιλ(ε)ῖ σε Ῥώμη  Genio coloniae Lepcis Magnae Crescentina fecit
EDCS-06000537.translation: To Curnius, Lucius Volusius Bassus Caerealis, most distinguished man and consular curator of his own state, who among other works which he renewed with utmost industry and effort, restored the Ulpian basilica with its ORVS to a better condition at public expense. The people of Lepcis express their gratitude publicly. 'Great Lepcis loves you, Rome.' Crescentina made this for the Genius of the colony of Lepcis Magna.
EDCS-06000537.summary: This inscription is a public dedication by the citizens of Lepcis Magna to Lucius Volusius Bassus Caerealis, a distinguished official who restored the Ulpian basilica, and also includes a dedication to the Genius of the colony by Crescentina.

EDCS-06000538.text_edition: Lucio Volusio Basso Cereali clarissimo viro legato totius innocentiae et aequitatis et consimilis moderationis viro Lepcimagnenses ex decreto ordinis municipi et patrono perpetuo
EDCS-06000538.translation: To Lucius Volusius Bassus Cerealis, most distinguished man, legate of all innocence and fairness, and a man of similar moderation, the people of Lepcis Magna, by decree of the municipal order, and as their perpetual patron.
EDCS-06000538.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the people of Lepcis Magna, by municipal decree, to Lucius Volusius Bassus Cerealis, a distinguished legate, honoring him as their perpetual patron.

EDCS-06000553.text_edition: Dulciti  Aurelio Sempronio Sereno equiti Romano principali Alexandriae ob insigne et admirabile studiorum ingenium praeter deccurionatuum honorem et statuam apud se conlocatam etiam apud Lepcimagnenses    clarissimo viro agenti vices praefectorum praetorio per Africanas provincias Lepcimagnensis ordo et populus ut incomparabilium beneficiorum eius memoria etiam ad posteros mitteretur praeter hKospitalem#hospitalem#KOSPITALEM tesseram etiam statuam marmoream constituendam esse duxerunt ut servati moderatione iudiciorum eius ac multis tempestatibus to
EDCS-06000553.translation: To Dulcitius Aurelius Sempronius Serenus, Roman knight, principal of Alexandria, on account of his remarkable and admirable intellectual talents, beyond the honor of a decurion and a statue placed in his honor, also among the people of Lepcis Magna, for the most distinguished man acting as deputy praetorian prefect for the African provinces. The order and people of Lepcis Magna decided that a marble statue should be erected, in addition to the tessera hospitalis, so that the memory of his incomparable benefits would be passed down to posterity, having been preserved by his moderation in judgments and through many storms.
EDCS-06000553.summary: This inscription is a public dedication by the people of Lepcis Magna to Dulcitius Aurelius Sempronius Serenus, a distinguished Roman knight and deputy praetorian prefect, acknowledging his intellectual talents, moderation, and beneficial actions, and dedicating a marble statue in his honor.

EDCS-06000555.text_edition: Consentii  Lucio Domitio Iusto Aemiliano equiti Romano viro perfectissimo curatori rei publicae ob eximium amorem in patriam et cives ex decreto ordinis et suffragiis populi civi karissimo Lepcitani publice  Quintii flaminis perpetui sacerdotalis  Ob meritum laboris et legationis perfectae Lucio Aemilio Quinto flamini perpetuo sacerdotali ordo splendidissimus civitatis Lepcimagnensium dedit
EDCS-06000555.translation: To Consentius Lucius Domitius Justus Aemilianus, Roman knight, most perfect man, curator of the state, for his exceptional love for his homeland and citizens, by decree of the order and votes of the people, a most beloved citizen, the people of Lepcis publicly honor him. To Quintius, perpetual flamen sacerdotal. For the merit of his labor and accomplished legation, the most splendid order of the city of Lepcis Magna gave this to Lucius Aemilius Quintus, perpetual flamen sacerdotal.
EDCS-06000555.summary: This inscription is a double dedication: first, a public honor by Lepcis to Consentius Lucius Domitius Justus Aemilianus for his patriotism, and second, a dedication by the municipal order to Lucius Aemilius Quintus, a perpetual flamen, for his distinguished service.

EDCS-06000558.text_edition: Heraclii  Benignissimo viro principali prudentissimo et integeIrrimo#integerrimo#INTEGIRR Tito Flavio Frontino Heraclio viro perfectissimo auguri sacerdoti Laurentium Labinatium sic IIviro ob diversarum voluptatum exhibitiones adque admirabilem ludorum editionem amoremque incomparabilem in patriam et cives suos sufragio quietissimi populi et decreto splendidissimi ordinis
EDCS-06000558.translation: To Heraclius, the most benevolent, most prudent, and most upright principal man, Titus Flavius Frontinus Heraclius, most perfect man, augur, priest of Laurentium Lavinatium, also duumvir, on account of the various pleasures exhibited and the admirable staging of games, and his incomparable love for his homeland and citizens, by suffrage of the most peaceful people and decree of the most splendid order.
EDCS-06000558.summary: This inscription is a public dedication by the people and order of Lepcis to Titus Flavius Frontinus Heraclius, a distinguished local magistrate, honoring him for his benevolence, wisdom, and the public entertainments he provided.

EDCS-06000559.text_edition: Nepotianii  Flavio Nepotiano viro perfectissimo comiti et praesidi provinciae Tripolitanae  iustitia et integritati sic praecipuo moderatione ac benignitate praestantissimo abstinentiae et honestatis officiorumque omnium cultori rei etiam militaris peritissimo armis consiliisque inconparabili sic quod idem ordines universosque provinciales iuridicendo fide benivolentia multis beneficiis cumulaverit quod barbarorum insolentiam exercitio scientiae militaris adtriberit quod limitis defensionem tuitionemque perpetuam futuris etiam temporibus munitam securamque ab omni hostile incursione praestiterit quod civitatum moenia operum instauratione vel novitate decoraverit ordo civitatis Lepcimagnensis cum populo statuam marmoream ob haec merita decretis et sufragiis concinnentibus conlocavit
EDCS-06000559.translation: To Nepotianus, Flavius Nepotianus, most perfect man, comes and governor of the province of Tripolitana, preeminent in justice and integrity, outstanding in moderation and benevolence, a cultivator of abstinence and honesty and all duties, also most skilled in military affairs, incomparable in arms and counsel, because he has enriched all orders and all provincials with faithfulness, benevolence, and many benefits through his administration of justice; because he has crushed the insolence of the barbarians through the exercise of military knowledge; because he has ensured the defense and perpetual security of the frontier from all hostile incursions for future times; because he has adorned the walls of cities by the restoration or novelty of works; the order of the city of Lepcis Magna with the people erected a marble statue for these merits, with harmonious decrees and suffrages.
EDCS-06000559.summary: This inscription records the erection of a marble statue by the city of Lepcis Magna and its people to Flavius Nepotianus, the governor of Tripolitana, in recognition of his exceptional justice, military prowess, protection of the frontier, and urban improvements.

EDCS-06000560.text_edition: Petasii  Eximiae integritatis et inconparabilis prudentiae ac totius bonitatis viro Flavio Petasio viro perfectissimo ex praesidibus provinciae Tripolitanae ordo splendidissimae civitatis Lepcimagnensis decreto suo et populi sufragiis memor meritorum in se ab eius praestantia conlatorum statuam in foro novo Severiano ad perpetuitatis memoriam patrono dignissimo  posuit
EDCS-06000560.translation: To Flavius Petasius, a man of exceptional integrity, incomparable prudence, and complete goodness, a most perfect man, formerly governor of the province of Tripolitana, the order of the most splendid city of Lepcis Magna, by its decree and the votes of the people, mindful of the merits bestowed upon it by his excellence, placed this statue in the new Severan forum, as a memorial of perpetuity, to a most worthy patron.
EDCS-06000560.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue by the city of Lepcis Magna to Flavius Petasius, a former governor of Tripolitana, in recognition of his virtues and merits, likely dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-06000561.text_edition: Heraclii  Uno eodemque anno duumviro Lepcimagnensium et sacerdoti provinciae Tripolitanae innocentissimo viro principali integerrimo amatori patriae ac civium suorum Tito Flavio Vibiano viro perfectissimo flamini perpetuo et pontifici curatori rei publicae Lepcimagnensium sacerdoti Laurentium Labinatium sic et sacerdoti Matris Deum praefecto omnium sacrorum ob diversarum voluptatum exhibitionem et Libycarum ferarum X  ex populi sufragio et ordin(is) decreto
EDCS-06000561.translation: To Heraclius, in the same year, twice duumvir of the people of Lepcis Magna and most innocent priest of the province of Tripolitana, a most upright principal man, a lover of his homeland and his fellow citizens, Titus Flavius Vibianus, a most perfect man, perpetual flamen and pontiff, curator of the public affairs of Lepcis Magna, priest of Laurentius Lavinatium, and also priest of the Mother of the Gods, prefect of all sacred rites, on account of the exhibition of various entertainments and ten Libyan wild beasts, by the vote of the people and the decree of the order.
EDCS-06000561.summary: This inscription commemorates Titus Flavius Vibianus, a prominent citizen and priest of Lepcis Magna, honoring him for his public service and lavish games, likely set up in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-06000567.text_edition: Romulii  ob tantam moderationem iudiciorum provisionesque saluberrimas adque felicitatem adventus eius Laenatio Romulo viro perfectissimo praesidi provinciae Tripolitanae sufragio quietissimi populi et  decreto splendidissimi ordinis sumptibus suis posuit
EDCS-06000567.translation: On account of such great moderation in judgments and most salutary provisions and the happiness of his arrival, to Laenatius Romulus, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Tripolitana, by the suffrage of the most peaceful people and the decree of the most splendid order, he placed this at his own expense.
EDCS-06000567.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument, paid for by the dedicator himself, to Laenatius Romulus, governor of Tripolitana, in honor of his just administration and arrival, likely dating to the 3rd or 4th century AD.

EDCS-06000568.text_edition: Flavianii  Omnium virtutum experientissimo viro ingenitae mansuetudinis et benignitate patientiae pollenti iudiciorum aequissimo moderatori antistiti innocentiae Magnio Aspero Flaviano viro perfectissimo praesidi provinciae Tripolitanae Lepcimagnenses ob eius amorem in se conlatum et integritatem perpensae sic sibi cognitam patrono dignissimo famae et gloriae perpetuum testimonium posuerunt
EDCS-06000568.translation: To Magnio Asper Flavianus, a man most experienced in all virtues, possessing innate gentleness and benevolent patience, a most just moderator of judgments, a guardian of innocence, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Tripolitana, the people of Lepcis Magna, on account of his love bestowed upon them and his integrity so known to them, erected this as a perpetual testimony to his fame and glory for a most worthy patron.
EDCS-06000568.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by the people of Lepcis Magna to Magnio Asper Flavianus, governor of Tripolitana, in praise of his virtues and integrity as their patron, likely dating to the 3rd or 4th century AD.

EDCS-06000569.text_edition: Inachii  Omnium virtutum viro Publicio Valeriano viro perfectissimo ex praesidibus singularis integritatis et moderationis Lepcimagnenses patrono praestantissimo posuerunt
EDCS-06000569.translation: To Publicius Valerianus, a man of all virtues, a most perfect man, formerly governor of singular integrity and moderation, the people of Lepcis Magna erected this to a most outstanding patron.
EDCS-06000569.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by the citizens of Lepcis Magna to Publicius Valerianus, a former governor, in honor of his exceptional character and patronage, likely dating to the 3rd or 4th century AD.

EDCS-06000570.text_edition: Obsequii  Caio Valerio Vibiano viro perfectissimo praesidi provinciae Tripolitanae singularis aequitatis et beniboli sic vicoris sic M omnium virtutum viro ob tunc incipientem deinceps iugem erga se eius dignationem ordo splendidissimus Lepcimagnensium et populus patrono ex decreto splendidissimi ordinis posuerunt
EDCS-06000570.translation: To Gaius Valerius Vibianus, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Tripolitana, a man of singular equity and good will, and victorious, and a man of all virtues, on account of his then beginning and thereafter continuous regard for them, the most splendid order of Lepcis Magna and the people erected this to their patron by decree of the most splendid order.
EDCS-06000570.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the city of Lepcis Magna and its people to Gaius Valerius Vibianus, governor of Tripolitana, celebrating his fairness and continued favor, likely dating to the 3rd or 4th century AD.

EDCS-06000571.text_edition: Amelii  Multiplici laborum merito varioque voluptatum genere stimulantibus paternis avitiis etiam documentis ab ineunte aetate patriam civesque suos promerenti Marco Vibio Anniano Gemino viro perfectissimo flamini perpetuo pontifici sacerdotali provinciae Tripolitanae bis IIviro ex sufragio quietissimi populi et decreto splendidissimi ordinis
EDCS-06000571.translation: To Marcus Vibius Annianus Geminus, a most perfect man, perpetual flamen, pontiff, sacerdotal priest of the province of Tripolitana, twice duumvir, who by manifold merit of labors and various kinds of pleasures, stimulated by paternal and ancestral examples, from an early age deserved well of his homeland and his fellow citizens, by the vote of the most peaceful people and the decree of the most splendid order.
EDCS-06000571.summary: This inscription commemorates Marcus Vibius Annianus Geminus, a prominent religious and political figure in Tripolitana and Lepcis Magna, honoring him for his extensive public service and virtues, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-06000572.text_edition: Aemiliae Lychnidi Clementillae Aemili Arriani Caeciliani duoviralici pontificis filiae Lucius Volusius Gallus eques Romanus pontifex duoviralis flamen perpetuus ob insignem adfectionis castitatem matri Volusiorum Appianillae Bassi et Cerealis filliorum suorum permissu splendidissimi ordinis posuit
EDCS-06000572.translation: To Aemilia Lychnis Clementilla, daughter of Aemilius Arrianus Caecilianus, a duumviral pontiff; Lucius Volusius Gallus, a Roman knight, pontiff, duumviral, perpetual flamen, by permission of the most splendid order, placed this for the mother of his sons Volusius Appianilla Bassus and Cerealis, on account of her remarkable affection and chastity.
EDCS-06000572.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Lucius Volusius Gallus to his wife, Aemilia Lychnis Clementilla, honoring her as the mother of his sons and praising her chastity and affection, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-06000580.text_edition: Caio Aelio Rufino flamini Aquilia Blaesilla mater filio piissimo
EDCS-06000580.translation: To Gaius Aelius Rufinus, flamen. Aquilia Blaesilla, his mother, to her most dutiful son.
EDCS-06000580.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Aquilia Blaesilla to her son, Gaius Aelius Rufinus, who held the priestly office of flamen, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-06000583.text_edition: Tiberius Claudius Amicus Marcus Heliodorius Apollonides aediles mensas pecunia sua dono dederunt
EDCS-06000583.translation: Tiberius Claudius Amicus and Marcus Heliodorius Apollonides, aediles, gave these tables as a gift with their own money.
EDCS-06000583.summary: This inscription records a public benefaction by two aediles, Tiberius Claudius Amicus and Marcus Heliodorius Apollonides, who donated tables using their own funds, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-06000602.text_edition: Amatori patriae et civium suorum quod ex indulgentia sacra civibus suis feras dentatas quattuor vivas donavit ex decreto splendidissimi ordinis bigam decreverunt Porfyri Porfyri
EDCS-06000602.translation: To the lover of his homeland and his fellow citizens, because by sacred indulgence he donated four live, toothed beasts to his fellow citizens, the most splendid order decreed a biga. Porfyri, Porfyri decreed this.
EDCS-06000602.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a biga (two-horse chariot) by the city council and individuals Porfyri, Porfyri to an unnamed benefactor who donated four live wild animals to the citizens, likely for spectacles.

EDCS-06000625.text_edition: Cnae-- Calpurni   ex pecunia publica O
EDCS-06000625.summary: This fragment likely records a public work or dedication funded by public money, possibly overseen by or dedicated to Gnaeus Calpurnius.

EDCS-06000678.translation: To Calliepi, for Publius Cornelius Saecularis.
EDCS-06000678.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication by Calliepi to Publius Cornelius Saecularis, possibly a funerary dedication or an honorific.

EDCS-06000683.text_edition: []  P[]  thi  Presbyti  Geronti  Euphrasi  Patricii viri clarissimi  Virtute praestanti aequitate miravili sic temperantia moderato sic defensori iustitiae innocentium vindici Flavio Macedonio Patricio viro clarissimo comiti et duci provinciae Tripolitanae ordo splendidus et populus universus Lepcis Magnae civitatis digno patrono decrevit adque constituit
EDCS-06000683.translation: To P[]thi Presbyti Geronti Euphrasi Patricius, a most distinguished man. To Flavius Macedonius Patricius, a most distinguished man, count and dux of the province of Tripolitana, for his outstanding virtue, admirable equity, tempered moderation, defender of justice, avenger of the innocent, the splendid order and the entire people of the city of Lepcis Magna decreed and established this to a worthy patron.
EDCS-06000683.summary: This inscription is an elaborate honorific decree by the city council and people of Lepcis Magna to Flavius Macedonius Patricius, a distinguished count and dux, praising his virtues and acknowledging him as a patron.

EDCS-06000694.text_edition: Dis Manibus Fulviae Crescentillae uxoris sanctissimae Quintus Marcius Candidus Rusonianus maritus fecit
EDCS-06000694.translation: To the Divine Manes of Fulvia Crescentilla, his most holy wife, Quintus Marcius Candidus Rusonianus, her husband, made this.
EDCS-06000694.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the dedication of a tomb or monument by Quintus Marcius Candidus Rusonianus to his deceased wife, Fulvia Crescentilla.

EDCS-06000798.text_edition: Celso II et Marcello II consulibus
EDCS-06000798.translation: In the consulship of Celsus II and Marcellus II.
EDCS-06000798.summary: This inscription provides a precise date, referring to the second consulship of Celsus and Marcellus, which corresponds to the year 204 CE.

EDCS-06000810.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely part of a longer text, possibly mentioning a name like Calistus or a related term.

EDCS-06000822.text_edition: FRV  fecerunt de c MK VALCI   ETAT coll
EDCS-06000822.translation: FRV made this from the public money. MK VALCI [...] age [...] collected.
EDCS-06000822.summary: This fragment suggests a public work or dedication made from public funds, possibly by or for individuals whose names are partially preserved, and possibly mentioning their age or a collection.

EDCS-06000825.summary: This extremely fragmentary inscription contains only a few letters and is too incomplete to provide a meaningful summary of its original content.

EDCS-06000860.text_edition: Dis Manibus Quinti Aemili Frontonis Massenaganis filius
EDCS-06000860.translation: To the Divine Manes of Quintus Aemilius Frontonis, son of Massenaganis.
EDCS-06000860.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Quintus Aemilius Frontonis, explicitly naming his father Massenaganis.

EDCS-06000864.text_edition: orum Geta et Roae et Cea Domitius C et Casana quae et gyddus es filis suis simis
EDCS-06000864.translation: Of Geta and Roa and Cea, Domitius C. and Casana, who is also Gyddus, for their dearest children.
EDCS-06000864.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely for a tomb, dedicated by Domitius C. and Casana (also known as Gyddus) to their beloved children Geta, Roa, and Cea.

EDCS-06000865.text_edition: Ex auctoritate Imperatoris divi Vespasiani filii Domitiani Augusti Germanici pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate VI imperatoris XIIII consulis XIII censoris perpetui patris patriae iussu Suelli Flacci legati Augusti pro praetore terminus positi sic inter nationem sic Muduciuviorum et Zamuciorum ex conventione utrarumque nationum
EDCS-06000865.translation: By the authority of the divine Emperor Vespasian's son, Domitian Augustus Germanicus, Pontifex Maximus, in his 6th tribunician power, 14th acclamation as Imperator, 13th consulship, perpetual censor, father of his country; by order of Suellius Flaccus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, a boundary stone was placed between the nation of the Muduciuvi and the Zamuci, by agreement of both nations.
EDCS-06000865.summary: This inscription records the placement of a boundary stone, marking the territory between the Muduciuvi and Zamuci nations, executed by order of Domitian, likely around 87-88 CE, and overseen by his legate Suellius Flaccus.

EDCS-06000899.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute et victoria dominorum nostrorum Imperatorum Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Augusti nostri filii et [Publi] [Septimi Getae] Augusti Augusti nostri filii Augusti nostri fratris et Iuliae Augustae matris castrorum Marcus Caninius Adiutor Faustinianus praefectus cohortis II Hamiorum praepositus vexillatione legionis III Augustae piae vindicis aram posuit et dedicavit
EDCS-06000899.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for the health and victory of our lords, the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, our Augustus's son, and Publius Septimius Geta Augustus, our Augustus's son and our Augustus's brother, and Julia Augusta, mother of the camps; Marcus Caninius Adiutor Faustinianus, prefect of the Second Cohort of Hamii, in charge of a detachment of the Third Augustan Legion, Pious and Vindicator, placed and dedicated this altar.
EDCS-06000899.summary: This altar inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus by Marcus Caninius Adiutor Faustinianus, prefect of Cohors II Hamiorum, for the well-being and victory of the Severan imperial family, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-06000913.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Invictus Augustus et Marcus Iulius Philippus Caesar noster regionem limitis Tentheitani partitam et eius viam incursibus barbarorum constituto novo centenario  -AS praecluserunt Cominio Cassiano legato Augustorum pro praetore Gallicano viro egregio praeposito limitis cura Numisii Maximi domo sia tibuni
EDCS-06000913.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Invictus Augustus and Marcus Iulius Philippus Caesar, our Caesar, closed off the divided region of the Tentheitan frontier and its road against barbarian incursions by establishing a new centenarium. This was done under Cominius Cassianus, legate of the Augusti with propraetorian power, an excellent man, and under Gallicanus, in charge of the frontier, with the care of Numisius Maximus, from his home, a tribune.
EDCS-06000913.summary: This inscription records the restoration and fortification of a road and frontier region against barbarian incursions by Emperors Philip the Arab and his son Philip II, overseen by their legates Cominius Cassianus and Gallicanus, likely dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-06000940.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate X imperatori XI consuli III patri patriae et Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate III consuli et Lucio Septimio GetaeCaesari Augusto per vexillationem legionis III Augustae
EDCS-06000940.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus, Pontifex Maximus, in his 10th tribunician power, 11th acclamation as Imperator, 3rd consulship, father of his country; and to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, in his 3rd tribunician power, consul; and to Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus, by a detachment of the Third Augustan Legion.
EDCS-06000940.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Severan emperors: Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, erected by a detachment of Legio III Augusta, likely dating to 202-203 CE.

EDCS-06000941.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto tribunicia potestate IX imperatori XI consuli II patri patriae et Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate [IIII et Lucio] Septimio Getae Caesari Augusto Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augustorum consulari legionis III Augustae piae vindicis
EDCS-06000941.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, in his 9th tribunician power, 11th acclamation as Imperator, 2nd consulship, father of his country; and to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, in his 4th tribunician power; and to Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus; under Quintus Anicius Faustus, consular legate of the Third Augustan Legion, Pious and Vindicator.
EDCS-06000941.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Severan emperors: Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, overseen by Quintus Anicius Faustus, the consular legate of Legio III Augusta, likely dating to 201 CE.

EDCS-06000943.text_edition: Quaesii multum quot sic memoriae tradere agens prae cunctos in hac castra milites votum communem sic proque reditu exercitus inter priores et futuros reddere dum quaero mecum digna divom nomina inveni tandem nomen et numen deae votis perennem sic quem sic dicare in hoc loco salutis igitur quandium sic cultores sient qua potui sanxi nomen et cunctis dedi veras salutis lymphas tantis ignibus in istis semper harenacis sic collibus nutantis austeri solis flammas fervidas tranquille ut nando delenirent corpora ita tu qui sentis magnam facti gratiam aestuantis animae fucilari sic spiritum noli pigere laudem voce reddere veram qui voluit esse te sanum tibi set sic protestare vel salutis gratia
EDCS-06000943.translation: I have sought much how to hand down to memory, acting before all in this camp of soldiers, a common vow, and to render it for the return of the army, among past and future; while I seek with myself worthy names of the gods, I finally found the name and divine power of a goddess, eternal in vows, whom I vowed to dedicate in this place of salvation. Therefore, as long as there are worshippers, I have sanctioned the name as much as I could and given to all the true waters of salvation, so that with such fires on these ever sandy hills, they might tranquilly soothe their bodies by swimming, despite the burning flames of the harsh sun, which waver. So you, who feel great gratitude for the deed, do not be slow to render true praise with your voice to him who wished you to be healthy, and declare this, even for the sake of salvation.
EDCS-06000943.summary: This verbose and poetic inscription describes the dedication of a source of 'salvation' (likely water) to a goddess for the health and return of the army, mitigating the harsh desert environment, and encourages praise for its benefactor.

EDCS-06000945.text_edition: Iovi Hammoni reduci Augusti sacrum Tullius Romulus centurio ex maioriario praepositus vexillationi
EDCS-06000945.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Hammon for the safe return of Augustus. Tullius Romulus, a centurion from the chief clerk's staff, in charge of a detachment, (set this up).
EDCS-06000945.summary: This is a religious dedication to Jupiter Hammon for the emperor's safe return, made by Tullius Romulus, a centurion commanding a detachment, suggesting a military context and an emperor's journey.

EDCS-06000947.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Euasius qui et Calvana vixit annum I menses IIII hic situs
EDCS-06000947.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Euasius, who was also called Calvana, lived for 1 year, 4 months. He lies here.
EDCS-06000947.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Euasius, also known as Calvana, who died at the young age of one year and four months, and is buried at this location.

EDCS-06000949.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus Dacicus maximus tribuniciae potestatis tertium imperator V pontifex maximus et Caius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar princeps iuventutis Germanicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus Dacicus maximus pontes vetustate delapsos et iter longa iniuria corruptum restituerunt sua infaticabili sic providentia pervium commeantibus reddiderunt I
EDCS-06000949.publication: IRT 00924 = BCTH-1912-CLXXXIII = ILAfr 00650 = RRMTrip 00004 = AE 1912, 00023 = AE 1926, 00138
EDCS-06000949.translation: The Emperor Caesar Gaius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus Maximus Sarmaticus Maximus Dacicus Maximus, in his third tribunician power, fifth acclamation as Imperator, Pontifex Maximus; and Gaius Iulius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, Germanicus Maximus Sarmaticus Maximus Dacicus Maximus, restored bridges that had collapsed due to age and a road corrupted by long neglect. Through their indefatigable foresight, they rendered it passable for travelers. I.
EDCS-06000949.summary: This inscription records the restoration of dilapidated bridges and a damaged road by the Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus, showcasing their providentia, likely dating to around 237 CE.

EDCS-06000951.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatae sic II consul milia V
EDCS-06000951.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the second time, consul, [at] the fifth milestone.
EDCS-06000951.summary: This inscription is a milestone (miliarium) recording the name and titles of Emperor Tacitus, indicating the fifth mile from a specific point, likely dating to his reign (275-276 AD).

EDCS-06000955.text_edition: Imperatoris Tiberi Caesaris Augusti iussu Lucius Aelius Lamia proconsul ab oppido in mediterraneum direxsit sic milia passuum XLIV
EDCS-06000955.translation: By order of the Emperor Tiberius Caesar Augustus, Lucius Aelius Lamia, proconsul, directed [the road] from the town into the interior for 44 miles.
EDCS-06000955.summary: This inscription is a milestone (miliarium) documenting the construction or repair of a road by proconsul Lucius Aelius Lamia, under the authority of Emperor Tiberius, extending 44 miles inland from a town, likely dating to Tiberius's reign (14-37 AD).

EDCS-06000965.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio GENA Galenio sic Pio invicto PIP Augusto Pio ANMOX Parthico maximo   IVGRIE  ONSAI milia passuum LIII
EDCS-06000965.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius [Valerianus] Galenius, Pious, unconquered, Pious, Parthicus maximus, [at] the fifty-third milestone.
EDCS-06000965.summary: This inscription is a milestone (miliarium) honoring Emperor Gallienus and his titles, noting the fifty-third mile, likely dating to his sole reign (260-268 AD).

EDCS-06000966.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis his pater patriae consul milia passuum numero LVII
EDCS-06000966.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, father of his country, consul, [at] the fifty-seventh milestone.
EDCS-06000966.summary: This inscription is a milestone (miliarium) recording the name and titles of Emperor Gordian III, indicating the fifty-seventh mile, likely dating to his reign (238-244 AD).

EDCS-06000983.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Egnatio Gallienio sic invicto Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo Parthico maximo Germanico tribunicia potestate X consuli V patri patriae proconsuli miliarium
EDCS-06000983.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Egnatius Gallienus, unconquered, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, pontifex maximus, Parthicus maximus, Germanicus, holding tribunician power for the tenth time, consul for the fifth time, father of his country, proconsul, this milestone.
EDCS-06000983.summary: This inscription is a milestone (miliarium) dedicated to Emperor Gallienus, listing his extensive titles and honors, and indicates it was erected during his tenth year of tribunician power and fifth consulship, likely dating to 262 AD.

EDCS-07000898.text_edition: Publio Fabio Luci filio Q(uirina) Firmano legato consuli patrono civitatis senatus decreto pecunia publica
EDCS-07000898.translation: To Publius Fabius Firmanus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, legate, consul, patron of the city, by decree of the senate, with public money.
EDCS-07000898.summary: This inscription is a honorary dedication erected by a city's senate with public funds to Publius Fabius Firmanus, acknowledging his roles as legate, consul, and patron of the city.

EDCS-07400706.text_edition: Aulo Caecina Severo proconsule legio III Augusta IIII
EDCS-07400706.translation: Under Aulus Caecina Severus, proconsul, the Third Augustan Legion [built] the fourth [tower/segment].
EDCS-07400706.summary: This inscription records a construction activity by the Legio III Augusta, likely the building of a section of a road or a defensive structure (such as a tower or a fort segment, 'quartum' potentially referring to the fourth such structure), under the proconsulship of Aulus Caecina Severus, likely dating to the early 1st century AD in Africa.

EDCS-07600465.text_edition: Geminia Lucana Geminaes sic filia Tertullaes sic vixit annos LXXXVI hic sita est ossa tua bene quiescant tibi terra levis sit
EDCS-07600465.translation: Geminia Lucana, daughter of Geminia Tertulla, lived 86 years. She is buried here. May your bones rest well; may the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-07600465.summary: This inscription is a funerary epitaph for Geminia Lucana, placed by her mother Geminia Tertulla, commemorating her long life and invoking a common wish for peaceful rest in the afterlife.

EDCS-07600466.text_edition: Caius Iulius Impetratus pius vixit annis LXXXI
EDCS-07600466.translation: Gaius Julius Impetratus, pious, lived 81 years.
EDCS-07600466.summary: This inscription is a simple funerary epitaph for Gaius Julius Impetratus, noting his piety and age at death.

EDCS-07600467.text_edition: Sextus Tullius Fuscus pius vixit annis LXX ex eius ffiliis duobus
EDCS-07600467.translation: Sextus Tullius Fuscus, pious, lived 70 years, from his two sons.
EDCS-07600467.summary: This inscription is a funerary epitaph for Sextus Tullius Fuscus, placed by his two sons, recording his piety and age at death.

EDCS-08000806.text_edition: [Lucillae Augustae] Imperatoris Antonini Augusti patris patriae filiae Imperatoris Luci Aureli Veri coniugi pagus Uchitanorum Maiorum decreto decurionum pecunia publica fecit Caio Mario Faustino Lucio Propertio Rogato magistris pagi
EDCS-08000806.translation: To Lucilla Augusta, daughter of Imperator Antoninus Augustus, father of his country, wife of Imperator Lucius Aurelius Verus. The pagus of the Greater Uchitani made this with public money by decree of the decurions, with Gaius Marius Faustinus and Lucius Propertius Rogatus as magistrates of the pagus.
EDCS-08000806.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by the pagus of the Greater Uchitani to Lucilla Augusta, daughter of Marcus Aurelius and wife of Lucius Verus, likely during the Antonine dynasty.

EDCS-08000809.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary dedication or monument belonging to Emperor Severus Alexander, who ruled from 222-235 AD.

EDCS-08201701.text_edition: Pro salute Imperatorum dddominorum nnnostrorum Auggustorum Luci Septimi Severi et Marci Aureli Antonini Britannici Parthici Germanici et Iuliae Augustae matris castrorum vexillatio legionis III Augustae piae vindicis quae Vezerei praetendit et Titus Genius  Ianuarius optio   VE immunis                      s inus us    principalis  Car Publilius centuria Erri Saturnini Iulius  Iulius P Porcius Silvanus Granius Felix Aufidius Victorianus Varius Saturninus centuria Auli Suri Iusti Mundius  Co   centuria Valeri Proculi nnus Silvanus Silicius Urbanus Corn Rogatus Marius Marcellus Iulius Victor Sedius Felix Iulius Faustus Helvius Silvanus Valerius Rogatus Aurelius Severianus Iulius Primus principalis Aelius Saturn principalis Aelius Saturn sequtor sic Mussius Fuscinus Annius Quintus Flavius Victor Lurius Beatus Pomponius Silvanus Marcus Acilius Fortunatus Papirius Sallustianus Aelius Crescens Flavius Vibianus Aelius Vitalis Pontius Donatus Antistius Felix Safidius Ammon Cattius Ingenuus Iulius Primus sequtor sic Papius Fortun Pinarius Martialis Iulius Donatus Quintus Sulpicius Saturninus Scribonius Priscus Florius Rogatus centuria Flavi Victoris Pompeius Liberalis Euped sic Catullinus Flavius Victor centuria  Maximi Annius Saturus Caius Aufidius Niger centuria Quinti Gelli Iuvenalis Aelius Iucundus attius Fortunatus  cohortis III  centuria Iuli   Donatus Iulius        Aemilius Victor Vitellius Ianuarius Flavius Proculus centuria Domiti Maximi Lucilius Iulianus Quintus Flavius Ianuarius Eppius Donatus Iulius Donatus Veturius Proculus Hostilius Salutaris Sulpicius Successus Clodius Antoninus Publius Clementi--- C Stertinius Iuvenalis Flavius Verecundus Agrius Felix Arrunt Maximus Caius Pompeius Donatus Silicius Quartus Cornelius Saturninus Geminius Rufus Flavius Saturninus Iulius Honoratus Fannius Catullinius Pomponius Novatianus Iunius Verissimus Gemini Fortunatus Petronius Felix Marcus Aemilius Felix Caius Pompeius Felix centuria Aureli Aucti Domitius Amo Dentilius Adiutor nnus Felix Aemilius Sa Geminius C Aurelius   Iulius  Iulius  Iulius  Iulius  Statilius            D [[]] [[]] DEM
EDCS-08201701.translation: For the health of our lords the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Britannicus Parthicus Germanicus, and Julia Augusta, mother of the camps, a vexillation of the Third Augustan Legion, Pious and Vindicator, which is stationed at Vezerei, and Titus Genius Ianuarius, optio, exempt; ... principalis ... Car Publilius, centuria of Errius Saturninus; Julius, Julius P. Porcius Silvanus, Granius Felix, Aufidius Victorianus, Varius Saturninus, centuria of Aulus Surus Justus; Mundius, ... centuria of Valerius Proculus; ... Silvanus, Silicius Urbanus, Cornelius Rogatus, Marius Marcellus, Julius Victor, Sedius Felix, Julius Faustus, Helvius Silvanus, Valerius Rogatus, Aurelius Severianus, Julius Primus, principalis; Aelius Saturn, principalis; Aelius Saturn, follower; Mussius Fuscinus, Annius Quintus, Flavius Victor, Lurius Beatus, Pomponius Silvanus, Marcus Acilius Fortunatus, Papirius Sallustianus, Aelius Crescens, Flavius Vibianus, Aelius Vitalis, Pontius Donatus, Antistius Felix, Safidius Ammon, Cattius Ingenuus, Julius Primus, follower; Papius Fortun(atus), Pinarius Martialis, Julius Donatus, Quintus Sulpicius Saturninus, Scribonius Priscus, Florius Rogatus, centuria of Flavius Victor; Pompeius Liberalis, Euped(es) Catullinus, Flavius Victor, centuria of Maximus; Annius Saturus, Gaius Aufidius Niger, centuria of Quintus Gellius Juvenalis; Aelius Jucundus, Attius Fortunatus, ... of the Third Cohort, centuria of Julius Donatus, Julius ...; Aemilius Victor, Vitellius Ianuarius, Flavius Proculus, centuria of Domitius Maximus; Lucilius Julianus, Quintus Flavius Ianuarius, Eppius Donatus, Julius Donatus, Veturius Proculus, Hostilius Salutaris, Sulpicius Successus, Clodius Antoninus, Publius Clementius, C. Stertinius Juvenalis, Flavius Verecundus, Agrius Felix, Arruntius Maximus, Gaius Pompeius Donatus, Silicius Quartus, Cornelius Saturninus, Geminius Rufus, Flavius Saturninus, Julius Honoratus, Fannius Catullinius, Pomponius Novatianus, Junius Verissimus, Geminius Fortunatus, Petronius Felix, Marcus Aemilius Felix, Gaius Pompeius Felix, centuria of Aurelius Auctus; Domitius Amo, Dentilius Adiutor, ... Felix, Aemilius Sa..., Geminius C. Aurelius ..., Julius ..., Julius ..., Julius ..., Julius ..., Statilius ...
EDCS-08201701.summary: This lengthy inscription is a votive dedication for the well-being of Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, and Empress Julia Domna, made by a detachment of the Third Augustan Legion stationed at Vezerei, listing numerous soldiers by name and their centuriae, dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-08201775.translation: Arisus rests in peace. Born at the sixth hour, he lived for nine scruples (or years).
EDCS-08201775.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Arisus, stating his time of birth and his age, likely indicating a very young age based on the unit 'scrupula'.

EDCS-08201842.translation: Hermes to his sweetest wife and son(s).
EDCS-08201842.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Hermes dedicates a monument to his beloved wife and son or sons.

EDCS-08201940.translation: To Imperator Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantius, Pious, Fortunate, Undefeated Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power, consul for the third time, proconsul.
EDCS-08201940.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantius I Chlorus, listing his full titulature and honors, dating to his reign between 293 and 306 AD.

EDCS-08300556.text_edition: Iohannes fidelis in pace vixit annos LXXX depositus XIII Kalendas Octobres
EDCS-08300556.translation: John, faithful in peace, lived for 80 years. Buried on the 13th day before the Kalends of October.
EDCS-08300556.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription for John, noting his age and burial date, likely from Late Antiquity.

EDCS-08300568.text_edition: Epifanius sic fidelis in pace vixit annis LX depositus IIII Nonas Sebtenbres sic
EDCS-08300568.translation: Epiphanius, faithful in peace, lived for 60 years. Buried on the 4th day before the Nones of September.
EDCS-08300568.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription for Epiphanius, stating his age and burial date, likely from Late Antiquity.

EDCS-08300569.text_edition: Maria fidelis in pacae!  fidelis in pacae sic vixit annis XXX
EDCS-08300569.translation: Maria, faithful in peace, lived for 30 years.
EDCS-08300569.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription for Maria, indicating her age at death, likely from Late Antiquity.

EDCS-08300668.inscription_type: reges, termini, tituli honorarii, tituli sacri
EDCS-08300668.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pater patriae et Publius Septimius Geta [] castellanis Thudedensibus fines et immunitatem a rege Iuba per confirmatione divi Augusti concessos post multis maximisque saeculis felicissimis temporibus suis divino iudicio restituerunt Thudedenses determinatione saeculi terminos posuerunt et dedicaverunt feliciter
EDCS-08300668.translation: Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, Father of the Country, and Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Father of the Country, and Publius Septimius Geta, have restored to the Thudedensian castellani the boundaries and immunity granted by King Juba through the confirmation of the divine Augustus, after many and very great centuries, in their most prosperous times, by divine judgment. The Thudedenses, by determination of the age, set the boundaries and happily dedicated this.
EDCS-08300668.summary: This inscription records the restoration of boundaries and immunities to the Thudedensian castellani by Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, which had originally been granted by King Juba and confirmed by Augustus, dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-08400553.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Sextilius Donatus miles cohortis I urbanae militavit annos VI vixit annos XXV hic situs est
EDCS-08400553.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Gaius Sextilius Donatus, a soldier of the first urban cohort, served for six years, lived for twenty-five years. He is laid here.
EDCS-08400553.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Sextilius Donatus, a soldier of the urban cohort, likely erected by comrades or family after his death at 25.

EDCS-08400554.text_edition: TI  perp  IIII publica Africae   Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Sextilius Donatus pius vixit annis LXXXX hic situs est
EDCS-08400554.translation: To the Divine Shades. Gaius Sextilius Donatus, dutiful, lived for ninety years. He is laid here. TI. PERP. IIII PUBLICAE AFRICAE.
EDCS-08400554.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Sextilius Donatus, who lived to ninety years, possibly indicating an older man with some public connection in Africa (given the additional inscription).

EDCS-08400556.text_edition: Sexto Furnio Fausto Sulpiciano clarissimo puero
EDCS-08400556.translation: To Sextus Furnius Faustus Sulpicianus, a most distinguished boy.
EDCS-08400556.summary: This is a dedication to a young boy of high social standing, Sextus Furnius Faustus Sulpicianus, likely a funerary monument or an honorary dedication by his family.

EDCS-08400557.summary: This is a fragment likely referring to a person named Victor, a freedman of the emperor, indicating his status and imperial connection.

EDCS-08400558.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Calpurnia Quartilla vixit annis LXXXVI mensibus IIII hic sita est  Dis Manibus sacrum Selnius Optatus vixit annis LXXXVII mensibus VII hic situs est
EDCS-08400558.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Calpurnia Quartilla lived for eighty-six years, four months. She is laid here. Sacred to the Divine Shades. Selnius Optatus lived for eighty-seven years, seven months. He is laid here.
EDCS-08400558.summary: This is a funerary inscription for two individuals, Calpurnia Quartilla and Selnius Optatus, recording their ages and burial places, likely a shared tomb for a couple or family members.

EDCS-08400562.text_edition: Honeratus sic et Lucia qum sic filis suis votum quot sic fecerunt libentes animo solverunt
EDCS-08400562.translation: Honeratus and Lucia, with their children, willingly and with pious hearts fulfilled the vow they made.
EDCS-08400562.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Honeratus and Lucia together with their children, indicating a religious dedication or offering.

EDCS-08500626.translation: For the health of the Emperor.
EDCS-08500626.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the well-being of the reigning emperor, likely erected by loyal subjects.

EDCS-08500628.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Aufidia dia pia mater vixit annis LX
EDCS-08500628.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Aufidia, dutiful mother, lived for sixty years.
EDCS-08500628.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aufidia, a dutiful mother, recording her age at death, likely erected by her family.

EDCS-08500631.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Lappius Saturninus pius vixit annis XXXVII Lappia Felicitas vixit annis XVI
EDCS-08500631.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Quintus Lappius Saturninus, dutiful, lived for thirty-seven years. Lappia Felicitas lived for sixteen years.
EDCS-08500631.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Lappius Saturninus and Lappia Felicitas, recording their ages at death, suggesting a monument for a family or a couple with a younger relative.

EDCS-08500632.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum  Cezzonia sic Lucaniane havete Zasgia sic Victoria Cezzonia sic bene merens femina pia vixit nnos XXXV menses VIII dies XVI Lucius Turius Fabius Lucanianus filius eius pius vixit nnos IIII menses IIII dies XVIIII hic siti sunt titulo usi bene quiescant
EDCS-08500632.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Cezzonia Lucaniana, Victoria Cezzonia, a well-deserving and dutiful woman, lived for thirty-five years, eight months, sixteen days. Lucius Turius Fabius Lucanianus, her dutiful son, lived for four years, four months, nineteen days. They are laid here; may they rest well, having used their title.
EDCS-08500632.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cezzonia Lucaniana and her young son, Lucius Turius Fabius Lucanianus, detailing their lifespans and expressing a wish for their peaceful rest, likely a monument erected by surviving family members.

EDCS-08500633.text_edition: Calpurnia Quinti filia Gemina pia vixit annos LX  Lucius Calpurnius S
EDCS-08500633.translation: Calpurnia Gemina, daughter of Quintus, a dutiful person, lived 60 years. Lucius Calpurnius S[---]
EDCS-08500633.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Calpurnia Gemina, who lived 60 years, seemingly set up by Lucius Calpurnius.

EDCS-08500634.text_edition: Sextus Terentius Saturninus Donnicianus et Bommia Saturnina Terentilla titulos sibi fecerunt et dedicaverunt
EDCS-08500634.translation: Sextus Terentius Saturninus Donnicianus and Bommia Saturnina Terentilla made and dedicated these memorial stones for themselves.
EDCS-08500634.summary: This inscription records that Sextus Terentius Saturninus Donnicianus and Bommia Saturnina Terentilla prepared and dedicated their own funerary monuments.

EDCS-08500639.summary: This appears to be a funerary inscription for Gaius Sempronius Ianuarius and Petronia Proba, possibly a married couple or relatives.

EDCS-08500641.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum  Bommia Luci filia Saturnina pia vixit annos hic sita est
EDCS-08500641.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Bommia Saturnina, daughter of Lucius, a dutiful person, lived for [---] years; she is buried here.
EDCS-08500641.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes for Bommia Saturnina, daughter of Lucius, who is buried at this spot.

EDCS-08500642.text_edition: Titus Volusenus Potitus Rufiani filius pius vixit annos LXVII hic situs est Geminia Secunda inpensis suis fecit
EDCS-08500642.translation: Titus Volusenus Potitus, son of Rufianus, a dutiful person, lived 67 years; he is buried here. Geminia Secunda made this at her own expense.
EDCS-08500642.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Volusenus Potitus, who lived 67 years, commissioned and paid for by Geminia Secunda.

EDCS-08500643.text_edition: Perellia Fortunata Luci Perelli Luperci contubernalis Lucius Cornelius Saturninus A
EDCS-08500643.translation: Perellia Fortunata, companion of Lucius Perellius Lupercus. Lucius Cornelius Saturninus A[---]
EDCS-08500643.summary: This inscription likely commemorates Perellia Fortunata, identified as the companion of Lucius Perellius Lupercus, with Lucius Cornelius Saturninus also mentioned, possibly as the dedicator or a related individual.

EDCS-08500644.text_edition: Apollini Patrio Augusto Sextus Iulius Possessor praefectus cohortis Gallorum curator numeri Syrorum sagittariorum item alae primae Hispanorum tribunus militum legionis XII fulminatae adlectus in decurias ab optimis maximisque Impperatoribus Antonino et Vero Auggustis adiutor praefecti annonae ad horrea Ostiensia et Portuensia procurator Augusti ad ripam Baetis procurator Augusti Ostis ad annonam procurator Augusti Alexandriae ad Mercurium statuam aheneam transmare advectam dono dedit

EDCS-08600646.text_edition: Imperatori Tito Aelio Antonino Caesari Augusto templum Minervae Augustae sacravit Apisa minus ex arca publlica fecerunt sufetes Macer et Baliato
EDCS-08600646.translation: To the Emperor Titus Aelius Antoninus Caesar Augustus, they consecrated the temple of Minerva Augusta. Apisa [---] out of the public treasury, the sufetes Macer and Baliato made [it].
EDCS-08600646.summary: This inscription records the consecration of a temple to Minerva Augusta in honor of Emperor Antoninus Pius, funded by the public treasury and built by the sufetes Macer and Baliato.

EDCS-08600647.text_edition: Plutoni Augusto sacrum pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Commodus! Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Sarmatici Germanici maximi  patris patriae civitas AbbiritanaLICVIERIII AFCTA ampliavit VSILERIIA per Natalem llisii et Namphamonem Gudis sufetes pecunia publica fecerunt
EDCS-08600647.translation: Sacred to Pluto Augustus, for the well-being of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, Sarmaticus, Germanicus Maximus, father of his country. The city of Abbi(ri)tana enlarged [---] through Natalis Ilisii and Namphamo Gudis, sufetes, they made it with public money.
EDCS-08600647.summary: This inscription dedicates a sacred monument to Pluto Augustus for the well-being of Emperor Commodus, which the city of Abbi(ri)tana enlarged and built with public funds through its sufetes Natalis Ilisii and Namphamo Gudis.

EDCS-08600648.text_edition: Caius Iulius Paris Igilgili vixit annos XXXV hic est situs
EDCS-08600648.translation: Gaius Iulius Paris from Igilgili lived 35 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-08600648.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Iulius Paris, originally from Igilgili, who lived 35 years and is buried at this site.

EDCS-08600649.text_edition: Lucius Iulius Mustus vixit annos LXXXV hic situs est
EDCS-08600649.translation: Lucius Iulius Mustus lived 85 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-08600649.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial place and age of death for Lucius Iulius Mustus.

EDCS-08600650.text_edition: Publius Iulius Ianuarius vixit annis XXXV hic situs est
EDCS-08600650.translation: Publius Iulius Ianuarius lived 35 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-08600650.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial place and age of death for Publius Iulius Ianuarius.

EDCS-08600653.text_edition: Saturno  Domitius Saturninus soliarius palmis argenteis ex libris IIII votum solvit
EDCS-08600653.translation: To Saturn. Domitius Saturninus, a 'soliarius', fulfilled his vow with silver palms weighing four pounds.
EDCS-08600653.summary: This votive inscription records Domitius Saturninus fulfilling a vow to the god Saturn by dedicating silver palms.

EDCS-08600655.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Quintus Fabius Antistianus pius vixit annis XVIIII ossa tibi bene quiescant
EDCS-08600655.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Quintus Fabius Antistianus, dutiful, lived 19 years. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-08600655.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Fabius Antistianus, wishing well to his remains.

EDCS-08600656.text_edition: IV sacrum Marcus Salluvius Hamillus de sua pecunia
EDCS-08600656.translation: Sacred to IV. Marcus Salluvius Hamillus [made this] from his own money.
EDCS-08600656.summary: This inscription records Marcus Salluvius Hamillus dedicating something, possibly a statue or altar, to an unknown deity or entity referred to as 'IV', at his own expense.

EDCS-08600663.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus Pius Felix Augustus fecerunt coloni loci Legum Maiorum et dono dederunt
EDCS-08600663.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus. The colonists of the place of the Elder Laws made [this] and gave [it] as a gift.
EDCS-08600663.summary: This inscription records the colonists of a place named 'Leges Maiores' (Elder Laws) making and dedicating something in honor of the Emperor Tacitus, likely during his reign (275-276 CE).

EDCS-08600666.text_edition: Marco Asellio Rogato patri piissimo Marcus Asellius Mummius
EDCS-08600666.translation: To Marcus Asellius Rogatus, a most dutiful father. Marcus Asellius Mummius [erected this].
EDCS-08600666.summary: This is a funerary or commemorative inscription dedicated by Marcus Asellius Mummius to his most dutiful father, Marcus Asellius Rogatus.

EDCS-08600668.text_edition: Quinto Calpurnio Honorato Quintus Calpurnius Ianuarius filio piissimo
EDCS-08600668.translation: To Quintus Calpurnius Honoratus. Quintus Calpurnius Ianuarius [erected this] to his most dutiful son.
EDCS-08600668.summary: This is a funerary or commemorative inscription dedicated by Quintus Calpurnius Ianuarius to his most dutiful son, Quintus Calpurnius Honoratus.

EDCS-08600671.text_edition: Pinariae Publi filiae Extricatae Sextus Valerius Fuscinus augur Thevestini filius matri piissimae item Publio Valerio Fuscino filio
EDCS-08600671.translation: To Pinaria Extricata, daughter of Publius, a most dutiful mother. Sextus Valerius Fuscinus, augur of Theveste, her son [erected this], likewise to Publius Valerius Fuscinus, his son.
EDCS-08600671.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Sextus Valerius Fuscinus, an augur from Theveste, to his most dutiful mother Pinaria Extricata, and also to his son, Publius Valerius Fuscinus.

EDCS-08600672.text_edition: Lucio Marcio Luci filio Quirina Feroci flamini Augggustorum marito Iuliana eius
EDCS-08600672.translation: To Lucius Marcius Ferox, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, priest of the Augusti. Iuliana, his wife [made this].
EDCS-08600672.summary: This is a funerary or commemorative inscription dedicated by Iuliana to her husband, Lucius Marcius Ferox, who was a flamen (priest) of the Augusti.

EDCS-08600674.translation: She made this for her dearest son, Felix.
EDCS-08600674.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a mother's dedication to her beloved son, Felix.

EDCS-08600675.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aponi A Mai Helvia
EDCS-08600675.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Aponius, son of Aponius, of the Mae(cian tribe), Helvia (made this).
EDCS-08600675.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the spirits of the dead for Aponius, made by Helvia.

EDCS-08600676.text_edition: Dis Manibus Tiberius Claudius Exupius bonus vir vixit annos XXVII menses VII dies
EDCS-08600676.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Tiberius Claudius Exupius, a good man, lived 27 years, 7 months, [and a certain number of] days.
EDCS-08600676.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Tiberius Claudius Exupius, noting his age at death.

EDCS-08600678.text_edition: Lucius Munatius Pastor vixit annos XXXV Talincia uxor marito fecit
EDCS-08600678.translation: Lucius Munatius Pastor lived 35 years. His wife Talincia made this for her husband.
EDCS-08600678.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Talincia to her husband, Lucius Munatius Pastor, stating his age.

EDCS-08600679.text_edition: Alto labsa sic loco casu defuncta nefando primis rapta annis istic Fidentia dormit cui pater hunc tumulum sibi haec solacia fecit Fidentius filiae
EDCS-08600679.translation: Fidentia, having fallen from a high place, thus ended by an unspeakable accident, snatched away in her prime, sleeps here. Her father Fidentius made this tomb and these comforts for himself and his daughter.
EDCS-08600679.summary: This is a poignant funerary inscription from a father, Fidentius, for his daughter Fidentia, who died young in a tragic accident.

EDCS-08600810.translation: Quintus Fabius Victor, a veteran of the Third Augustan Legion, lived 71 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-08600810.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Fabius Victor, a veteran of the Legio III Augusta, noting his age and burial place.

EDCS-08601123.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Gaius Caeletharidas Faustinianus. He lived 18 and a half years. He is buried here.
EDCS-08601123.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Caeletharidas Faustinianus, recording his age at death and burial.

EDCS-08601312.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum  Marco Praecilio Secundo Cai filio vixit annis LXX Praecili Rogatus et Saturninus patri karissimo fecerunt
EDCS-08601312.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Praecilius Secundus, son of Gaius, he lived 70 years. Praecilius Rogatus and Praecilius Saturninus made this for their dearest father.
EDCS-08601312.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by sons, Praecilius Rogatus and Praecilius Saturninus, to their beloved father, Marcus Praecilius Secundus.

EDCS-08601517.publication: ILTun 01632 = Zarker 00143 = CLEAfrique 00065 = CLEAfr-01, p 89 = CLEAfr-02, 00017 = AE 2014, 01526
EDCS-08601517.translation: Publia Ogulnia made this for Gaius Attius Felix Postumus, her husband, and established an eternal home. For they lived as equals for a long time, [and] in harmony for many flourishing years together.
EDCS-08601517.summary: This is a funerary inscription from Publia Ogulnia for her husband, Gaius Attius Felix Postumus, emphasizing their long and harmonious married life.

EDCS-08900651.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning a consul named Volasenna, likely part of a public or honorary monument.

EDCS-08900659.translation: The owner of the estate established the temple of Caelestis.
EDCS-08900659.summary: This inscription records that the owner of an estate dedicated a temple to the goddess Caelestis.

EDCS-09001575.text_edition: Saturno Augusto sacrum Caius Calvissius Bullus votum solvit
EDCS-09001575.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Gaius Calvissius Bullus fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09001575.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Saturn Augustus, recording that Gaius Calvissius Bullus fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-09200547.text_edition: [Aureliae Galeriae] [Lucillae Augustae] Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Aureli Veri Augusti Armeniaci Parthici Maximi Medici decreto decurionum pecunia publica
EDCS-09200547.translation: [To Aurelia Galeria] [Lucilla Augusta], by decree of the decurions, with public money, [the daughter] of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus Armeniacus Parthicus Maximus Medicus.
EDCS-09200547.summary: This inscription records the public dedication of a monument to Aurelia Galeria Lucilla Augusta, likely a statue, funded by public money and decreed by the decurions, probably around 164-169 CE.

EDCS-09200548.text_edition: Aureliae Sabinae Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aurelii Antonini Augusti Armeniaci Parthici Medici filiae decreto decurionum pecunia publica
EDCS-09200548.translation: To Aurelia Sabina, daughter of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Parthicus Medicus, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-09200548.summary: This inscription records the public dedication of a monument to Aurelia Sabina, daughter of Marcus Aurelius, funded by public money and decreed by the decurions, likely around 164-169 CE.

EDCS-09200550.text_edition: Sabiniae Tranquillinae Augustae coniugi domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Antoni Gordi[ani Pii Felicis Invicti Augusti &
EDCS-09200550.translation: To Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, wife of our lord the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordian[us Pius Felix Invictus Augustus], and...
EDCS-09200550.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, wife of Emperor Gordian III, likely from the period of his reign (238-244 CE).

EDCS-09200552.text_edition: iuridico in! Piceno et Apuliam sic legato legionis XXII Primigeniae Decianae legato legionis XIII geminae Decianae praefecto aerarii Saturni consuli
EDCS-09200552.translation: …juridicus in Picenum and Apulia, thus legate of the XXII Primigenia Deciana legion, legate of the XIII Gemina Deciana legion, praefectus aerarii Saturni, consul…
EDCS-09200552.summary: This inscription is part of a career epitaph, detailing the numerous high-ranking military and civil appointments, including a consulate, of an unnamed individual during the Severan period, likely the reign of Decius (249-251 CE).

EDCS-09200555.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Lollius Munnas civis Belalitanus pius vixit annis LXXXX hic situs est
EDCS-09200555.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Lollius Munnas, a pious citizen of Belalis, lived 90 years. He is laid to rest here.
EDCS-09200555.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument for Lollius Munnas, a citizen from Belalis, who lived to be 90 years old.

EDCS-09200556.translation: Of Lucius, son of [---], of the Arnensis tribe, Ru[---].
EDCS-09200556.summary: This fragment appears to be part of a patronymic inscription, possibly funerary or honorific, identifying an individual named Lucius from the Arnensis tribe.

EDCS-09200558.text_edition: Marco Lurio Marci filio Arnensi Fausto Caeciliano equo publico adlecto praefecto cohortis II Bracaraugustanorum
EDCS-09200558.translation: To Marcus Lurius, son of Marcus, of the Arnensis tribe, Faustus Caecilianus, admitted to public cavalry, prefect of the Second Cohort of Bracaraugustani.
EDCS-09200558.summary: This inscription is an honorific or funerary monument for Marcus Lurius Faustus Caecilianus, detailing his military career as a cavalryman and prefect of an auxiliary cohort.

EDCS-09200559.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio  Felicis Arnensis fili sui
EDCS-09200559.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, of his son Felix Arnensis.
EDCS-09200559.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely by his son, potentially Marcus Aurelius, or an individual named Felix of the Arnensis tribe, suggesting an honorific monument during his reign (138-161 CE).

EDCS-09200560.text_edition: Publio Thermestino Crescenti Caeciliano decurioni coloniae Concordiae Iuliae Karthaginis flamini pe[rpetuo &
EDCS-09200560.translation: To Publius Thermestinus Crescens Caecilianus, decurion of the colony Concordia Iulia Karthago, perpetual flamen, and...
EDCS-09200560.summary: This is a fragment of a dedication to a distinguished citizen of Carthage, Publius Thermestinus Crescens Caecilianus, likely erected to honor his public offices.

EDCS-09200561.text_edition: ius Lucius Sc Ianuarius  ob honorem magistratus
EDCS-09200561.translation: ...his Lucius Sc[---] Ianuarius, on account of the honor of the magistracy.
EDCS-09200561.summary: This fragment likely records a dedication made by Lucius Sc[---] Ianuarius, possibly in thanks or commemoration of a magistracy he held.

EDCS-09200569.text_edition: Dio sic Mercurio Thermesi Augusto sacrum
EDCS-09200569.translation: Sacred to Dio, thus to Mercury Thermesus Augustus.
EDCS-09200569.summary: This is a religious dedication to the deity Dio, identified with Mercury Thermesus Augustus, indicating a syncretic cult.

EDCS-09400692.text_edition: Positum CA III Idus Iunias Saturno Augusto sacrum Caius Manius Felix Fortunatianus sacerdos in somnis monitus Saturni numine iussus Manius hic votum solvi sacrumque dicavi pro comperta fide et pro servata salute votum solvi libens animo
EDCS-09400692.translation: Set up on the 3rd day before the Ides of June, sacred to Saturn Augustus. Gaius Manius Felix Fortunatianus, priest, warned in his dreams by the divine power of Saturn and commanded, Manius, I have fulfilled this vow and dedicated this sacred offering for proved faith and for preserved health. I have willingly fulfilled the vow with a grateful heart.
EDCS-09400692.summary: This is a votive inscription to Saturn Augustus by a priest, Gaius Manius Felix Fortunatianus, who records fulfilling a vow after being divinely commanded in a dream, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09400700.text_edition: CA CIBOS NAO agricolae in spl(endidissima) re publica Bihensi Bilta conductori pariatori restitutori fundi Aufidiani et praeter cetera bona quae in eodem fundo fecit steriles quoque oleastri surculos inserendo plurimas oleas instituit puteum iuxta viam pomarium cum trichilis post collectarium vineas novellas sub silva aeque instituit uxor marito incomparabili fecit
EDCS-09400700.translation: To Caius Cibos Nao, farmer in the most splendid republic of Bihensis Bilta, contractor, partner, restorer of the Aufidian estate, and besides the other good things he did on the same estate, he also established many olive trees by grafting barren wild olive shoots, a well near the road, an orchard with arbors behind the cistern, and likewise established new vineyards under the forest. His wife made this for her incomparable husband.
EDCS-09400700.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a wife for her husband, Caius Cibos Nao, celebrating his extensive agricultural and infrastructural improvements to the Aufidian estate.

EDCS-09700765.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Decimus Volasenna Dorotheus pius vixit nnis LXXX mensibus XI diebus VII hic situs est   D A pi vixit annis LX mensibus  diebus hic sit-- est
EDCS-09700765.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Decimus Volasenna Dorotheus the pious lived 80 years, 11 months, 7 days. He is buried here. [---] D.A.P.I. lived 60 years, [---] months, [---] days. He is buried here.
EDCS-09700765.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial places and lifespans of two individuals, Decimus Volasenna Dorotheus and another whose name is partially lost, dedicated to the Manes.

EDCS-09701302.text_edition: Memoriae Simpliciae coniugis quae vixit annos XXXIII recessit VI Idus Maias (anno) provinciae CCXXVI item Exupi fili vixit annos VI menses VIII dies VI recessit XIII Kalendas supra scriptas (anno) provinciae supra scripto et Monnicae filiae vixit nnos V menses VIIII recessit XIIII Kalendas Iulias (anno) provinciae supra scripto Iulius Exupius piissimis fecit
EDCS-09701302.translation: To the memory of Simplica, my wife, who lived 33 years, departed on the 6th day before the Ides of May in the 226th year of the province. Likewise, to Exupius, my son, who lived 6 years, 8 months, 6 days, departed on the 13th day before the aforesaid Kalends in the aforesaid year of the province. And to Monnica, my daughter, who lived 5 years, 9 months, departed on the 14th day before the Kalends of July in the aforesaid year of the province. Julius Exupius made this for his most pious family.
EDCS-09701302.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Julius Exupius for his wife Simplica and their two children, Exupius and Monnica, noting their ages and precise dates of death according to a provincial calendar.

EDCS-09800356.text_edition: Barbaro et Regulo consulibus ex officina Faviana Flavi Apri opus doliare Fadi Euhelpisti
EDCS-09800356.translation: During the consulship of Barbarus and Regulus, from the Faviana workshop, a barrel made by Flavius Aper, belonging to Fadius Euhelpistus.
EDCS-09800356.summary: This inscription records the production details of a barrel, including the consuls in office for dating, the workshop, the maker (Flavius Aper), and the owner (Fadius Euhelpistus).

EDCS-10300258.text_edition: Neptuno Augusto sacrum Lucius Caecilius Maximinus ex summa honoraria flamoni sui posuit idemque dedicavit decreto decurionum
EDCS-10300258.translation: Sacred to Neptune Augustus. Lucius Caecilius Maximinus placed this from the total honorarium of his flamenship and also dedicated it by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-10300258.summary: This is a dedication to Neptune Augustus by Lucius Caecilius Maximinus, funded by his flamen's honorarium and approved by the city council.

EDCS-10300299.translation: Julia Victoria, daughter of Gaius, lived 35 years. Gaius Cornelius Cassius placed this for his wife, a thrifty and deserving woman, and for his daughter, an example of his piety.
EDCS-10300299.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Gaius Cornelius Cassius for his wife, Julia Victoria, celebrating her virtues and his devotion to her and his daughter.

EDCS-10300303.translation: Sextus Metilius of the Stellatine tribe, Livi's son, lived 20 years. He served in the military for 2 years. Tiberius Quintius (set this up).
EDCS-10300303.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Metilius, a young soldier who died at age 20, set up by Tiberius Quintius, likely a relative or comrade.

EDCS-10300306.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. [---]us [---]VII. Sacred to the Divine Manes. Iulia Rogatina lived 25 years; she lies here. Her parents placed this for their dearest daughter. Sacred to the Divine Manes. Iulia Inventa lived 30 years; she lies here. Her parents placed this for their dearest daughter. Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Magnius Satur, a hardworking man, not worthy of this place, having died abroad on the Hadrumetine road, lived 55 years. Iulia Quinta, his dearest wife, placed this for her husband. He lies here.
EDCS-10300306.summary: This entry records four distinct funerary inscriptions, dedicating tombs to Iulia Rogatina, Iulia Inventa, and Lucius Magnius Satur, each set up by their grieving parents or wife, along with one fragmentary inscription.

EDCS-10300523.translation: N[---] NAE LI[---] sacred I[---] criobolium.
EDCS-10300523.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly related to a religious ceremony involving a 'criobolium,' a sacrifice of a ram.

EDCS-10300552.text_edition: Iussu domini Aesculapi Lucius Numisius Luci filius Vitalis podium de suo fecit quisquis intra podium adscendere volet a muliere a suilla a faba a tonsore a balineo commune custodiat triduo cancellos calciatus intrare nolito
EDCS-10300552.translation: By order of the lord Aesculapius, Lucius Numisius Vitalis, son of Lucius, made the platform from his own funds. Whoever wishes to ascend inside the platform, let him abstain for three days from women, pork, beans, barbers, and the public bath. Do not enter in sandals through the barriers.
EDCS-10300552.summary: This inscription records the construction of a podium by Lucius Numisius Vitalis for the god Aesculapius and specifies ritual purity rules for those wishing to ascend it.

EDCS-10300607.translation: To Publius Attius Extricatianus, son of Publius, of the Arnensian tribe, priest (flamen) of the deified Titus for the colony of Iulia Carthago, priest of Aesculapius twice, admitted to public cavalry by the Emperors Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustuses, Germanicus, Sarmaticus, on account of his honorable generosity. Iulia Bassilia, perpetual priestess (flaminica), his mother, by decree of the decurions, with public money, set this up.
EDCS-10300607.summary: This is a public honorific inscription dedicated to Publius Attius Extricatianus, a distinguished priest and magistrate of Carthage, funded by the local council and his mother, commemorating his munificence and imperial recognition during the reign of Marcus Aurelius and Commodus.

EDCS-10300622.text_edition: Iuliae Domnae Augustae matri Auggustorum castrorumque nnue victoriis eorum Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi fortissimi felicissimi coniugi civitas Giufitana decreto decurionum dedit posuit eademque dedicavit
EDCS-10300622.translation: To Julia Domna Augusta, mother of our Augusti and of the camps, through their victories, wife of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, most brave, most fortunate. The civitas of Giufi, by decree of the decurions, gave and dedicated this.
EDCS-10300622.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the town of Giufi to Julia Domna, Empress and mother of emperors, celebrating her role and the military successes of her husband, Septimius Severus.

EDCS-10300630.translation: To the deified Hadrian, father of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country. Germanus, son of Passus Germanus, from Sutunurcum, in honor of the perpetual flaminate of his daughter Quinta, from the legitimate sum of 4,000 sesterces, and having added 1,525 sesterces from his own funds, made statues of the deified Hadrian and Lucius Aelius Caesar, by decree of the decurions and at his own expense, and for the dedication, he provided a public distribution of meat and a gymnasium for the people.
EDCS-10300630.summary: This inscription records Germanus, son of Passus Germanus, dedicating statues of the deified Hadrian and Lucius Aelius Caesar, funded by his daughter's priestly office and his own contribution, and providing public festivities for the dedication, likely dating to the early Antonine period.

EDCS-10300636.text_edition: Augusto deo cives Romani qui Thinissut negotiantur curatore Lucio Fabricio
EDCS-10300636.translation: To the divine Augustus, the Roman citizens who do business in Thinissut, under the curator Lucius Fabricius.
EDCS-10300636.summary: This is a dedication to the deified Augustus by Roman citizens engaged in trade in Thinissut, overseen by Lucius Fabricius, functioning as a civic or religious monument by a local community.
