EDCS-13002596.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Porcius Felix lived for 92 years. Porcia Iuliana and Zaba, his heirs, made this. He is buried here.
EDCS-13002596.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Porcius Felix, erected by his heirs, Porcia Iuliana and Zaba, detailing his advanced age at death and burial location.

EDCS-13002597.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Pothus lived 31 years. Ianuaria, his wife, made this for her devoted husband. He is buried here.
EDCS-13002597.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a tombstone, dedicated by Ianuaria to her husband Pothus, who lived for 31 years.

EDCS-13002598.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lucius Publicius Successus lived 14 years, 3 months, 15 days. Successus, his father, made this for his devoted son.
EDCS-13002598.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a tombstone, dedicated by Successus, the father, to his son Lucius Publicius Successus, who died at the age of 14.

EDCS-13002599.translation: To the Divine Shades. Quinquatralis lived 18 years, 5 months, 23 days. Tsidina, his sister, made this.
EDCS-13002599.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a tombstone, dedicated by Tsidina to her brother Quinquatralis, who died at the age of 18.

EDCS-13002601.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Marcus Sextilius Donatus lived 30 years. He is buried here. His sons made this for their dearest father.
EDCS-13002601.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a tombstone, dedicated by the sons of Marcus Sextilius Donatus to their father, who lived 30 years.

EDCS-13002602.translation: To the Divine Shades of Rogata Valentia. Sextus, her son, made this. She lived 45 years.
EDCS-13002602.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a tombstone, dedicated by Sextus to his mother Rogata Valentia, who lived 45 years.

EDCS-13002604.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. For Marcus Valerius. He lived 50 years. [The rest is missing or illegible.]
EDCS-13002604.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription likely from a tombstone, dedicated to Marcus Valerius, who lived 50 years, with the dedicator's identity missing.

EDCS-13002605.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Gaius Valerius Fortis lived 60 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-13002605.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a tombstone, commemorating Gaius Valerius Fortis, who lived 60 years and is buried at the site of the monument, with the dedicator's identity missing or implied.

EDCS-13002607.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Gaius Vibius Geminus lived 19 years. [A relative, possibly Vibius Postumius], made this for his most devoted son.
EDCS-13002607.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription from a tombstone, dedicated by a father, possibly Vibius Postumius, to his son Gaius Vibius Geminus, who died at 19 years old.

EDCS-13002608.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Victoria [made this] for her father and daughter Q[...]
EDCS-13002608.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription likely from a tombstone, dedicated by Victoria to her father and daughter, whose full name is incomplete.

EDCS-13002609.translation: To the Divine Shades. Victorinus lived 22 years.
EDCS-13002609.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a tombstone, commemorating Victorinus, who lived 22 years, with the dedicator's identity not explicitly stated.

EDCS-13002610.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. To Roia(?) MMACA(?) VS Vasia (or a name ending in -vs Vasia), his most chaste wife, who lived 20 years, 10 months, and 15 days. She lies here.
EDCS-13002610.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman named Roia, erected by her husband, dating to the late 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-13002622.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-13002622.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gaius Iulius Felix lived 58 years, 7 months. Iulius Felix, his son, made this for his most devoted father. He lies here. Table of Sisatius.
EDCS-13002622.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Iulius Felix, set up by his son, Iulius Felix, likely from the 2nd century AD, with an additional mention of 'Mensa Sisatiu'.

EDCS-13002625.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Claudius Annus Honoratianus, son of Marcus, of the Papiria tribe, lived 25 years, 2 months, 25 days. Marcus Ann(ius) Octavianus, his unfortunate father, made this. He lies here.
EDCS-13002625.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Claudius Annus Honoratianus, erected by his grieving father, Marcus Ann(ius) Octavianus, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-13002626.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Valeria Severa lived 28 years, 7 months. She lies here. Iulius Constans made this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-13002626.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valeria Severa, commissioned by her husband, Iulius Constans, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-13002661.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Iulius Elianus Pulneus lived for years (number missing). Felicitas lived 35 years. Her husband made this.
EDCS-13002661.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulius Elianus Pulneus and Felicitas, erected by Felicitas's husband, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-13002662.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Getula lived about 30 years.
EDCS-13002662.summary: This is a brief funerary inscription for Getula, indicating she lived approximately 30 years, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-13002664.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Iulius [...]nius lived 25 years. Ponponia Ianuaria, his wife, made and dedicated this from her own resources. He lies here.
EDCS-13002664.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulius [...]nius, erected and dedicated by his wife, Ponponia Ianuaria, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-13002666.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Victoria lived for 3 years, 5 months. Her parents made this for their sweetest daughter.
EDCS-13002666.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young girl named Victoria, erected by her parents, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-13002773.translation: In the reign of the Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus. Sacred to Victoria Augusta. Lucius Granius Honoratus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, from the century of Marcus Antonius, took care of having this made at his own expense because of his decurionate.
EDCS-13002773.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Victoria Augusta, commissioned by Lucius Granius Honoratus, a soldier of Legio III Augusta, to commemorate his decurionate, dating to the reign of Emperor Nerva (96-98 AD).

EDCS-13003658.translation: [...]ius Martialis, a veteran of the Third Augustan Legion, lived 90 years. He lies here.
EDCS-13003658.summary: This is a funerary inscription for an elderly veteran named [...]ius Martialis, who served in Legio III Augusta, likely dating from the 1st to 3rd century AD.

EDCS-13003659.text_edition: Sextus Sulpicius Sexti filius Quirina Senilis miles legionis III Augustae centuria Caesoni beneficiarius Tetti Iuliani et Iavoleni Prisci legatorum Augusti vixit annos XXV militavit nnos VI hic situs est Marcus Aurelius Candidus |(centurio) legionis III Augustae heres eius posuit
EDCS-13003659.translation: Sextus Sulpicius Senilis, son of Sextus, of the Quirina voting tribe, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, from the century of Caesonius, beneficiarius of Tettius Iulianus and Iavolenus Priscus, Augustan legates, lived for 25 years, served for 6 years; he is buried here. Marcus Aurelius Candidus, centurion of the Third Augustan Legion, his heir, set this up.
EDCS-13003659.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Sulpicius Senilis, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, erected by his heir, Marcus Aurelius Candidus, a centurion of the same legion, likely dating to the 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-13003662.translation: [...]nius Vitalis, a veteran, lived for 80 years. [...]ria MA [...] took care of this.
EDCS-13003662.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for an octogenarian veteran named [...]nius Vitalis, with a mention of someone named [...]ria who oversaw its creation.

EDCS-13003663.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Adaucta lived for 21 years. [---]XV.
EDCS-13003663.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead for a woman named Adaucta, who died at the age of 21.

EDCS-13003664.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. For Titus Aelius Princeps, freedman of Augustus, who lived for 77 years; he is buried here. Martialis.
EDCS-13003664.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead for Titus Aelius Princeps, a freedman of the emperor, who lived to 77, set up by Martialis, likely a relative or associate, likely dating to the 1st-2nd century AD.

EDCS-13003665.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. For Aemilia Marcella, six years old. Her father made this.
EDCS-13003665.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead for a young girl named Aemilia Marcella, who died at the age of six, erected by her father.

EDCS-13003667.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Lucius Appius Maximus Saturninus lived for 30 years. Gaius Lucius Appius Saturninus made this for his dearest brother; he is buried here.
EDCS-13003667.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead for Lucius Appius Maximus Saturninus, who died at 30, erected by his brother, Gaius Lucius Appius Saturninus.

EDCS-13003668.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Aponia Polla lived for 35 years; she is buried here. Cornelius Saturninus made this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-13003668.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead for Aponia Polla, who died at 35, erected by her husband, Cornelius Saturninus.

EDCS-13003669.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Butis made this for her dearest husband, Paris. He lived for 10 years [---]; he is buried here.
EDCS-13003669.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead for Paris, erected by his wife Butis, indicating he died at a young age.

EDCS-13003671.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Quintus Caecilius Felicior lived for 55 years. Caecilius Nundinarius made this for his brother.
EDCS-13003671.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead for Quintus Caecilius Felicior, who died at 55, erected by his brother, Caecilius Nundinarius.

EDCS-13003672.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Lucius Caecilius Gaetulicus, also known as Pistor, lived for 24 years and 9 months. Lucius Caecilius Gaetulus, his father, and Caecilia Spesina, his mother, made this for their most devoted son; he is buried here.
EDCS-13003672.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead for Lucius Caecilius Gaetulicus (Pistor), who died at the age of 24, erected by his grieving parents, Lucius Caecilius Gaetulus and Caecilia Spesina.

EDCS-13003673.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Gaius Caecilius Thevestinus lived 52 years. His father made this for his dearest son. B. H. M.
EDCS-13003673.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording Gaius Caecilius Thevestinus, erected by his father for his son, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-13003674.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Caecilia Cinitia lived 51 years. Caecilius Fortunatus, her freedman and dearest wife, made this monument. She is buried here.
EDCS-13003674.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caecilia Cinitia, erected by her freedman and husband Caecilius Fortunatus, noting she is buried on site, likely from the imperial era.

EDCS-13003675.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Caecilia Fortunata lived 42 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-13003675.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Caecilia Fortunata, indicating her age and burial location, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-13003677.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lucius Iulius Octavus lived forty years. His wife Tevestina dedicated this.
EDCS-13003677.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Iulius Octavus, dedicated by his wife Tevestina, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-13003679.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely for a soldier, specifying his name and legion, probably dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-13003680.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. [...] Erucius lived [...]13 years.
EDCS-13003680.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for an individual named Erucius, indicating his age, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-13003682.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. F. Felix lived 14 years, 5 months, 19 days. F. Lixestinus made this for his dearest son.
EDCS-13003682.summary: This is a funerary inscription for F. Felix, erected by his father F. Lixestinus, detailing the deceased's age, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-13003683.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Flavia Aeregia lived 31 years, 8 months. She is buried here. Gaius Caecilius Cinnas made this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-13003683.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Aeregia, erected by her husband Gaius Caecilius Cinnas, noting her age and burial location, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-13003685.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Quintus Helvius Saturninus, a dutiful man, lived 50 years. He is buried here. All the sons of Helvius, with the care of Cornelius Optatus, ensured this was made.
EDCS-13003685.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Helvius Saturninus, erected by his sons with the assistance of Cornelius Optatus, noting his age and burial location, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-13003689.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Gaius Iulius Fortunatianus lived 17 years, 25 days, 8 hours. After Fabia Fortunata, his dutiful mother, he lived 3 years, 6 months, 21 days, 1 hour. He is buried here.
EDCS-13003689.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Iulius Fortunatianus, detailing his age and the length of time he lived after his mother's death, indicating his burial location and likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-13003690.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gaius Iulius Ianuarius lived 40 years. Prima, his wife, made this.
EDCS-13003690.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording Gaius Iulius Ianuarius, set up by his wife, Prima, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-13003693.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Marcus Iunius Victor lived 80 years. Ianuaria made this for her husband; he is buried here.
EDCS-13003693.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Iunius Victor, erected by his wife Ianuaria, indicating his burial spot and likely from the Imperial era.

EDCS-13003694.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Iunia Optatula lived 35 years. Herennia Ianuaria made this for her daughter; she is buried here.
EDCS-13003694.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iunia Optatula, dedicated by her mother Herennia Ianuaria, noting her burial place and probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13003696.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Megatia lived 22 years.
EDCS-13003696.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Megatia, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13003698.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Sada made this. Valeria Quarta lived 30 years.
EDCS-13003698.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valeria Quarta, set up by Sada, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-13003700.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Sittia Gemellina lived 21 years. Nutrius Ianuarius, her devoted husband, made this; she is buried here.
EDCS-13003700.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sittia Gemellina, erected by her devoted husband Nutrius Ianuarius, indicating her burial location and likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13003701.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. [---] Vaberius Victor(?)
EDCS-13003701.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for a person named Vaberius Victor, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13003702.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gaius Valerius Valerianus lived 60 years. Valerius Valens made this for his dearest father; he is buried here.
EDCS-13003702.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Valerius Valerianus, dedicated by his son Valerius Valens, marking his burial place and likely from the Imperial era.

EDCS-13003703.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Velleia Respecta lived 50 years; she is buried here. Novellianus, her son, made this for his devoted mother.
EDCS-13003703.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Velleia Respecta, set up by her son Novellianus for his devoted mother, indicating her burial location and likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13003704.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gaius Vibius Celer lived 20 years; he is buried here. Gaius Vibius Novellus made this for his devoted brother.
EDCS-13003704.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Vibius Celer, erected by his brother Gaius Vibius Novellus for his devoted sibling, marking his burial place and likely from the Imperial era.

EDCS-13003705.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Quintus Lutatius(?) Victor lived 60 years.
EDCS-13003705.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Quintus Lutatius Victor at the age of 60.

EDCS-13003706.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Cotisa(?) lived 21 years. Sacred to the Divine Shades. Victoria lived 20 years.
EDCS-13003706.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating two individuals, Cotisa, who lived 21 years, and Victoria, who lived 20 years.

EDCS-13003707.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Umbria Victorina lived 35 years; she is interred here. Umbrius Adventus made this for his dearest mother.
EDCS-13003707.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Umbrius Adventus to his beloved mother, Umbria Victorina, who died at the age of 35 and is buried here.

EDCS-13003729.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Marcus Annius Seranus, son of Marcus, of the Papiria tribe, lived 18 years, 11 months, 12 days; he is interred here.
EDCS-13003729.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Annius Seranus, a young man who died at the age of 18 years, 11 months, and 12 days.

EDCS-13003736.summary: This is a dedication made by Ummidius Bubalio to the god Saturn Augustus.

EDCS-13003801.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. [---] Flavius Vindex lived 80 years; he is interred here.
EDCS-13003801.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavius Vindex, who died at the age of 80 and is buried at this location.

EDCS-13003806.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. [---] Iulius Ianuarius lived 36 years [---].
EDCS-13003806.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Iulius Ianuarius, who lived 36 years.

EDCS-13003807.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Iulius Mamarius lived 26 years.
EDCS-13003807.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulius Mamarius, who died at the age of 26.

EDCS-13003808.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Iulia Saturnina lived 70 years.
EDCS-13003808.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Iulia Saturnina, who lived to be 70 years old.

EDCS-13003809.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Olia Rustica lived 71 years, 10 months, 11 days; she is interred here.
EDCS-13003809.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Olia Rustica, who died at the age of 71 years, 10 months, and 11 days, and is buried at this site.

EDCS-13003868.text_edition: Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Nervae Traiani Caesaris Augusti Germanici Dacici consulis VI imperatoris XIII Lucius Acilius Strabo Clodius Nummus legatus Augusti pro praetore inter Musulamios et Madaurenses
EDCS-13003868.translation: By the authority of Imperator Nerva Trajan Caesar Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, consul for the sixth time, imperator for the thirteenth time, Lucius Acilius Strabo Clodius Nummus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, between the Musulamii and the Madaurenses.
EDCS-13003868.summary: This inscription records a territorial dispute resolution or boundary demarcation undertaken by a Roman legate under the authority of Emperor Trajan, dating to around 112-114 AD.

EDCS-13100001.text_edition: Helvius Fuscus Luci filius Quirina Emerita militavit legione III Augusta vixit annos  militavit annos XXXIIII hic situs est EIus ProculusAVI
EDCS-13100001.translation: Helvius Fuscus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, from Emerita, served in the Third Augustan Legion. He lived for [---] years, served for 34 years. He lies here. EIus Proculus AVI [---].
EDCS-13100001.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Helvius Fuscus, a veteran of the Legio III Augusta, indicating his service duration and the location of his burial, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-13100004.text_edition: eus Cai filius Arnensis  miles legionis III Augustae centuria Curti vixit annosXVI militavit nnos hic situs est R
EDCS-13100004.translation: eus, son of Gaius, of the Arnensis tribe, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, from the centuria of Curti. He lived for 16 years, served for [---] years. He lies here. R [---].
EDCS-13100004.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for a young soldier of the Legio III Augusta, specifying his military unit and short lifespan, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-13100005.text_edition: lusio O  vixsit sic nnos XLIII  militavit nnos XXIII hic situs est curam egerunt Caius Catellius
EDCS-13100005.translation: lusio O [---] lived for 43 years, served for 23 years. He lies here. Gaius Catellius took care of it.
EDCS-13100005.summary: This is a funerary inscription for an individual whose name is partially lost, noting their age, years of military service, and that Gaius Catellius was responsible for its erection, probably from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-13100006.text_edition: s Cai filius  Celer --- mil(es) legionis III Augustae
EDCS-13100006.translation: s, son of Gaius, Celer [---], a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion.
EDCS-13100006.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription identifying a soldier named Celer, son of Gaius, belonging to the Legio III Augusta, likely part of a funerary monument or dedication, dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-13100007.translation: [---]us, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, from the centuria of [---]cus. He lived for 67 years, served for 24 years. [---].
EDCS-13100007.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for a veteran of the Legio III Augusta, detailing his age and length of military service, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-13100039.translation: Euploeae by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13100039.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Euploea (a goddess associated with safe voyages) made by order of the decurions, indicating a public act of gratitude or vow, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13300154.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Augusto tribunicia potestate XVIII  Lucius Asprenas
EDCS-13300154.translation: To Imperator Caesar Augustus, with tribunician power for the eighteenth time. Lucius Asprenas [---].
EDCS-13300154.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to Emperor Augustus, noting his tribunician power for the eighteenth time (10 BC), indicating an official act by Lucius Asprenas.

EDCS-13302432.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Marco Annio Floriano Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo fortissimo adque sic indulgentissimo principi restitutori orbis potestate consuli II patri patriae procons(uli) res publica coloniae Hipponensium Regiorum
EDCS-13302432.translation: To Imperator Caesar Marcus Annius Florianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, most brave and most indulgent prince, restorer of the world, with tribunician power, consul for the second time, father of the country, proconsul. The Republic of the colony of Hipponensium Regiorum [erected this].
EDCS-13302432.summary: This inscription is a public dedication by the colony of Hipponensium Regiorum to Emperor Florianus, praising his virtues and titles, and dating to his brief reign in 276 AD.

EDCS-13302452.translation: To Quintus Domitius Marsianus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, procurator Augusti of the patrimony in the province of Narbonensis, procurator Augusti for the iron mines, procurator Augusti for collecting taxes in Gaul in the province of Belgica through the regions of the Tungri and Frisavones and Lower Germany and the Batavians, prefect of soldiers, adlected into the decuriae by Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus Caesars. When the equestrian order had voted for it to be erected at public expense, Lucius Domitius Fabianus, his brother, remitting the public expense, erected it at his own cost. An example of the codicil: 'Caesar Antoninus Augustus to his Marsianus, greetings. Long wishing to promote you to the splendor of a ducenarian procuratorship, I now take advantage of an opportune moment. Therefore, succeed Marius Pudens with as much hope of my perpetual favor as you retain the consciousness of your innocence, diligence, and experience. Farewell, my dearest Marsianus.'
EDCS-13302452.summary: This inscription is a public monument dedicated to Quintus Domitius Marsianus, detailing his extensive career as an imperial procurator, including a personal letter from Emperor Caracalla (Antoninus Augustus), erected by his brother Lucius Domitius Fabianus at his own expense, likely dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-13900478.text_edition: Sabinae Augustae Hadriani Augusti decreto decurionum pecunia publica
EDCS-13900478.translation: To Sabina Augusta, by decree of Hadrian Augustus, by public money of the decurions.
EDCS-13900478.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Sabina Augusta, wife of Hadrian, funded by public money and decreed by the local council.

EDCS-14200001.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely recording a dedication or a gift, with very little contextual information remaining.

EDCS-14200019.text_edition: Dis Manibus Quinti Porci Iovini vixit annis X Quintus Porcius Reginus pater piissimus filio quieto fecit
EDCS-14200019.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Porcius Iovinus lived for 10 years. Quintus Porcius Reginus, a most devoted father, made this for his tranquil son.
EDCS-14200019.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Quintus Porcius Reginus to his son, Quintus Porcius Iovinus, who died at the age of ten.

EDCS-14200131.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto tribunicia potestate VIIII imperatori consuli II patri patriae et Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate IIII et Lucio Septimio [Getae] Caesari Augusto Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augustorum consulare legionis III Augustae piae vindicis
EDCS-14200131.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, with tribunician power for the ninth time, imperator, consul for the second time, father of the country; and to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with tribunician power for the fourth time; and to Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus. Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of the Augusti, consular of the Third Augustan Legion, Pious and Vindicator.
EDCS-14200131.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the co-emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus), and Geta, erected by Quintus Anicius Faustus, a high-ranking military official.

EDCS-14200135.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepote Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate IIII consule III Quintus Servilius Candidus sua inpensa aquam quaesitam et elevatam in coloniam perduxit
EDCS-14200135.translation: When Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the fourth time, consul for the third time, was in office, Quintus Servilius Candidus, at his own expense, found and raised water and brought it to the colony.
EDCS-14200135.summary: This inscription records the construction of an aqueduct by Quintus Servilius Candidus at his own expense to bring water to a colony, during the reign of Emperor Hadrian around 120 CE.

EDCS-14200138.text_edition: Heraclii Dignissimo principali innocentissimo puero Tito Flavio Vibiano Iuniori pontifici duumviro filio ac collegae Titi Flavi Frontini Heraclii in parvulis annis exhibenti aequaliter voluptatum genera patris sui studiis populi sufragio et decreto ordinis  Divina stirpe progenito domino nostro fortissimo principi Valentiniano victori Pio Felici ac triumphatori semper Augusto Flavius Vivius Benedictus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Tripolitanae numini maiestatique eius semper devotus
EDCS-14200138.translation: To Heraclius, most worthy chief citizen, most innocent boy, Titus Flavius Vibianus Junior, pontifex, duumvir, son and colleague of Titus Flavius Frontinus Heraclius, who in his early years equally showed all kinds of delights through his father's pursuits, by the suffrage of the people and the decree of the order. To our lord, begotten of divine stock, most valiant prince Valentinian, victor, pious, fortunate and always triumphant Augustus, Flavius Vivius Benedictus, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Tripolitana, always devoted to his divinity and majesty.
EDCS-14200138.summary: This inscription is a dual dedication: one to Titus Flavius Vibianus Junior, a young notable honored by the community, and another to the Emperor Valentinian, dedicated by Flavius Vivius Benedictus, the provincial governor.

EDCS-14200141.text_edition: TI(ito)#T(ito)#I Axio Quirina pro liberis Celeri Tito Axio Titi filio Quirina
EDCS-14200141.translation: Titus Axius, of the Quirina tribe, for his children Celer and Titus Axius, son of Titus, of the Quirina tribe.
EDCS-14200141.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription made by Titus Axius for his children, Celer and Titus Axius, indicating their shared tribal affiliation.

EDCS-14200148.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto patre patriae et Imperatore Caesare Lucio Aurelio Vero Armeniaco Augusto Servius Cornelius Scipio Salvidienus Orfitus proconsul cum Uttedio Marcello legato suo dedicavit Caius Calpurnius Celsus curator muneris publici munerarius IIvir quinquennalis flamen perpetuus arcum pecunia sua solo publico et fundavit et marmore solido fecit
EDCS-14200148.translation: When Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, father of the country, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Armeniacus Augustus were in office, Servius Cornelius Scipio Salvidienus Orfitus, proconsul, dedicated this with his legate Uttedius Marcellus. Gaius Calpurnius Celsus, curator of public works, donor, duumvir quinquennalis, perpetual flamen, funded and founded an arch on public land and made it of solid marble at his own expense.
EDCS-14200148.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a marble arch on public land by Gaius Calpurnius Celsus, a prominent local official, during the joint reign of Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, with the proconsul Servius Cornelius Scipio Salvidienus Orfitus officiating the dedication.

EDCS-14200155.translation: To Gaius Caecilius Claudianus Aelianus, suffete, perpetual flamen, who held various positions and served his homeland, an excellent citizen, who faithfully performed duties in his magistracy and decurionate. The order of the Gigthensii decreed to erect a statue for him on account of the legations zealously carried out in Rome, which his devotion and zeal were attested to with the fullest testimony by illustrious consular men. He himself, content with the honor of the decree, took care to have it made at his own expense.
EDCS-14200155.summary: This inscription is a honorary dedication by the council of Gigthis to Gaius Caecilius Claudianus Aelianus, a local dignitary, in recognition of his diligent service as a suffete, flamen, and his effective representation of the city's interests in Rome, for which he personally funded the statue.

EDCS-14900027.translation: When Aulus Licinius Nva Silianus was consul, the community of Gurza from Africa entered into a bond of hospitality with Quintus Aufustius Macrinus, son of Gaius, of the Galeria tribe, prefect of engineers. They co-opted him and his children and descendants as their patron, for themselves, their children, and their descendants. Gaius Aufustius Macrinus, son of Gaius, of the Galeria tribe, prefect of engineers, received the Gurzensian community from Africa, and their children and descendants, into his and his family's trust and clientage. The legates who acted were Herennius Maximus, son of Rusticus, and Sempronius Quartus, son of Iafis.
EDCS-14900027.summary: This inscription records a formal agreement of hospitality and patronage established between the African community of Gurza and Quintus Aufustius Macrinus, a prefect of engineers, during the consulship of Aulus Licinius Nva Silianus.

EDCS-14900078.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Gaius Mummius Maximus lived 56 years. With the care of… may the earth lie light upon you. Aelius Saturninus lived 59 years, with the care of…
EDCS-14900078.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lives of Gaius Mummius Maximus and Aelius Saturninus, likely set up by their caretakers or family members, probably dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14900165.text_edition: Sint licet exiguae fugentia tempora vitae parvaque raptorum cito transeat hora dierum mergat et Elysiis mortalia corpora terris adsidue rupto Lachesis male conscia penso iam tamen inventa est blandae rationis imago per quam prolatos homines in tempora plura longior excipiat memoratio multaque servet secum per titulos mansuris fortius annis ecce recens pietas omni placitura favore ingentem famae numerum cum laude meretur exemplo iam plena novo quam Flavius alto more Secundus agens patrio signavit honore quis non iam pronis animi virtutibus adsit quis non hoc miretur opus fusasque videndo divitias stupeat tantos se cernere census per quos aetherias surgunt monimenta per auras haec est fortunae melius laudanda facultas sic sibi perpetuas faciunt impendia sedes sic immortales scit habere pecunia mores aeterno quotiens stabilis bene figitur usu viderit ille furor nimio qui ducitur auro quem trahit argenti venalis sanguine candor viderit et fusae vanis in amoribus errans gloria luxuriae peregrinas quaerere magno quae didicit vestes gemmasque nitore placentes aut ab Aeruthreo venientia munera fluctu quam laedunt gentes vario certamine rerum Graecia cum pueris Hispania Pallados usu venatu Lybiae tellus orientis amomo Aegyptos Phariis levitatibus artibus actis Gallia semper ovans dives Campania vino haec cito deficiunt et habent breve munus amoris momentis damnata suis sed si quis ad omnes respiciat vitae casus hominemque laboret metiri brevitate sua tunc credere discet nil aliut sic melius fieri nisi viribus aevi quot sic possit durare diu sub honore deorum nunc ego non dubitem tacitis Acherontos in umbris si post Fata manent sensus gaudere parentem saepe Secunde tuum reliquas et spernere turmas quod sciat hic tantam faciem superesse sepulchri perpetua novitate sui sic stare nitentes consensus lapidum sic de radice levatos in melius crevisse gradus ut et angulus omnis sic quasi mollitae ductus sit stamine cerae mobilibus signis hilaris scalptura novatur et licet atsidue sic probet hos vaga turba decores lucentes stupeat pariter pendere columnas quit sic cum militiae titulos ipsumque parentem numinibus dederis haec gaudia saepe videntem quae quondam dedit ipse loco dum munera Bacchi multa creat primasque cupit componere vites et nemus exornat revocatis saepius undis permittant mihi fata loqui noctisque timendae regnator Stygius sic immortalis haberi iam debet pater ecce tuus Ditisque relicti tristem deservisse domum dum tempore toto mavolt sic haec monumenta sequi scriptisque per aevom vivere nominibus solitis insistere lucis adsidue patrias hinc cernere dulciter arces  quosque dedit natis prope semper habere Penates forsitan haec multi vano sermone ferentes venturae citius dicant praesagia mortis si quis dum vivit ponat monimenta sic futuris temporibus mihi non tales sunt pectore sensus set sic puto securos fieri quicumque parare aeternam volvere domum certoque rigore numquam lapsuros vitae defigere muros fatis certa via est neque se per stamina mutat Atropos ut primo coepit decurrere filo crede Secunde mihi pensatos ibis in annos set sic securus eris set sic toto pectore dives dum nulli gravis esse potes nec plena labore testamenta facis tuus hoc dum non timet heres ut sic aedificet iam nunc quodcumque relinques totum perveniet tua quo volet ire voluntas sed revocat me cura operis celsique decores stat sublimis honor vicinaque nubila pulsat et solis metitur iter si iungere montes forte velint oculi vincuntur in ordine colles si videas campos infra iacet abdita tellus non sic Romuleas exire colossos in arces dicitur aut circi medias obeliscus in auras nec sic sistrigeri demonstrat pervia Nili dum sua perspicuis aperit Pharos aequora flammis quid non docta facit pietas lapis ecce foratus luminibus multis hortatur currere blandas intus apes et cerineos componere nidos ut semper domus haec Thymbraeo nectare dulcis sudet florisapos dum dant nova mella liquores huc iterum pietas venerandas erige mentes et mea quo nosti carmina more fove ecce Secundus adest iterum qui pectore sancto non monimenta patri sed nova templa dedit quo nunc Calliope gemino me limite cogis quas iam transegi rursus adire vias nempe fuit nobis operis descriptio magni diximus et iunctis saxa polita locis circuitus nemorum currentes dulciter undas atque sic reportantes mella frequenter apes hoc tamen hoc solum nostrae puto defuit arti dum cadis ad multos ebria Musa iocos in summo tremulas galli non diximus alas altior extrema qui puto nube volat  cuius si membris vocem natura dedisset cogeret hic omnes surgere mane deos et iam nominibus signantur limina certis cernitur et titulis credula vita suis opto Secunde geras multos feliciter annos et quae fecisti tu monimenta legas  Titus Flavius Secundus filius fecit Tito Flavio Secundo patri pio militavit annos XXXIII vixit annos CX hic situs est Flaviae Urbanae matri piae vixit annos CV hic sita est Flaviae Secundae sorori piae vixit annos XX hic sita est Tito Flavio Marcello fratri pio vixit annos XX hic situs est Tito Flavio Martiali fratri militavit annos XII vixit annos XXXV hic situs est Flaviae Speratae sorori piae vixit annos XXXVI hic sita est Aemiliae Sexti filiae Pacatae uxori piae flaminicae perpetuae vixit annos LIII hic sita est Titus Flavius Titi filius Papiria Secundus ipse flamen perpetuus vixit annos LX hic situs est Flaviae Titi filiae Pacatae flaminicae perpetuae coloniae Theleptensis filiae piae Flavia Libera mater statuam posuit vixit annos XV menses X hic sita est Flavia Libera Titi Flavi Secundi uxor pia vixit annos LXXXVIII hic sita est  Flavia Faustina pia vixit annos XXXVIII hic sita est Titus Flavius Faustinus et Flavia Victoria parentes posuerunt  Flavia Libera pia vixit annos XVI menses VI Titus Flavius Faustinus et Flavia Victoria parentes posuerunt hic sita est  Titus Flavius Papiria Receptus aedilicius quaestor aerarii decurio coloniae Theleptensis pius vixit annos XXXVI hic situs est Titus Flavius Faustinus et Flavia Victoria parentes posuerunt
EDCS-14900165.translation: Although the fleeting moments of life are brief, and the small hour of snatched days passes quickly, and Lachesis, ill-conscious of her constantly broken thread, buries mortal bodies in the Elysian fields, yet now an image of pleasing reason has been found, through which a longer remembrance may embrace people extended to more times, and keep many things with it through titles stronger than the passing years. Behold, a recent piety, destined to please with all favor, earns a great number of fame with praise, by a new example, which Flavius Secundus, acting in the noble manner, has marked with ancestral honor. Who would not now assist with virtues of a ready mind? Who would not admire this work and, seeing the scattered riches, be amazed to perceive such great wealth, through which monuments rise to the ethereal breezes? This is the faculty of fortune to be praised better. Thus do they make for themselves perpetual abodes with expenditures; thus does money know how to have immortal customs, as often as it is well fixed with eternal use. Let that madness see, which is led by too much gold, which is dragged by the venal gleam of silver's blood; let it also see, wandering in vain loves, the glory of luxury, which seeks foreign clothes and gems pleasing with their splendor at great cost, or gifts coming from the Erythraean wave, how much the nations harm with varied strife of things: Greece with boys, Hispania with the use of Pallas, Libya with hunting, the land of the East with incense, Egypt with Pharian frivolities and practiced arts, Gaul always triumphing, Campania rich in wine. These quickly fail and have a brief gift of love, condemned by their own moments. But if one looks at all the chances of life and labors to measure man by his brevity, then he will learn to believe that nothing else is done so well unless by the strength of age, so that it may endure long under the honor of the gods. Now I would not doubt, in the silent shades of Acheron, if after the Fates senses remain, that your father, Secundus, often rejoices and scorns the remaining multitudes, because he knows that such a face of a sepulcher remains here with its perpetual newness, that the agreement of stones stands thus shining, that the steps, raised from the root, have grown for the better, so that every corner has been drawn as if with a thread of softened wax. The cheerful carving is renewed with mobile signs, and although a wandering crowd constantly approves these beauties, they are equally amazed to see the shining columns suspended. What did you not give, when you gave to the deities the titles of military service and your very father, often seeing these joys, which he himself once gave to the place while he created many gifts of Bacchus and desired to plant the first vines, and adorned the grove by often recalling the waters? Let the Fates permit me to speak, and the Stygian ruler of the dreaded night, that your father, behold, should now be held immortal, and should have left the sad house of dis (Pluto), while throughout time he prefers to follow these monuments, and to live through ages by inscribed names, to constantly tread the accustomed lights, to sweetly behold his ancestral strongholds from here, and to always have near the household gods he gave to his children. Perhaps many, bearing these things with vain talk, will say these are prophecies of an earlier death, if anyone places monuments while alive for future times; for my part, such thoughts are not in my breast, but I think that whoever prepares to roll an eternal home and to fix the walls of life with certain rigor, never to fall, thus becomes secure. There is a certain path of the Fates, nor does Atropos change herself through the threads as she began to run with the first thread. Believe me, Secundus, you will go into measured years, but thus you will be secure, but thus rich in your whole heart, while you can be burdensome to no one, nor make testaments full of labor. Your heir does not fear this, so that whatever you leave, he may now build in such a way that your will may go where it wishes. But the care of the work and the lofty beauties recall me; the sublime honor stands and strikes the neighboring clouds, and measures the path of the sun, if by chance eyes wish to join mountains, the hills are conquered in order if you see the plains, the hidden earth lies beneath. Neither are the colossal statues said to come out to the Roman citadels, nor the obelisk into the middle air of the circus, nor does the sistrum-bearer thus show the permeable waters of the Nile, while her Pharos opens its waters with clear flames. What does learned piety not do? Behold, a stone perforated with many lights encourages the gentle bees within to run and arrange their waxen nests, so that this house may always sweat with Thymbraean nectar, while the flower-producing bees give new honey-liquids. To this place again, piety, raise your venerable minds, and cherish my verses in the way you know. Behold, Secundus is here again, who with a sacred heart has given not monuments to his father, but new temples. To what limit, Calliope, do you now compel me to revisit the paths I have already traversed? Indeed, we had a description of a great work; we spoke of polished stones in joined places, of the circuits of groves, of sweetly flowing waters, and of bees frequently bringing back honey. Yet this, this alone, I think, was lacking in our art, while, drunken Muse, you fall to many jokes: we did not mention the trembling wings of the cock at the top, which I think flies higher than the outermost cloud, whose voice, if nature had given it limbs, would force all the gods to rise early. And now the thresholds are marked with certain names, and a credulous life is perceived by its own titles. I wish, Secundus, that you may enjoy many years happily, and read the monuments you have made. Titus Flavius Secundus, son, made this for Titus Flavius Secundus, his pious father, who served 33 years and lived 110 years; he lies here. For Flavia Urbana, his pious mother, who lived 105 years; she lies here. For Flavia Secunda, his pious sister, who lived 20 years; she lies here. For Titus Flavius Marcellus, his pious brother, who lived 20 years; he lies here. For Titus Flavius Martialis, his brother, who served 12 years and lived 35 years; he lies here. For Flavia Sperata, his pious sister, who lived 36 years; she lies here. For Aemilia, daughter of Sextus, Pacata, his pious wife, perpetual flamen, who lived 53 years; she lies here. Titus Flavius Secundus, son of Titus, of the Papirian tribe, himself perpetual flamen, who lived 60 years; he lies here. Flavia Libera, the mother, erected a statue for Flavia Pacata, daughter of Titus, perpetual flamen of the colony of Theleptis, her pious daughter, who lived 15 years and 10 months; she lies here. Flavia Libera, pious wife of Titus Flavius Secundus, who lived 88 years; she lies here. Flavia Faustina, pious, who lived 38 years; she lies here. Titus Flavius Faustinus and Flavia Victoria, parents, erected this. Flavia Libera, pious, who lived 16 years and 6 months. Titus Flavius Faustinus and Flavia Victoria, parents, erected this; she lies here. Titus Flavius Receptus, son of Titus, of the Papirian tribe, ex-aedile, quaestor of the treasury, decurion of the colony of Theleptis, pious, who lived 36 years; he lies here. Titus Flavius Faustinus and Flavia Victoria, parents, erected this.
EDCS-14900165.summary: This is a lengthy and complex funerary inscription, combining an extensive poem reflecting on life, death, and the enduring power of monuments, with a prose dedication by Titus Flavius Secundus to his deceased family members, including his father, mother, siblings, wife, and others, meticulously detailing their names, relationships, ages, and sometimes civic roles, likely dating from the 2nd or 3rd century CE given its style and the mention of colonial officials.

EDCS-14900309.translation: To our lords Diocletian Augustus for the 7th time and Maximian Augustus for the 6th time, consuls. On the Kalends of April, the portico of the theater of the colony of Ammaedara was restored at public expense.
EDCS-14900309.summary: This inscription records the public restoration of the theater's portico in the colony of Ammaedara, undertaken on April 1st, 304 CE, during the consulship of Emperors Diocletian and Maximian.

EDCS-14900319.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Aelia Festula lived 24 years, 9 months, 18 days. Herennius Victor, with his sons, placed this for his most excellent wife. She is buried here.
EDCS-14900319.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelia Festula, set up by her husband Herennius Victor and their sons, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14900320.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Aelia Fortunata lived 43 years, 8 months; she is buried here. Lucius Cascellius Rufus placed this for his most pious and chaste wife. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead.
EDCS-14900320.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelia Fortunata, erected by her husband Lucius Cascellius Rufus in her memory, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14900321.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Gaius Aelius Namphamo lived 38 years. His pious wife placed this.
EDCS-14900321.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Aelius Namphamo, set up by his devoted wife, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14900322.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Publius Aelius Rogatus lived 66 years; he is buried here. Aelius Rogatus placed this for his pious father.
EDCS-14900322.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Rogatus, erected by his son, also named Aelius Rogatus, in honor of his pious father, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14900324.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Flavia Hilara lived 22 years. Annius Severus placed this for his most rare wife; she is buried here. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Annius Severus lived 32 years, 1 month, 11 days; he is buried here.
EDCS-14900324.summary: This is a double funerary inscription, with Annius Severus first dedicating a monument to his wife Flavia Hilara, and then a subsequent inscription for Annius Severus himself, likely erected by another family member after his death, probably dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14900326.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Modia Saturnina lived 90 years; she is buried here. Her children placed this for their dearest mother. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Lucius Atilius Macedo lived 70 years; he is buried here. Lucius Atilius Extricatus and Antilia, his children, placed this for their most pious father.
EDCS-14900326.summary: This is a double funerary inscription, with one dedication for Modia Saturnina placed by her children, and another for Lucius Atilius Macedo placed by his children, Lucius Atilius Extricatus and Antilia, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14900327.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Antonia Hilaria Modiana, daughter of Quintus, lived 10(?) years. Magnianus placed this for his sister.
EDCS-14900327.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Antonia Hilaria Modiana, dedicated by her brother Magnianus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14900328.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Gaius Camurenus Victoricus lived 70 years. Publius, his son, made this. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Gaius V. Victorinus lived 22 years. His devoted brother made this. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. I. Antonia lived 55 years. Her devoted son made this.
EDCS-14900328.summary: This is a funerary monument for three individuals: Gaius Camurenus Victoricus, Gaius V. Victorinus, and I. Antonia, each commemorated by a family member (son or brother).

EDCS-14900329.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Lucius Aquilius Suavis, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 75 years. His son placed this for his most devoted father. He is buried here.
EDCS-14900329.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a son for his deceased father, Lucius Aquilius Suavis, who belonged to the Quirina tribe.

EDCS-14900330.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Arria Sperata lived 53 years. She is buried here. Apuleius Licinianus placed this for his devoted wife.
EDCS-14900330.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Apuleius Licinianus to his devoted wife, Arria Sperata.

EDCS-14900331.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Alia Victoria lived 55 years. Assidonius Victoricus placed this for his excellent mother. She is buried here. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Assidonius Ingenuus lived 18 years, 6 months. Assidonius Liberalis, his devoted father, placed this for his excellent son. He is buried here. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Assidonius Liberalis lived 60 years. Assidonius Victoricus placed this for his dearest father. He is buried here.
EDCS-14900331.summary: This is a funerary monument commemorating three individuals: Alia Victoria (dedicated by her son), Assidonius Ingenuus (dedicated by his father), and Assidonius Liberalis (dedicated by his son), suggesting a family plot or monument across generations.

EDCS-14900332.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Assidonius Liberalis lived 47 years, 4 months, 5 days. He is buried here. Assidonius Calvus placed this for my master, his dearest brother.
EDCS-14900332.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Assidonius Calvus for his dearest brother, Assidonius Liberalis, referred to as 'master'.

EDCS-14900333.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Assidonia Aerope, devoted, lived 25 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-14900333.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Assidonia Aerope, who lived 25 years and is described as 'devoted'.

EDCS-14900334.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Aufidius Marius Castulus lived 25 years. He is buried here. His devoted father placed this.
EDCS-14900334.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a devoted father for his son, Aufidius Marius Castulus, who died at 25 years old.

EDCS-14900337.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Quintus Aurelius Impetratus lived 50 years. His sons placed this for their devoted father. He is buried here.
EDCS-14900337.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by the sons of Quintus Aurelius Impetratus to their devoted father.

EDCS-14900339.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Numisia Donata lived 34 years. She is buried here. Lucius Bolanus Felix placed this for his devoted wife. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Bolana Primula lived 52 years. She is buried here. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Lucius Bolanus Caelestinus lived 8 years. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Lucius Bolanus Felix lived [X years]. He is buried here.
EDCS-14900339.summary: This is a multi-part funerary monument, likely for a family, commemorating Numisia Donata (by her husband), Bolana Primula, Lucius Bolanus Caelestinus, and Lucius Bolanus Felix himself.

EDCS-14900341.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Caelia Donata, devoted, lived 100 years. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Marcus Caecilius Ianuarius lived 90 years.
EDCS-14900341.summary: This is a funerary monument for two individuals, Caelia Donata and Marcus Caecilius Ianuarius, both of whom lived to an advanced age.

EDCS-14900342.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. [Hel]via Gemellina lived 50 years. Matrona placed this for her mother. Sacred to the Divine Manes. Caelius Datus, a boy, lived 2 years. Matrona placed this. Sacred to the Divine Manes. Helvia Maior lived 31 years. Matrona placed this.
EDCS-14900342.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording three individuals: Helvia Gemellina, Caelius Datus, and Helvia Maior, for whom a woman named Matrona erected the monument.

EDCS-14900343.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Caelius Epicurus, pious, lived 32 years, 6 months. He is buried here. Publius Caelius Numisianus [placed this] for his excellent brother.
EDCS-14900343.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Lucius Caelius Epicurus, who lived for 32 years and 6 months, erected by his brother Publius Caelius Numisianus.

EDCS-14900344.translation: Marcus Caelius, son of Publius, of the Arnensian tribe, Quadratus, lived 50 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-14900344.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Caelius Quadratus, son of Publius, who belonged to the Arnensian tribe and lived for 50 years.

EDCS-14900346.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Calpurnia Victorina, also known as Faeda, lived 80 years, 1 month, 1 day. Gaius Iulius Proculus, her husband, piously placed this for his wife; she is buried here. Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gaius Iulius Proculus lived 101 years, 7 months. He is buried here.
EDCS-14900346.summary: This is a double funerary inscription dedicated first by Gaius Iulius Proculus to his wife Calpurnia Victorina (also called Faeda), and subsequently commemorating Gaius Iulius Proculus himself, who lived to an old age.

EDCS-14900349.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Carvilius Albinus lived 100 years. Sacred to the Divine Manes. Carvilia Albina lived 18 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-14900349.summary: This is a double funerary inscription for Lucius Carvilius Albinus, who lived to be 100, and for Carvilia Albina, who died at 18.

EDCS-14900351.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Cassius Faustus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 44 years. A good man, most necessary in business and to people, whom this great populace of his homeland seeks. The earth covers him, happy; but ungrateful light left him. Lucius Cassius F[---], his pious brother, placed this.
EDCS-14900351.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Lucius Cassius Faustus, a good and important man who lived 44 years, erected by his pious brother Lucius Cassius F[---].

EDCS-14900352.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Claudius Lucanus Tzainus lived 17 years, 10 months, 5 days. Claudius Sextilius Lucanus and Claudia Novella placed this for their sweetest son.
EDCS-14900352.summary: This funerary inscription was dedicated by Claudius Sextilius Lucanus and Claudia Novella to their dearest son, Claudius Lucanus Tzainus, who died at the age of 17 years, 10 months, and 5 days.

EDCS-14900353.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Tiberius Claudius Victor lived 70 years. Agria Rogata placed this for her sweetest husband. He is buried here. Whoever lives, will live forever.
EDCS-14900353.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Tiberius Claudius Victor, who lived 70 years, and was placed by his wife Agria Rogata, including a philosophical statement about eternal life.

EDCS-14900354.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Cluvius Donatus lived 31 years. His parents placed this for their son. He is buried here.
EDCS-14900354.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cluvius Donatus, who lived 31 years, set up by his parents for their son.

EDCS-14900358.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Cornelia Clodian lived 3 years, 8 months, 25 days. Her pious father placed this for his sweetest daughter.
EDCS-14900358.summary: This funerary inscription was dedicated by a pious father to his sweetest daughter, Cornelia Clodian, who died at a very young age of 3 years, 8 months, and 25 days.

EDCS-14900359.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Cornelia Crispina lived for 52 years; she lies here. Gaius Pinarius Vitalis placed this for his devoted mother.
EDCS-14900359.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording Cornelia Crispina's age and burial, dedicated by her son Gaius Pinarius Vitalis to his mother.

EDCS-14900361.translation: To the Divine Manes. Cornelia Romana, daughter of Marcus, lived for 25 years. Messius Saturninus, for his excellent wife, she lies here.
EDCS-14900361.summary: This is a tombstone commemorating Cornelia Romana, daughter of Marcus, dedicated by her husband Messius Saturninus.

EDCS-14900362.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Domitia Veneria lived for 95 years; she lies here. Fortunata placed this for her devoted mother.
EDCS-14900362.summary: This is a funerary monument for Domitia Veneria, erected by her daughter Fortunata.

EDCS-14900364.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Duronius Neptunalis lived for 25 years; he lies here. Sacred to the Divine Manes. Duronius Successor lived for 13 years; he lies here.
EDCS-14900364.summary: This is a double funerary inscription commemorating two individuals, Duronius Neptunalis and Duronius Successor, likely sharing a single monument.

EDCS-14900365.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Aetria Victoria lived for 44 years. Her devoted parents placed this; she lies here.
EDCS-14900365.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aetria Victoria, dedicated by her devoted parents.

EDCS-14900366.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Euplius lived for 37 years and 7 days; he lies here. Valeria Concordia placed this for her dearest father.
EDCS-14900366.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Euplius, dedicated by his daughter Valeria Concordia to her father.

EDCS-14900369.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Flavia Primitiva Crescentina lived for 1 year [...
EDCS-14900369.summary: This is an incomplete funerary inscription for Flavia Primitiva Crescentina, who died at the age of one.

EDCS-14900370.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Flavia Secur, who lived for 18 years; he lies here.
EDCS-14900370.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Secur, recording her age at death, with a grammatical error in the final word indicating gender.

EDCS-14900371.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gaius Gabinius Ingenuus lived for [...
EDCS-14900371.summary: This is an incomplete funerary inscription for Gaius Gabinius Ingenuus.

EDCS-14900372.translation: Gargilia Saturnina the devoted, lived for 80 years; she lies here. Her daughters Postumia Pacata, Sperata, and Quieta placed this for their devoted mother.
EDCS-14900372.summary: This is a funerary monument for Gargilia Saturnina, dedicated by her three daughters Postumia Pacata, Sperata, and Quieta.

EDCS-14900373.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Geminius Fortunatianus lived 22 years, 7 months, 14 days. His pious father placed this. Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Pinar[ia He]donin[a] lived 40 years, [---] months, [---] days, [---].
EDCS-14900373.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the deaths of Geminius Fortunatianus, erected by his father, and of Pinaria Hedonina, likely erected by a relative, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14900374.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Geminius Laetus, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 66 years. He lies here.
EDCS-14900374.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Geminius Laetus, likely placed by his family, indicating his age and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-14900375.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Herennius, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, lived 25 years, 4 months, 20 days. He lies here. Quintus Herennius, his father, placed this for his pious son.
EDCS-14900375.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Quintus Herennius for his son, Titus Herennius, recording the son's age at death and his tribal affiliation.

EDCS-14900376.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Herennia Celsiana, pious, lived 40 years, 6 months. She lies here.
EDCS-14900376.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Herennia Celsiana, likely placed by her family, noting her piety and age at death.

EDCS-14900377.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Julius Felix lived 60 years, 3 months. Augendus, his son, placed this for his dearest father.
EDCS-14900377.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Augendus for his beloved father, Julius Felix, recording the father's age at death.

EDCS-15100322.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Julius Licinianus lived 19 years, 8 months. He lies here. Julius Secundianus, his pious father, placed this. Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Manilia Saturnina lived 2 years, 10 months. She lies here. Julius Secundianus, her pious grandfather, placed this. Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Cornelia Julia Crescentia lived 22 years. She lies here. Julius Secundianus, her dearest husband, placed this.
EDCS-15100322.summary: This is a complex funerary monument recording the deaths of Julius Licinianus, Manilia Saturnina, and Cornelia Julia Crescentia, all seemingly commemorated by Julius Secundianus as father, grandfather, and husband respectively.

EDCS-15100324.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus Julius Saturninus, pious, lived 35 years. He lies here. Quintus Julius Fortunatus, a good man, placed this for his pious brother.
EDCS-15100324.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Julius Saturninus, erected by his brother, Quintus Julius Fortunatus, noting the deceased's piety and age.

EDCS-15100325.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Julius [---] lived [---] years. [---] Cimus lived 85 years. He lies here.
EDCS-15100325.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription for Gaius Julius [---] and [---] Cimus, the latter recorded as living to 85 years, likely part of a family monument.

EDCS-15100326.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Julius [---]nus lived 32 years, [---] months. He lies here. Julius Liberalis placed this for his brother.
EDCS-15100326.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julius [---]nus, erected by his brother Julius Liberalis, recording the deceased's age at death.

EDCS-15100327.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Julia Nampamina lived 80 years. Julius Antiochenus placed this. She lies here. Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Julia [---] lived [---].
EDCS-15100327.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julia Nampamina, erected by Julius Antiochenus, stating her age at death, and possibly part of a larger monument for another Julia.

EDCS-15100330.translation: Iunia Fructuosa, daughter of Marcus, lived 22 years, 11 months. She lies here. Pleminius Donatus placed this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-15100330.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Iunia Fructuosa and dedicated by her husband, Pleminius Donatus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15100331.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Memia Quietula lived for [---] years. She lies here. Liberalis for herself, and the same Quietula placed this for herself while alive. Sacred to the Divine Shades. Liberalis lived for [---] years. He lies here. She placed this for herself while he was alive.
EDCS-15100331.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Memia Quietula and Liberalis, seemingly a couple who prepared their own tomb while still alive, probably dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15100332.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Licinius Donatus lived 60 years. He lies here. Licinia Tertiola placed this tomb for her father out of piety.
EDCS-15100332.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Licinius Donatus, erected by his daughter Licinia Tertiola, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15100333.translation: To the Divine Shades. Ligarius Ligarianus lived 30 years. Ligarius Liberalis placed this for his dutiful brother. He lies here.
EDCS-15100333.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ligarius Ligarianus, dedicated by his brother Ligarius Liberalis, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15100334.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Sextus Magnius Verus lived 53 years. He lies here. Quintus Magnius Hilarus(?) placed this for his dutiful father.
EDCS-15100334.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Magnius Verus, erected by his son Quintus Magnius Hilarus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15100335.translation: [To the Divine Shades.] Manilius Longus, son of Lucius, lived 54 years. He lies here. Quintus Manilius Maximus placed this for his best and dutiful [relative].
EDCS-15100335.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Manilius Longus, dedicated by Quintus Manilius Maximus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15100336.translation: Quintus Manilius Maximus, perpetual flamen, lived 52 years, 5 months. He lies here. Iulia Stolis placed this for her dutiful husband.
EDCS-15100336.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Manilius Maximus, who was a flamen, erected by his wife Iulia Stolis, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15100337.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Manilia Rufina lived 45 years. Vinnius Silo placed this for his most dutiful wife. She lies here.
EDCS-15100337.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Manilia Rufina, dedicated by her husband Vinnius Silo, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15100338.translation: [To the Divine Shades.] For Mantia Lucisca, daughter of Publius, best grandmother, lived 80 years.
EDCS-15100338.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Mantia Lucisca, a grandmother, likely erected by her descendants, probably dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15100339.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Publius Marius Pasi[---] lived 75 years. Publius Marius Saturninus, his son, placed this for his father.
EDCS-15100339.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Marius Pasi[---], dedicated by his son Publius Marius Saturninus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15100340.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Marcia Fortunata lived 90 years; she is buried here. Her sons, Marcius Fortunatus, Secundinus, Ampliatus, and Honoratus, erected this for their devoted mother.
EDCS-15100340.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of Marcia Fortunata, set up by her four sons for their pious mother.

EDCS-15100341.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Margarita lived 20 years; she is buried here. Venustus placed this for his wife.
EDCS-15100341.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Margarita, erected by her husband Venustus.

EDCS-15100342.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Quintus Marius Felix lived 67 years; he is buried here. Perellia Saturnina placed this for her husband.
EDCS-15100342.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Marius Felix, dedicated by his wife Perellia Saturnina.

EDCS-15100345.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Memmia Saturnina... Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. [A man named --- Satur--- placed this]... Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Quintus Memmius Saturninus lived 83 years; he is buried here. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Aemilia Donata lived 90 years; she is buried here. Memmius Aemilianus placed this for his devoted mother.
EDCS-15100345.summary: This monument contains multiple funerary inscriptions: one for Memmia Saturnina, one for an unknown individual, one for Quintus Memmius Saturninus, and another for Aemilia Donata, erected by her son Memmius Aemilianus.

EDCS-15100346.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Mevia Felicitas lived 40 years. Quintus Calpurnius Fortunatus, her husband, placed this for his dearest wife. An undeserving wife, you decided to depart life too quickly, poor thing. You should have lived for nearly a hundred years; it was permitted. For her beauty was certain, and her character eloquent, and her modesty I would say was not found in others nor is it to be contended. Since the Manes exist, may the earth be light for you. She is buried here.
EDCS-15100346.summary: This is an elaborate funerary inscription for Mevia Felicitas, erected by her husband Quintus Calpurnius Fortunatus, expressing profound grief and praise for her virtues.

EDCS-15100347.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Mevia (?) Felicitas lived 4 years, 4 months. Her devoted mother placed this.
EDCS-15100347.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young girl named Mevia (?) Felicitas, erected by her grieving mother.

EDCS-15100348.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Gaius Sentius Fortunatus lived 90 years. Gaius Sentius Rogatianus, his son, placed this for his devoted father; he is buried here. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Gaius Minucius Valentinus lived 88 years. His devoted sons placed this for their father; he is buried here.
EDCS-15100348.summary: This monument includes two funerary inscriptions: one for Gaius Sentius Fortunatus, erected by his son Gaius Sentius Rogatianus, and another for Gaius Minucius Valentinus, set up by his sons.

EDCS-15100349.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. [---]tia, daughter of Sextus... Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Modia Maximina, daughter of Gaius, a devoted woman, lived 24 years; she is buried here. Gaius Modius Maximus placed this for his excellent daughter. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Gaius Modius Maximinus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 24 years, 2 months; he is buried here. Gaius Modius Maximus placed this for his son.
EDCS-15100349.summary: This monument contains multiple funerary inscriptions: one for an unknown woman, one for Modia Maximina dedicated by her father Gaius Modius Maximus, and another for Gaius Modius Maximinus, also dedicated by his father Gaius Modius Maximus.

EDCS-15100352.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Nonia Suriscina, daughter of Marcus, is buried here.
EDCS-15100352.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription marking the burial place of Nonia Suriscina.

EDCS-15100353.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. For Quintus Orbius Salutaris Connarius, who lived 28 years, 1 month, 26 days; he is buried here. The Orbii placed this for their foster child.
EDCS-15100353.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Orbius Salutaris Connarius, a foster child, erected by the Orbius family.

EDCS-15100355.translation: Here, in peaceful rest among the spirits of the dead, Passiena Casta reposes. She lived 45 years, pious, generous, benevolent. Her parents and brothers established this tomb for her.
EDCS-15100355.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Passiena Casta, set up by her parents and brothers, likely from the Imperial period given the formulaic language.

EDCS-15100356.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Petronia Casta lived 75 years; she is buried here. Kastus, her son, placed this for his mother.
EDCS-15100356.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Petronia Casta, erected by her son Kastus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-15100357.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Plotius Alexander lived [---]
EDCS-15100357.summary: This is an incomplete funerary inscription for Plotius Alexander, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-15100358.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Pompeia Fortunata lived 85 years. Her dutiful sons placed this. // Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Publius Sextilius(?) Donatus lived 60 years. His dutiful wife placed this for her husband.
EDCS-15100358.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording two separate burials: one for Pompeia Fortunata, set up by her sons, and another for Publius Sextilius Donatus, placed by his wife, both likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-15100359.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Pomponia Quarta, daughter of Gaius, pious, lived 18 years.
EDCS-15100359.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Pomponia Quarta, a young woman, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-15100360.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Pomponia Valeria lived 15 years [---]
EDCS-15100360.summary: This is an incomplete funerary inscription for Pomponia Valeria, a young woman, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-15100362.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Publius Praecilius Pollio, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, lived [---] years; he is buried here. Praecilius Fidus and Vitalis, his brothers, took care of having this made.
EDCS-15100362.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Praecilius Pollio, set up by his brothers Praecilius Fidus and Vitalis, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-15100363.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Refrius Successus lived 47 years; he is buried here. Refrius Nundinarius placed this for his dutiful brother.
EDCS-15100363.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Refrius Successus, erected by his brother Refrius Nundinarius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-15100364.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Gaius Remmius Felix, son of Gaius, a fodder-dealer, dutiful, lived 21 years.
EDCS-15100364.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Remmius Felix, a fodder-dealer, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-15100365.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Remmia Tertullina Mustelica lived 23 years. Gaius Titius Clemens(?), dutiful, placed this for his dutiful wife; she is buried here.
EDCS-15100365.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Remmia Tertullina Mustelica, set up by her husband Gaius Titius Clemens(?), likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-15100366.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. To Remmia Secunda, daughter of Gaius, who lived 28 years.
EDCS-15100366.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a tomb to Remmia Secunda, daughter of Gaius, who died at the age of 28.

EDCS-15100367.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Publius Rutilius Vitalis, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 38 years; he is buried here. Publius Rutilius Fortunatus erected this for his excellent brother.
EDCS-15100367.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Publius Rutilius Vitalis, who lived 38 years, and was erected by his brother, Publius Rutilius Fortunatus.

EDCS-15100368.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Quintus Sempronius Lucifer, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, a dutiful man, lived 80 years. Quintus Sempronius Antenor and Sempronia Mallina, his heirs, erected this for their patron.
EDCS-15100368.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by the heirs Quintus Sempronius Antenor and Sempronia Mallina to their patron, Quintus Sempronius Lucifer, who lived 80 years.

EDCS-15100369.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. To Sempronia Mallina, who lived 80 years...
EDCS-15100369.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sempronia Mallina, who lived to be 80 years old, but the text is incomplete.

EDCS-15100370.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. To Sextia Fortunata, who lived for [---] years...
EDCS-15100370.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to Sextia Fortunata.

EDCS-15100371.translation: To Tannonius Marcellus, who lived 70 years. Tannonius Sterceius, his son, erected this for his dutiful father; he is buried here.
EDCS-15100371.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Tannonius Sterceius for his father, Tannonius Marcellus, who lived 70 years.

EDCS-15100372.translation: Gaius Thar[---], son of Titus [---], one year, one month [---]; he is buried here [---] his [---].
EDCS-15100372.summary: This is a highly fragmentary funerary inscription for a young individual, Gaius Thar[---], who died at a young age.

EDCS-15100373.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Marcus Titinius Fuscus lived 80 years; he is buried here. Gaius Titinius Fuscus erected this for his father. // Sacred to the Divine Manes. Agria Extricata lived 70 years; she is buried here. Gaius Titinius made this for his mother. // Sacred to the Divine Manes. Iulia Victoria lived 50 years. Gaius Titinius Fuscus erected this for his wife.
EDCS-15100373.summary: This monument is a series of three funerary inscriptions by Gaius Titinius Fuscus dedicating memorials to his father Marcus Titinius Fuscus, his mother Agria Extricata, and his wife Iulia Victoria.
