EDCS-23200266.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Marcus Assidonius Marianus, described as 'dutiful', indicating a dedication by a relative, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200267.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Lucius Atilius Maioricus, lived 40 years. Atilius Faustianus placed this for his dearest brother. He is buried here. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. [First name illegible] Atilius Clodianus, lived 23 years. His son placed this. He is buried here.
EDCS-23200267.summary: This is a double funerary inscription, one dedicated by Atilius Faustianus to his dearest brother Lucius Atilius Maioricus, and another by a son to his father Atilius Clodianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200271.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. G. Pescenia, lived 2 years. She is buried here. Ianuarius placed this for his daughter. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Augendus, lived 4 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-23200271.summary: This is a double funerary inscription: one dedicated by Ianuarius to his daughter G. Pescenia, who lived 2 years, and another for Augendus, who lived 4 years, both likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200272.translation: Titus Caecilius Macer, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, lived 20 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-23200272.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Caecilius Macer, a young man who died at the age of 20, providing his full name and tribe, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200274.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Aemilia Restutula, lived 21 years. She is buried here. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Caelius Marius, lived 60 years. Claudia Donata placed this for her husband.
EDCS-23200274.summary: This is a double funerary inscription, one for Aemilia Restutula who lived 21 years, and another dedicated by Claudia Donata to her husband Caelius Marius, who lived 60 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200276.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. To Caelia Octavia, lived [illegible] 13 years, 10 months. She is buried here. [Name illegible] placed this for his most chaste and dearest wife.
EDCS-23200276.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Caelia Octavia, who lived 13 years and 10 months, dedicated by her husband to his most chaste and dearest wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200277.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Caninia Verecunda Italica, lived 21 years. She is buried here. Pompeius Aemilianus, her husband, placed this for his incomparable wife. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Pompeia Fortunata, lived 27 years. She is buried here. Her dutiful father placed this.
EDCS-23200277.summary: This is a double funerary inscription: one dedicated by Pompeius Aemilianus to his incomparable wife Caninia Verecunda Italica, who lived 21 years; and another by a dutiful father for his daughter Pompeia Fortunata, who lived 27 years, both likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200278.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Cutullus lived for 48 years. His devoted sister placed this. He is laid here.
EDCS-23200278.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Cutullus at age 48, set up by his sister, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200280.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Iulia Memoria lived for 25 years; she is laid here. Iulius Secundianus placed this for his dearest mother. // Sacred to the Divine Shades. Publius Cornelius Massuricus lived for 3 years, 5 months. Gaius Massuricus, his devoted father, placed this for his son. // Aelia Crescentia lived for 70 years; she is laid here. Cornelius Massuricus placed this.
EDCS-23200280.summary: This is a funerary monument comprising three separate inscriptions, dedicated to Iulia Memoria by her son, to Publius Cornelius Massuricus by his father, and to Aelia Crescentia by Cornelius Massuricus, all likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200281.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Publius Cornelius Secundianus lived for 5 years; he is laid here. Iulius Saturninus placed this for his devoted son. // Sacred to the Divine Shades. Publius Cornelius Victor, also known as Massuricus, lived for 46 years; he is laid here. Iulia Saturnina placed this for her devoted husband. // Sacred to the Divine Shades. Iulia Prima lived for 15 years; she is laid here. Iulia Saturnina placed this for her devoted foster-child.
EDCS-23200281.summary: This monument contains three distinct funerary inscriptions, one for Publius Cornelius Secundianus by his father, another for Publius Cornelius Victor by his wife, and a third for Iulia Prima by her foster-mother, Iulia Saturnina, indicative of the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200282.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Concordia Pia lived for 7 years and 7 months. Ianuarius, her father, placed this for his devoted daughter. She is laid here.
EDCS-23200282.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by the father, Ianuarius, to his young daughter, Concordia Pia, likely dating to the Imperial era.

EDCS-23200284.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Clodia Secunda lived for 20 years; she is laid here. Iulius Maximus Barda placed this for his devoted wife.
EDCS-23200284.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Clodia Secunda, aged 20, set up by her husband Iulius Maximus Barda, typical of the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200286.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Steia Donatula, [daughter of] Rupila, lived for 50 years; she is laid here. Titus Lartius Quintulus placed this for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-23200286.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Steia Donatula, aged 50, and was erected by her husband Titus Lartius Quintulus, typical of the Imperial era.

EDCS-23200287.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Fabia Secunda lived for 16 years; she is laid here. Her parents placed this for their sweetest daughter. // Na[---] TA[---] lived for [---] years [---]
EDCS-23200287.summary: This is a funerary monument with two separate inscriptions, the first dedicated to Fabia Secunda by her parents, and the second, largely fragmented, for another individual, both from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200288.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Iulia Aemiliana lived for 48 years; she is laid here. // Sacred to the Divine Shades. Quintus Fannius Longinus lived for 52 years; he is laid here.
EDCS-23200288.summary: This monument contains two separate funerary inscriptions, one for Iulia Aemiliana and another for Quintus Fannius Longinus, both likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200289.translation: EM[---]OROS // To the Divine Shades // Sacred to the Divine Shades. Flavia Dativa lived for 60 years. Flavia Restuta placed this for her dearest sister. I, Flavia Restuta, and Flavia Pulla [did this].
EDCS-23200289.summary: This monument features three parts: a fragmented inscription, a simple dedication to the Divine Shades, and a funerary dedication by Flavia Restuta and Flavia Pulla for Flavia Dativa, their sister, typical of the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200290.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. [Munatius Q]uintasius lived for [---] years, [---] days; he is laid here. [---] placed this for his devoted [---]. // Sacred to the Divine Shades. Flavia C. Piscenila lived for 25 years, 4 months; she is laid here. Munatius Quintasius placed this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-23200290.summary: This monument contains two funerary inscriptions, one for a partially preserved Munatius Quintasius erected by an unnamed dedicator, and another for Flavia C. Piscenila, placed by her husband Munatius Quintasius, both characteristic of the Imperial period.

EDCS-23200291.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Geminius Felix lived 51 years, 12 months. I...
EDCS-23200291.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Geminius Felix, noting his age at death and likely set up by a loved one, though the dedicator is incomplete.

EDCS-23200292.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Germanus lived for [---] years.
EDCS-23200292.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Germanus, recording his name and a portion of his age.

EDCS-23200293.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Helvius Donatus lived for [---] years; he is buried here. His most devoted sons placed this.
EDCS-23200293.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Helvius Donatus, placed by his devoted sons, recording his age and burial place.

EDCS-23200295.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Iulius Donatus lived 22 years. His parents made this for their dutiful son. He is buried here.
EDCS-23200295.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Iulius Donatus, erected by his parents for their pious son, stating his age and burial location.

EDCS-23200296.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Pudentilla lived 85 years. Her most beloved daughter made this for her mother. // Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Iulius Felix lived 82 years, 7 months. Unworthily, Felix, you lost your life too quickly, poor man. You should have lived almost 100 years. If there are spirits, may the earth be light for you. He is buried here. His most beloved wife [made this].
EDCS-23200296.summary: This is a funerary monument for two individuals, Pudentilla and Gaius Iulius Felix; Pudentilla's epitaph was placed by her daughter, while Gaius Iulius Felix's lengthy epitaph, expressing grief and a wish for a light earth, was placed by his wife.

EDCS-23200303.translation: Iulia Casta lived 36 years, 9 months. Iuli[---] Vic[---].
EDCS-23200303.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Casta, recording her age at death, with the remainder of the text being fragmentary.

EDCS-23200337.translation: [---]uria [---]onilla [---] 1 year, 11 months. She is buried here.
EDCS-23200337.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for a woman named [---]onilla, recording her very young age at death and her burial place.

EDCS-23200445.text_edition: Pro restituta salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Armeniaci Parthici Medici pontificis maximi patris patriae tribunicia potestate XXIII consulis III imperatoris V civitas Mactaritanorum  extruxit anno Sexti Laterani proconsulis clarissimi viri Lucius Rupilius Au legatus eius dedicavit
EDCS-23200445.translation: For the restored health of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Parthicus Medicus, pontifex maximus, father of his country, in his 23rd tribunician power, 3rd consulship, 5th imperatorial acclamation, the city of Mactaris built this in the year of Sextus Lateranus, the most distinguished man, proconsul. Lucius Rupilius Au[---], his legate, dedicated it.
EDCS-23200445.summary: This public inscription records the construction of a monument or building by the city of Mactaris for the restored health of Emperor Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus) in 221 CE, and its dedication by the proconsul's legate.

EDCS-23200467.translation: Caeselia Namina [---]. [---]lianus, dutiful, lived. Dutiful, she lived for [---] years. [---] years. [---] I was [---]. I was born of humble, poor parents, who had neither wealth nor a home. From the time I was born, I lived by cultivating the countryside, and there was never rest for me in the countryside, nor always for me. And when the year produced ripe crops, then I was the first harvester of the stalks. When the scythe-wielding troop of men advanced into the fields, whether heading for Cirta, Nomados, or Jupiter's fields, I, the first harvester, went before all in the fields, leaving dense swathes behind my back. I cut twelve harvests under the fierce sun, and afterwards I became a leader from that work. We led eleven troops of reapers for eleven years, and our hand cut the fields of Numidia. This labor and life, content with little, made me master of a home, and a villa is ready, and the house itself lacks no resources. And our life received the fruits of honors; I myself was written among the enrolled. I was chosen from the order in the temple and sat, and I myself, from a rustic, was a censor. And I begat and saw young men and dear grandsons. For the merits of life, we spent illustrious years, which no harsh tongue harms with accusation. Learn, mortals, to lead a life without crime; thus he deserved who lived without fraud to die. // Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Mulceius Maximus lived 30 years. // Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Sextus Aurelius F[---]nus lived 40 years.
EDCS-23200467.summary: This extensive funerary monument contains three separate epitaphs: a very long, moralizing autobiographical poem for an unnamed individual (likely the head of the family, detailing his rise from humble origins to wealth and civic honor), and two shorter, standard epitaphs for Gaius Mulceius Maximus and Sextus Aurelius F[---]nus, likely family members.

EDCS-23400751.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, the city of Uzappa [erected this] by decree of the decurions with public money.
EDCS-23400751.summary: This is a public dedicatory inscription by the city of Uzappa to Emperor Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus), funded by public money and decreed by the decurions, likely for a statue or monument.

EDCS-23400753.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Probus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, holding tribunician power, Father of his Country, Consul for the third time, the citizens of Uzappa (dedicated this).
EDCS-23400753.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the citizens of Uzappa to Emperor Probus, likely on a public monument or building, dating to around 279 AD during his third consulship.

EDCS-23400833.text_edition: Pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi Britannici maximi fili divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepotis divi Antonini Pii pronepotis divi Hadriani abnepotis divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepotis Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis principis iuventutis Augusti Parthici maximi Brittannici maximi pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate XV imperatoris II consulis III patris patriae et Iuliae Domnae Augustae Piae Felicis matris Augusti et castrorum et senatus totiusque domus divinae Publius Opstorius Saturninus flamen perpetuus sacerdos Mercuri cum patriae suae Vazitanae triplicata summa flamoni perpetui HS III milibus nummum aedem Mercurio Sobrio pollicitus fuisset ampliata liberalitate eandem aedem cum pronao et ara fecit et ob dedicationem aepulum sic et gymnasium dedit loco dato decreto decurionum idem iam ante hoc ob honorem XI primatus aedem Aesculapio deo promissam bassilicam sic cohaerentem multiplicata pecunia fecit
EDCS-23400833.translation: For the well-being of Emperor Caesar, the deified Septimius Severus Pius, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva; and of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix, Prince of Youth, Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 15th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 3rd time, Father of his Country; and of Julia Domna Augusta, Pious, Felix, Mother of Augustus, of the camps, and of the senate, and of the entire divine household: Publius Opstorius Saturninus, perpetual flamen, priest of Mercury, having promised to his homeland, Vazitana, an additional sum of 3,000 sesterces for a perpetual flamen, and having promised a temple to Mercury Sobrius, with expanded generosity, built the same temple with a pronaos and altar, and for the dedication, provided a feast and a gymnasium, on a site granted by decree of the decurions. The same person, even before this, for the honor of his eleventh primacy, built a promised temple to the god Aesculapius, and an adjoining basilica, with multiplied funds.
EDCS-23400833.summary: This elaborate inscription records the extensive benefactions of Publius Opstorius Saturninus, a perpetual flamen and priest of Mercury, who built a temple to Mercury Sobrius, a gymnasium, and a temple to Aesculapius with an adjoining basilica in Vazitana, all dedicated to the imperial family and dating to the early 3rd century AD during the reign of Caracalla.

EDCS-23500042.text_edition: Victoriae Augustae ducatu instantiaque Claudi Constantis procuratoris Augusti contigit des et Musulamios gentesque aliasnminmerumptest anno  usque
EDCS-23500042.translation: By the victory of Augusta, and by the leadership and diligence of Claudius Constans, procurator of Augustus, it happened that the des and Musulamii and other numerous peoples were up to the year…
EDCS-23500042.summary: This fragmentary inscription seems to record a military victory against the Des and Musulamii tribes, likely achieved through the leadership of a procurator, Claudius Constans, under the auspices of an empress (Augusta).

EDCS-23500043.translation: Rasinia Secunda returned (died) on the 16th Kalends of November (October 17th) in the 199th year of the province.
EDCS-23500043.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death date of Rasinia Secunda, specified by the provincial era as October 17th, 199 A.P.

EDCS-24100009.text_edition: Mercurio et Minervae  Animosa Symmachi filia dedit dedicavit
EDCS-24100009.translation: To Mercury and Minerva, Animosa, daughter of Symmachus, gave and dedicated this.
EDCS-24100009.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Animosa, daughter of Symmachus, to the Roman gods Mercury and Minerva, likely for a shrine or an offering.

EDCS-24100011.text_edition: [Fulviae Plau][tillae Augustae] Antonini Augusti Pii sponsae dedicante Flavio Celere procuratore liberti et familia splendidissimi vectigalis IIII publicorum Africae
EDCS-24100011.translation: To Fulvia Plautilla Augusta, betrothed of Antoninus Augustus Pius, dedicated by Flavius Celer, procurator of the freedmen and staff of the most splendid tax of the four public revenues of Africa.
EDCS-24100011.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Fulvia Plautilla Augusta, the betrothed of Emperor Caracalla (Antoninus Augustus Pius), by Flavius Celer, an imperial procurator, likely commemorating a public work or monument in Africa during her brief status as empress.

EDCS-24100012.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis decem sic et octies imperator duodecies consul ter pater patriae proconsul coepit et ex maiore parte perfecit Imperator Caesar divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi Britannici maximi filius  divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepos divi Antonini Pii pronepos divi Hadriani abnepos divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepos Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurellius sic Antoninus Pius Augustus Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus Germanicus maximus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis decem sic novies imperator ter consul quater pater patriae proconsul perfici curavit
EDCS-24100012.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power eighteen times, imperator twelve times, consul three times, Father of his Country, proconsul, began and for the most part completed this work. Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Septimius Severus Pius, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva; Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power nineteen times, imperator three times, consul four times, Father of his Country, proconsul, took care to finish it.
EDCS-24100012.summary: This monumental inscription celebrates the construction of a public work, initiated by Emperor Septimius Severus and largely completed by him, and subsequently finished by his son, Emperor Caracalla, dating to the period after 213 AD when Caracalla held his fourth consulship.

EDCS-24100014.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare Vespasiano Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate III imperatore X consule IIII designato V patre patriae Quintus Manlius Ancharius Tarquitius Saturninus proconsul patronus dedicavit  Volumnius Memor Felix legatus pro praetore Iddibal Balsillecis filius Annobalis nepos Asmunis proneposus templum Matris Magnae et exornavit ex HS CC milibus nummum de sua pecunia dedit is et Passienus Maris E
EDCS-24100014.translation: During the reign of Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the third time, imperator for the tenth time, consul for the fourth time, designated consul for the fifth time, Father of his Country: Quintus Manlius Ancharius Tarquitius Saturninus, proconsul and patron, dedicated this. Volumnius Memor Felix, legate pro praetore, and Iddibal, son of Balsillec, grandson of Annobal, great-grandson of Asmun, dedicated and adorned the temple of Magna Mater with 200,000 sesterces from their own money. The same and Passienus Maris E (also contributed).
EDCS-24100014.summary: This inscription records the dedication and adornment of a temple to Magna Mater by Quintus Manlius Ancharius Tarquitius Saturninus, proconsul and patron, along with Volumnius Memor Felix, a legate, and Iddibal, who contributed funds, during the reign of Emperor Vespasian, specifically around 72/73 AD.

EDCS-24100015.text_edition: NI   OMM  honestissim cognoscendi   perpenso qu MEATO ob   qurem publicam exquisitisisionum generibus fecerit ampliorem instauratori moenium publicorum quod eius innumera circa se ac suos officia supra genitalis civis affectum Lepcis magna inclita fide devotione praestans multifariam senserit meritorum eius tenacissime memor per ordinis sui et populi viros Flavio Victori Calpurnio viro perfectissimo praesidi provinciae Tripolitanae patrono suo statuam decrevit et ob individuum mutui amoris affectum eamdem sic se propter constituit ac dedicavit
EDCS-24100015.translation: …the most honorable… having considered… by which he greatly increased the public good through various kinds of excellent services, a restorer of public walls, because Lepcis, great and renowned for its steadfast loyalty and devotion, has experienced his innumerable good deeds towards itself and its people, beyond the affection of a native citizen, and, most tenaciously mindful of his merits, has decreed a statue to Flavius Victor Calpurnius, most perfect man, governor of the province of Tripolitana, its patron, through the men of its own order and people. And due to the indivisible affection of mutual love, it positioned and dedicated the same statue next to itself.
EDCS-24100015.summary: This inscription records the erection and dedication of a statue to Flavius Victor Calpurnius, the governor of Tripolitana and patron of Lepcis Magna, by the city's citizens and councilors in gratitude for his numerous public services and affection for the city.

EDCS-24100028.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Aelia Arisuth vixit annos sexaginta plus minus  Dis Manibus sacrum Aelios Ma Iurathani bixit sic annosaginta quinque plus minus
EDCS-24100028.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Aelia Arisuth lived about sixty years. Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Aelios Ma(---) Iurathan lived about sixty-five years.
EDCS-24100028.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating two individuals, Aelia Arisuth and Aelios Ma(---) Iurathan, and records their approximate ages at death.

EDCS-24300128.translation: By the sacred indulgence of our lords Constantius and Constans Maximus...
EDCS-24300128.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription recording an act of imperial indulgence granted by the two emperors Constantius and Constans, likely dating to the mid-4th century AD.

EDCS-24300174.text_edition: Caius Geminius Rufus pius vixit annos LVII hic situs est
EDCS-24300174.translation: Gaius Geminius Rufus, pious, lived 57 years. He is interred here.
EDCS-24300174.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Geminius Rufus, a pious individual who lived 57 years, indicating a tombstone or burial monument.

EDCS-24300230.text_edition: Imperatore Nerva Caesare Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate consule III Quinto Fabio Barbaro Valerio Magno Iuliano legato Augusti pro praetore castellus sic Thigensium
EDCS-24300230.translation: When Emperor Nerva Caesar Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, consul for the third time, and Quintus Fabius Barbarus Valerius Magnus Iulianus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, were in office, the fort of Thigensium was built.
EDCS-24300230.summary: This inscription records the construction of a fort at Thigensium under Emperor Nerva and his legate, Quintus Fabius Barbarus Valerius Magnus Iulianus, precisely dated to Nerva's third consulate (AD 97).

EDCS-24501002.text_edition: Extricata Imr serva Felicis saltuari filia pia vixit annis IIII hic sita
EDCS-24501002.translation: Extricata, servant of Imr, daughter of Felix the forest ranger, pious, lived 4 years. She is interred here.
EDCS-24501002.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Extricata, a young servant girl and daughter of a forest ranger, who died at the age of four.

EDCS-24501023.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum  Lucius Pullaienus Papias pius vixit annos XVIII hic situs est  Primigenius pater posuit
EDCS-24501023.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Pullaienus Papias, pious, lived 18 years. He is interred here. Primigenius, his father, set this up.
EDCS-24501023.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Lucius Pullaienus Papias by his father, Primigenius, commemorating his son who died at age 18.

EDCS-24501045.translation: To Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 3rd time, imperator for the 7th time, father of the country, proconsul, the municipium Felix Thabbora, devoted to his divinity and majesty, [dedicated this].
EDCS-24501045.summary: This is a honorific inscription from the municipium Felix Thabbora, dedicated to Emperor Constantine the Great, listing his full titles and acknowledging the town's devotion to him, likely dating to AD 313 (his 8th tribunician power and 3rd consulate).

EDCS-24501052.text_edition: Amodb Secundi IAI ASAE pia vixit annos XVI  Lucius Secundus Mutthuni LIMBENE pius vixit annos LXXXV  Felix filius parentibus fecit
EDCS-24501052.translation: Amodb, daughter of Secundus, pious, lived 16 years. Lucius Secundus Mutthuni, pious, lived 85 years. Felix, their son, made this for his parents.
EDCS-24501052.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Felix for his parents, Amodb (who lived 16 years) and Lucius Secundus Mutthuni (who lived 85 years).

EDCS-24501099.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Commodo Antonino Augusto V Manio Acilio Glabrione II consulibus pridie Kalendas Februarias  et possessorum civium ibi Victor Callitiosi filius et Honoratus  filius um quaestos quod agri suorum pecoribus ovium devastarentur et  tem conrosas sic esse quod ipsum initium honoris nostri at  sit facta etiam mentione sacrarum litterarum ONnostramque denuntiationem initium honoris nostrit nec debeat eis contumacia eorum prodesse et aliter ea res s secundum sacras constitutiones actum fuerit sic quae decreto subiciendam existimavimus  et aecum mihi desiderium vestrum videtur et exemplo adiuvari --- in? agrum vestrum invitis vobis pecora pascendi gratia induccare quod si ignorante domino servus induxerrit pecora  in ipsum servum proconsul severe constituet si iusso sic domini etiam praetium sic servi ex forma censoria |(denarios) D dominus dare debebit  si  sua sponte id admiserint a proconsule flectentur sic ita ut in relegentur quit sic fieri placeret de ea re universi censuerunt  passim in territorio unius cuiuscumque pecora pascendi facultatemncentur et cum etiam post ex constitutione
EDCS-24501099.translation: When Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus was consul for the fifth time with Manius Acilius Glabrio for the second time, the day before the Kalends of February, concerning the possessors of citizens there, Victor son of Callitiosus and Honoratus son of Um, whose fields were being devastated by sheep, and thus eroded, which was the very beginning of our honor, mention was also made in the sacred letters of our denunciation. The beginning of our honor should not be hindered by their defiance, and if the matter proceeds according to sacred constitutions, we have deemed it worthy of a decree. Your desire seems fair to me and supported by precedent... not to drive livestock into your fields against your will for grazing. If a slave drives livestock in without the owner's knowledge, the proconsul will severely punish the slave himself. If it is by the master's order, the master must pay 500 denarii as the price of the slave according to the censorial form. If they admit this of their own accord, they will be swayed by the proconsul so as to be exiled. What was decided in this matter by all: that the right to graze livestock freely throughout the territory of anyone should be limited, and after this constitution...
EDCS-24501099.summary: This is a lengthy epigraphic text recording a legal decision or decree concerning disputes over grazing rights and damage caused by livestock to agricultural fields, issued during the consulship of Commodus and Manius Acilius Glabrio (January 31, AD 186).

EDCS-24501155.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a list of three names, possibly indicating individuals connected to a monument, grave, or a dedication.

EDCS-24501257.translation: ...when Marullus and Aelianus were consuls, he willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-24501257.summary: This is a fragmented inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow, dated to the consulship of Marullus and Aelianus (AD 184), indicating a votive dedication.

EDCS-24501260.translation: [---] has fulfilled his vow willingly and with a joyful heart, in the consulship of Fuscianus and Silanus.
EDCS-24501260.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow in 217 CE, dated by the consulship of Fuscianus and Silanus.

EDCS-24501266.translation: [---] fulfilled his vow willingly and with a joyful heart on the 4th Kalends of May, in the consulship of Tertullus and Clemens.
EDCS-24501266.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow on April 28, 195 CE, dated by the consulship of Tertullus and Clemens.

EDCS-24501906.translation: By public decree of the municipality of Sufetula at Musunii. Marcus Iunius Rufinus Sabinianus, most distinguished man.
EDCS-24501906.summary: This inscription records a public dedication by the municipality of Sufetula to or by Marcus Iunius Rufinus Sabinianus, a man of senatorial rank.

EDCS-24501908.translation: By public decree of the municipality of Sufetula at Musunii. Marcus Iunius Rufinus [Sabinianus, most distinguished man].
EDCS-24501908.summary: This inscription records a public dedication by the municipality of Sufetula to or by Marcus Iunius Rufinus Sabinianus, a man of senatorial rank.

EDCS-24501949.translation: [---] servant of Gnaeus Domitius Tullus, imperial legate, his secretary. He lies here. Pompeius Stephanus took care of it.
EDCS-24501949.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a slave who was the secretary of Gnaeus Domitius Tullus, an imperial legate, erected by Pompeius Stephanus.

EDCS-24501950.text_edition: Epithymetus Sergia Corneli Cethegi proconsulis servus hic situs est vixit nnos X
EDCS-24501950.translation: Epithymetus, slave of Sergius Cornelius Cethegus the proconsul, lies here. He lived 10 years.
EDCS-24501950.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Epithymetus, a slave of Proconsul Sergius Cornelius Cethegus, who died at the age of 10.

EDCS-24501960.translation: Petronius Gallianus, also known as Harpatius, lived 45 years. He lies here. Pompeia Maxima placed this for her devoted husband.
EDCS-24501960.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Petronius Gallianus (also called Harpatius) who lived 45 years, erected by his wife Pompeia Maxima.

EDCS-24502091.translation: Sacred to the Mother of the Gods, Great Idaean Augusta. For the well-being of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Probus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, and his entire divine household, Quintus Arellius Optatianus, Roman knight, priest, having completed the sacred rites according to custom, including the sacrifice of rams and bulls, approved as pontiff by the decree of his colony of Mactaris, at his own expense, with Rannius Salvius, Roman knight, pontiff, and Claudius Faustus, priests, and all the dendrophori and consecrated men and women, fulfilled his vow willingly and with a joyful heart.
EDCS-24502091.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to the Great Idaean Mother of the Gods, made by Quintus Arellius Optatianus, a Roman knight and priest from Mactaris, for the well-being of Emperor Probus and his family, after performing sacred rites including criobolia and taurobolia, around 276-282 CE.

EDCS-24502092.text_edition: Matri Deum Magnae Ideae Augustae sacrum pro salute Impperatorum Caessarum Cai Valeri Diocletiani Pii Felicis Augusti et Marci Aureli Valeri M[axi][miani] Pii Pii Felicis Augusti totiusque divinae domus eorum Quintus Minthonius Fortunatus sacerdos perfectis ritae sic sacris cernorum crioboli et tauroboli suffragio ordinis coloniae suae Mactaritanae comprobatus antistes sumptibus suis tradente Claudio Bono sacerdote una cum universis dendroforiis sic et sacratis utriusque sexus votum solvit libens animo
EDCS-24502092.translation: Sacred to the Mother of the Gods, Great Idaean Augusta. For the well-being of the Emperors Caesars Gaius Valerius Diocletianus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, and Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, and their entire divine household, Quintus Minthonius Fortunatus, priest, having completed the sacred rites according to custom, including the sacrifice of rams and bulls, approved as pontiff by the decree of his colony of Mactaris, at his own expense, with Claudius Bonus, priest, and all the dendrophori and consecrated men and women, fulfilled his vow willingly and with a joyful heart.
EDCS-24502092.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to the Great Idaean Mother of the Gods, made by Quintus Minthonius Fortunatus, a priest from Mactaris, for the well-being of Emperors Diocletian and Maximian and their families, after performing sacred rites including criobolia and taurobolia, around 286-305 CE.

EDCS-24700303.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 22nd time, Armeniacus, Medicus, Parthicus Maximus, acclaimed emperor for the 5th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of his country, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva.
EDCS-24700303.summary: This is a monumental dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla), listing his full imperial titles and ancestry, likely dating to 219 CE based on the tribunician power count.

EDCS-24700305.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus, son of divine Antoninus, brother of divine Verus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of divine Hadrian, great-grandson of divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 31st time, acclaimed imperator 9 times, consul for the 3rd time, father of his country. [---] Fortunatus [---] sum of his multiplied honorary magistracy.
EDCS-24700305.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla), likely from the period of his 31st tribunician power (around 228-229 AD), and was made by a man named Fortunatus, possibly commemorating his public office.

EDCS-24700308.translation: [For the well-being(?)] of Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, acclaimed imperator 4 times, consul for the 2nd time, father of his country, the republic of the Elder Uchitani made and dedicated this at its own expense, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-24700308.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by the local community of the Elder Uchitani (res publica Uchitanorum Maiorum) to Emperor Commodus, likely dating to around 179 AD, for his welfare, funded by public money and decreed by the town council.

EDCS-24700309.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, acclaimed imperator 4 times, consul for the 2nd time, father of his country, the republic of the Elder Uchitani made and dedicated this at its own expense, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-24700309.summary: This inscription is a dedication from the republic of the Elder Uchitani (res publica Uchitanorum Maiorum) to Emperor Commodus, likely from around 179 AD, funded by public money and decreed by the town council.

EDCS-24700311.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Augustus, father of his country, pontifex maximus, son of divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of divine Commodus, grandson of divine Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of divine Nerva, holding tribunician power for the 5th time, acclaimed imperator 9 times, consul for the 2nd time, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-24700311.summary: This inscription is a public dedication to Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus, dating to around 197 AD (his 5th tribunician power), erected by decree of the decurions and paid for with public funds.

EDCS-24700312.translation: To Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the camps, wife of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Augustus, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-24700312.summary: This inscription is a public dedication to Julia Domna, wife of Emperor Septimius Severus and 'mother of the camps,' erected by decree of the decurions and paid for with public funds.

EDCS-24700314.translation: [To Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar, designated Emperor(?)] son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Augustus, father of his country, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-24700314.summary: This inscription is a public dedication to Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus), son of Emperor Septimius Severus, while he was designated Caesar, erected by decree of the decurions and paid for with public funds.

EDCS-24700319.translation: In the reign of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-24700319.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning the reign of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Claudius (Gothicus), indicating a date between 268 and 270 AD.

EDCS-24900150.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. [---] Licinius Germullus, a soldier of the First Urban Cohort, served for 19 years, lived for [---] years.
EDCS-24900150.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Licinius Germullus, a soldier of the Urban Cohort, recording his military service and age, likely set up by his family or comrades.

EDCS-24900289.translation: Asyllia Pola, daughter of Lucius, a physician, lies here. She lived for 65 years. Euscius, her freedman, made this at his own expense.
EDCS-24900289.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Asyllia Pola, a female physician, who lived to 65, and was dedicated by her freedman, Euscius.

EDCS-24900292.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Quintus Julius Ingenuus, a soldier of the Seventh Gemina Felix Legion. He lived for 43 years, served for 21 years. Flavius Marinus, his heir, set this up.
EDCS-24900292.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Julius Ingenuus, a soldier of Legio VII Gemina Felix, recording his age and years of service, dedicated by his heir, Flavius Marinus.

EDCS-24900293.translation: To Cornelius (?) Marcellus (?), a soldier of the Thirteenth Urban Cohort. Cornelius Crescens lived for 30 years. To his patron, who deserved well.
EDCS-24900293.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Cornelius Crescens to his patron, Cornelius (?) Marcellus (?), a soldier of the Thirteenth Urban Cohort, likely from the early Roman Empire.

EDCS-24900299.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Aurelius [---], a home-born slave and physician of the Augusti, lived for 34 years, 8 months, 9 days. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900299.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Aurelius, a home-born slave and physician to the emperors (Augusti), detailing his age and profession.

EDCS-24900300.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Romanus, an imperial surveyor of land, pious, lived for 50 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900300.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Romanus, an imperial land surveyor, who lived for 50 years, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24900306.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Felix, a slave of our Caesar, a devoted attendant, lived for 48 years. He is buried here. Aelia Melpomene made this for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-24900306.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Felix, a slave of the emperor and an attendant, erected by his wife Aelia Melpomene, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24900311.translation: Primus, a slave of Caesar, pious, lived for 3 years. He is buried here. Nicodicus, a slave and letter-carrier of Caesar, made this for his pious son.
EDCS-24900311.summary: This is a funerary inscription made by Nicodicus, an imperial slave and letter-carrier, for his pious son Primus, also an imperial slave, who died at the age of three.

EDCS-24900314.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Publius Aelius Optatus, a freedman of the emperor and a clerk, pious, lived for 30 years. He is buried here. His sister made this.
EDCS-24900314.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Optatus, an imperial freedman and clerk, erected by his sister, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24900316.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Cladus, a slave of the emperor, assistant to the clerk, pious, lived for 27 years, 4 months. He is buried here. His devoted wife Quietula made this.
EDCS-24900316.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cladus, an imperial slave and assistant clerk, erected by his devoted wife Quietula, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24900318.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Saturninus, a slave of our Caesar, assistant to the clerk, pious, lived for 40 years, 7 months, 5 days. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900318.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Saturninus, an imperial slave and assistant clerk, detailing his age at death, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24900325.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Agatho [---], a slave, pious, lived for 45 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900325.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Agatho, a pious slave, detailing his age at death, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24900326.translation: Agathopus, a deputy of Vitalis, an imperial slave, lived for 24 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900326.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Agathopus, who was a deputy to an imperial slave named Vitalis, detailing his age at death, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24900330.translation: Attia Data, pious, lived 1 year and 10 months. She lies here. Her devoted parents made this.
EDCS-24900330.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Attia Data, a young girl, erected by her grieving parents, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-24900331.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Attis, slave of the Emperor, lived 11 years [and X months/days?]. She lies here. Spendo [made this] for her sweetest fellow freedwoman.
EDCS-24900331.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Attis, an imperial slave who died at age eleven, erected by Spendo for her beloved fellow freedwoman, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-24900338.translation: Campanus, slave of Caesar, pious, lived 14 years. He lies here. Gargilia Veneria, his wife, made this for her devoted husband.
EDCS-24900338.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Campanus, an imperial slave, erected by his wife Gargilia Veneria, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-24900342.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Chrestus, most devoted slave of the Emperors, lived 5 years, 11 months, 14 days. He lies here.
EDCS-24900342.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Chrestus, a very young imperial slave, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD given the plural 'Augustorum'.

EDCS-24900346.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Claudia Eugenia lived 24 years, 6 months. Claudia Cratiste lived 15 years. They lie here.
EDCS-24900346.summary: This is a joint funerary inscription for Claudia Eugenia and Claudia Cratiste, likely two women or girls, dating from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-24900348.translation: [Sacred to the Div]ine Shades. To [---]fius, freedman of the Emperor. [---]fo Cocceia [made this].
EDCS-24900348.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription is dedicated to a freedman of the emperor, likely made by Cocceia, dating from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-24900353.translation: Crescens lived 2 years. He lies here.
EDCS-24900353.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Crescens, a child who died at age two, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-24900354.translation: Crito, slave of Caesar, pious, lived 40 years. He lies here. His devoted children made this.
EDCS-24900354.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Crito, an imperial slave, erected by his children, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-24900362.translation: Erucia Vitalis, dutiful, lived for 34 years; she is buried here. Vitalis, a slave of our Caesar, made this for his dutiful wife.
EDCS-24900362.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the life of Erucia Vitalis, made by her husband, Vitalis, a slave of the emperor.

EDCS-24900363.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Eutaxia, a dutiful slave of our Caesar, lived for 9 years and 4 months; she is buried here. May your bones rest well. May the earth be light for you.
EDCS-24900363.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Eutaxia, a young imperial slave, likely erected by her family or owner, requesting peaceful rest.

EDCS-24900365.translation: Publius Fabius Secundus, dutiful, lived for 75 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-24900365.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription marking the burial place of Publius Fabius Secundus.

EDCS-24900367.translation: Faustilla, a dutiful slave of our Caesar, lived for 60 years. Her daughter, Helena, lived for 30 years. Felix lived for 2 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-24900367.summary: This is a funerary inscription for three individuals, Faustilla, an imperial slave, her daughter Helena, and Felix, likely a son or another family member, indicating their ages at death and shared burial site.

EDCS-24900370.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Faustus, Saturninus, Secundus, brothers, dutiful slaves of our Caesar, lived for 10, 8, and 6 years. He is buried here. Saturninus and Ianuaria, their parents, made this for their most dutiful sons.
EDCS-24900370.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by parents, Saturninus and Ianuaria, to their three young sons, Faustus, Saturninus, and Secundus, who were imperial slaves and died at ages 10, 8, and 6.

EDCS-24900373.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Felicula, a dutiful slave of the emperor, lived for 25 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900373.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Felicula, an imperial slave, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-24900376.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Felix, dutiful, lived for 90 years and 5 months; he is buried here.
EDCS-24900376.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Felix, who lived to an old age of 90 years and 5 months.

EDCS-24900382.translation: Flavia Magnia Callippina, dutiful, lived for 2 years and 4 months; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900382.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Magnia Callippina, a young girl who died at the age of two years and four months.

EDCS-24900383.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Flavia Safsula lived for 22 years, 25 days, and 9 hours; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900383.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Safsula, noting her precise age at death down to the hour.

EDCS-24900384.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Flavia Tertia, a dutiful freedwoman of the emperor, lived for 80 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900384.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Tertia, an imperial freedwoman, who lived to the age of 80.

EDCS-24900386.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Fuficius Nobilis lived piously for 46 years, 7 months, and 9 days. Maxima made this for her most pious husband. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900386.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Fuficius Nobilis, erected by his wife Maxima, likely from the Imperial period given the formulaic language.

EDCS-24900388.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. [---]us lived piously for 40 years. Gemellus made this for his most pious brother. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900388.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated by Gemellus to his pious brother, who lived for 40 years, dating probably to the Imperial era.

EDCS-24900390.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Heraclida lived piously for 62 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-24900390.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Heraclida, recording her age at death as 62, typical of Imperial Roman burial practices.

EDCS-24900391.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Hilarus, slave of our Caesar, lived piously for 11 years. Also, his brother Elix, lived piously for 2 years. They are buried here. Their father Elix made this for his most pious sons.
EDCS-24900391.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by the father Elix to his two pious sons, Hilarus, a slave of Caesar, and his brother Elix, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-24900392.translation: [---] Hygia lived piously for 30 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-24900392.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Hygia, noting that she lived for 30 years, consistent with Imperial Roman funerary practices.

EDCS-24900394.translation: Januarius, of Augustus, lived for 5 years.
EDCS-24900394.summary: This is a brief funerary inscription for Januarius, who lived for five years, potentially indicating he was a slave or freedman associated with the imperial household during the Imperial period.

EDCS-24900396.translation: [---]us Inginus F[---] lived for [---] years [---] 5 days [---].
EDCS-24900396.summary: This is a very fragmentary funerary inscription, preserving parts of a name and the age at death, likely for an individual named Inginus, from the Roman Imperial era.

EDCS-24900400.translation: Julia Tertulla lived piously for 55 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900400.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julia Tertulla, who lived 55 years, possibly with a grammatical error in the final line (situs instead of sita), dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24900401.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Licinia Tertulla, daughter of Marcus, lived piously for 28 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-24900401.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Licinia Tertulla, daughter of Marcus, who lived for 28 years, typical of Imperial Roman funerary monuments.

EDCS-24900402.translation: Lucinus lived piously for 5 years, 2 months, and 10 days.
EDCS-24900402.summary: This is a concise funerary inscription for Lucinus, recording his age at death as five years, two months, and ten days, consistent with Roman Imperial practice for children.

EDCS-24900410.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Munatia Apsens, daughter of Lucius, lived piously for 15 years, 10 months, 24 days. She lies here.
EDCS-24900410.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Munatia Apsens, likely erected by her family, recording her age and place of burial.

EDCS-24900411.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Murrania Maia lived piously for 50 years. She lies here.
EDCS-24900411.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Murrania Maia, noting her age and final resting place.

EDCS-24900413.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Norbania Saturnina, pious. D. Born, as fame proves, in rich Rome, worthy of remembrance, I surpassed twice twelve years of light, when we counted a part of twice six from the number of the year, and ten with combined two times three days. Chaste and with modesty I preserved the times of my life. Buried now, I rest happily in Libyan soil. You too, passing by, who read this tomb, spare my ashes from being trodden by wanton feet. Thus may much light overflow for you into the ethereal breezes.
EDCS-24900413.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Norbania Saturnina, recording her age, Roman origin, and an epitaph urging passersby to respect her grave, possibly from the 1st or 2nd century AD given the style and location in North Africa.

EDCS-24900415.translation: Publius Octavius Metro[---], a fellow freedman, for the sake of piety, from [...].
EDCS-24900415.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or funerary monument, set up by Publius Octavius Metro[---] on behalf of a fellow freedman, or dedicated to one.

EDCS-24900417.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Optata Piata lived piously for 2 years, 3 months, 8 days. She lies here.
EDCS-24900417.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young girl named Optata Piata, detailing her very short lifespan and burial.

EDCS-24900423.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Phaenippus, a slave of the Emperor, lived piously for 33 years. He lies here.
EDCS-24900423.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Phaenippus, an imperial slave, recording his age and place of burial, likely erected by a family member or fellow slave.

EDCS-24900424.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Philo, a slave of the Emperor, lived piously for 33 years. Laetina, his sister, made this. He lies here.
EDCS-24900424.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Philo, an imperial slave, erected by his sister Laetina, stating his age and burial site.

EDCS-24900426.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Prepusa, a slave of Caesar, lived piously for 7 years, 3 months. She lies here.
EDCS-24900426.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Prepusa, a young slave of Caesar, indicating her age at death and burial place.

EDCS-24900429.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Prima lived piously for 1 year, 9 months, 14 days. She lies here.
EDCS-24900429.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Prima, a very young child, detailing her age at the time of her death and her burial location.

EDCS-24900434.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Romanus, a slave of our Caesar, lived piously for 15 years. He lies here.
EDCS-24900434.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Romanus, a young imperial slave, recording his age and place of burial.

EDCS-24900435.translation: Salvia, slave of Caesar, dutiful, lived for 30 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-24900435.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Salvia, a 30-year-old female slave of Caesar, indicating her burial place.

EDCS-24900436.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Salvia, dutiful slave of Augustus, lived for 38 years. She is buried here. Titus Flavius Salvius had this made out of piety.
EDCS-24900436.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Salvia, an Augustan slave, by Titus Flavius Salvius, presumably her husband or father, recording her age and place of burial.

EDCS-24900438.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Salvius, slave of our Caesar, lived for two years and eight months. He is buried here. Salvius, his father, made this for his deserving son.
EDCS-24900438.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Salvius, a young slave of Caesar, who died at age two years and eight months, erected by his father, also named Salvius.

EDCS-24900439.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Sannacrio, dutiful, lived for 7 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900439.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for a child named Sannacrio, who died at the age of seven.

EDCS-24900440.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Saturnina, dutiful slave of our Caesar, lived for 10 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-24900440.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Saturnina, a young female slave of Caesar, who died at the age of ten.

EDCS-24900441.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Saturnina, dutiful slave of our Caesar, lived for 23 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-24900441.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Saturnina, a female slave of Caesar, who died at the age of twenty-three.

EDCS-24900445.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Secundus Gallus, dutiful slave of Augustus, lived for 72 years. Satura, his dutiful wife, lived for 68 years. They are buried here.
EDCS-24900445.summary: This is a joint funerary inscription for Secundus Gallus, an Augustan slave, and his wife Satura, recording their ages and joint burial.

EDCS-24900447.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gaius Servilius Dorus, dutiful, lived for 70 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900447.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Servilius Dorus, recording his age of 70 years and his burial place.

EDCS-24900449.translation: Titus Sextius Extricatus lived for 2 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-24900449.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for a young child, Titus Sextius Extricatus, who died at the age of two.

EDCS-24900453.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Speratus, slave of Augustus, lived for 85 years. He is buried here. Claudia Extricata made this for her dutiful husband.
EDCS-24900453.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Speratus, an Augustan slave who lived to 85, erected by his wife Claudia Extricata in his memory.

EDCS-24900454.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. [...]nda, pious, lived for [...] years. Here she is buried. Sterceius made this for his pious wife.
EDCS-24900454.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of a pious woman named [...]nda, set up by her husband Sterceius, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-24900458.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Syria lived for 3 years. Nymphas made this for his pious daughter.
EDCS-24900458.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young girl named Syria, erected by her father Nymphas, probably dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-24900460.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gaius Terentius Pudens, pious, lived for 19 years. Here he is buried.
EDCS-24900460.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young man named Gaius Terentius Pudens, likely dating from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-24900461.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Tertulla, pious, lived for 40 years. Here she is buried.
EDCS-24900461.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman named Tertulla, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25000608.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Trachalus, pious, lived for 37 years. Here he is buried.
EDCS-25000608.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a man named Trachalus, likely dating from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-25000609.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Triumphalis, a slave of the emperor, lived for 25 years and 4 months. Here he is buried. May your bones rest well. May the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-25000609.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Triumphalis, an imperial slave, dating to the Imperial period, featuring common valedictory formulae.

EDCS-25000612.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Valeria Prepusa lived for 32 years. Here she is buried.
EDCS-25000612.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman named Valeria Prepusa, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-25000643.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. [...]enus, pious, lived for [...] years.
EDCS-25000643.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for a pious individual whose name ended in [...]enus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25000646.translation: Gaius Accius Tiro, son of Spurius, is buried here.
EDCS-25000646.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of Gaius Accius Tiro, the son of Spurius, likely dating from the Republican or early Imperial period due to the explicit filiation.

EDCS-25000647.translation: Aebutia Felicula lived for 16 years. Here she is buried. May your bones rest well. May the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-25000647.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young woman named Aebutia Felicula, dating to the Imperial period and including common valedictory phrases.

EDCS-25000648.translation: ]lia [3] / of Marcus Bennius / a substitute / lived for four years.
EDCS-25000648.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of a four-year-old girl named ]lia, identified as a 'vicaria' (substitute or deputy) of Marcus Bennius, likely dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-25000649.translation: Communis, the slave of Marcus Vibius Martialis, is buried here.
EDCS-25000649.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Communis, a slave of Marcus Vibius Martialis, indicating a grave marker for a dependent individual.

EDCS-25000650.translation: Helena, the handmaiden of Procula Bennia, lived for 22 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-25000650.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Helena, a 22-year-old slave (ancilla) of Procula Bennia, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25000651.translation: Manlia Rufilla, daughter of Decimus, is buried here.
EDCS-25000651.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription marking the burial place of Manlia Rufilla, the daughter of Decimus.

EDCS-25000652.translation: Masclus, of Bennius Athymus, is buried here.
EDCS-25000652.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Masclus, likely a dependent or relative of Bennius Athymus, marking his burial place.

EDCS-25000653.translation: ]elissa, of Hedulus, is buried here; she lived for [---] years.
EDCS-25000653.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman named ]elissa, likely associated with Hedulus, recording her burial and partial age.

EDCS-25000655.translation: Gnaeus Munatius Diogenes, freedman of a woman, lived for 70 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-25000655.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gnaeus Munatius Diogenes, a 70-year-old freedman of a woman (indicated by the inverted 'C'), marking his burial site.

EDCS-25000657.summary: This is an inscription identifying Tryphaenis as a freedman of Procla, likely a funerary or dedicatory fragment.

EDCS-25000660.translation: ]hysis [3], handmaiden of Marcus Vibius Martialis, lived for 45 years.
EDCS-25000660.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of a 45-year-old woman named ]hysis, identified as a handmaiden (ancilla) of Marcus Vibius Martialis.

EDCS-25000669.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Marcus Bruttius Felix, pious, lived for 60 years; he is buried here. Bruttia Secunda, daughter of Marcus, pious, lived for [---] years; she is buried here. Bruttia Quarta, daughter of Marcus, pious, lived for 18 years; she is buried here. Sallustia Quarta, daughter of Lucius, pious, lived for [---] years; she is buried here.
EDCS-25000669.summary: This is a large family funerary monument (likely a tombstone or an altar) dedicated to the Manes, commemorating Marcus Bruttius Felix (aged 60), his daughters Bruttia Secunda and Bruttia Quarta (aged 18), and Sallustia Quarta, all noted for their piety, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25000672.translation: Caecilius, [a dutiful person], lived [---] years. Candidus...
EDCS-25000672.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for a person named Caecilius, possibly dedicated by or also commemorating Candidus.

EDCS-25000673.translation: Quintus Caecilius Rufus, son of Quintus, a dutiful person, lived four years.
EDCS-25000673.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young boy named Quintus Caecilius Rufus.

EDCS-25000680.translation: To the Divine Manes. Gaius Clodius D[---] lived [---]. Gaius Cl[odius---].
EDCS-25000680.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead, mentioning two individuals named Gaius Clodius.

EDCS-25000681.summary: This is a very fragmentary funerary inscription for a woman named Clodia.

EDCS-25000689.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Decum[---].
EDCS-25000689.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription, likely dedicated to the spirits of the dead and mentioning a person named Decumus.

EDCS-25000697.translation: Felicula, slave of Publius Lurius Dexter, a dutiful woman, lived 25 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-25000697.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a 25-year-old female slave named Felicula, belonging to Publius Lurius Dexter.

EDCS-25000700.translation: Quintus Figilius Diodorus, freedman of Quintus. Publius Fufius Dama, freedman of a woman. Fufia Helena, freedwoman of Publius.
EDCS-25000700.summary: This inscription records the names of three freedpersons: Quintus Figilius Diodorus, Publius Fufius Dama, and Fufia Helena, along with their former owners.

EDCS-25000702.translation: Marcus Fulvius Rufus, a dutiful person, lived 24 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25000702.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Fulvius Rufus, who lived 24 years.

EDCS-25000716.translation: Iulius [---]araus, a dutiful person, lived [---] years, [---] months.
EDCS-25000716.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for an individual named Iulius [---]araus.

EDCS-25000725.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Longeia Flavia Laurentia, a holy virgin, lived 17 years, 4 months, 23 days.
EDCS-25000725.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Longeia Flavia Laurentia, a young woman described as a 'holy virgin', who died at the age of 17.

EDCS-25000731.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Malia Nic[...]
EDCS-25000731.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead, mentioning a woman named Malia Nic[...].

EDCS-25000744.translation: To the Divine Manes, sacred. Perellia Eulymena, dutiful, lived for 22 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-25000744.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Perellia Eulymena, who lived 22 years, marking her burial place.

EDCS-25000762.translation: To the Divine Manes. Sallustia Exornata [...].
EDCS-25000762.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes, mentioning Sallustia Exornata.

EDCS-25000784.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. [---]ius Venustus is buried here. He lived dutifully for 28 years.
EDCS-25000784.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a man named [---]ius Venustus, who lived for 28 years and is buried here.

EDCS-25000786.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Vibius Psellus, dutiful, lived more or less 28 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25000786.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Vibius Psellus, who died at approximately 28 years old and is interred at this site.

EDCS-25000788.translation: To the Divine Manes, sacred. Victor lived for 18 years.
EDCS-25000788.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely for a tombstone, dedicated to Victor who lived for 18 years, with some spelling irregularities.

EDCS-25000792.translation: To the Divine Manes, sacred. Volcacia Charis, dutiful, lived for 70 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-25000792.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of Volcacia Charis, who lived to the age of 70.

EDCS-25000981.text_edition: Sexto Caecilio Sexti filio Quirina Aemiliano Xviro stlitibus iudicandis VIIviro epulonum quaestori Augusti candidato legato pro praetore provinciae Africae tribuno plebis praetori patrono decreto decurionum pecunia publica
EDCS-25000981.translation: To Sextus Caecilius Aemilianus, son of Sextus, of the Quirina tribe, decemvir for judging lawsuits, septemvir of the Epulones, quaestor, candidate of Augustus, legate pro praetore of the province of Africa, tribune of the plebs, praetor, patron. By decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-25000981.summary: This is an honorific inscription dedicated by public funds and decree of the decurions to Sextus Caecilius Aemilianus, detailing his distinguished senatorial career.

EDCS-25001151.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Sextila Sabina, dutiful, lived for 35 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-25001151.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextila Sabina, indicating she lived for 35 years and is interred at this spot.

EDCS-25001152.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Titus Sextius Adiectus, dutiful, lived for 13 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001152.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Titus Sextius Adiectus, who lived to be 13 years old and is buried here.

EDCS-25001153.translation: Titus Sextius Felicio, dutiful, lived 70 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-25001153.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Sextius Felicio, erected by an unspecified dedicant, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001154.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits of the Dead. Titus Sextius Festus, dutiful, lived 33 years. He is laid here. Faustina, his mother, made this.
EDCS-25001154.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated to Titus Sextius Festus by his mother, Faustina, recording his age at death, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001155.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits of the Dead. Titus Sextius Hilariscus, dutiful, lived 30 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-25001155.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Sextius Hilariscus, with an unspecified dedicant, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001156.translation: Sperata, freedwoman of Sextia Donata, dutiful, lived 55 years. Sextia [---]A [---] are laid here. Speratus [---] for his sisters.
EDCS-25001156.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sperata, a freedwoman, and possibly other individuals including Sextia [---] and Speratus, who dedicates it to his sisters, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001161.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits of the Dead. Sura, female slave of the emperor, lived 40 years. She is laid here. Faustus made this for his wife.
EDCS-25001161.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated to Sura, an imperial slave, by her husband Faustus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001166.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits of the Dead. Valeria, dutiful female slave of the emperor, lived 25 years and three months. She is laid here.
EDCS-25001166.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valeria, an imperial slave, with an unspecified dedicant, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001170.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits of the Dead. Victor, dutiful, lived 8 years. Ianuaria, dutiful, lived 12 years.
EDCS-25001170.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating two children, Victor and Ianuaria, who died at young ages, with an unspecified dedicant, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001180.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits of the Dead. Urbana, daughter, lived 19 years. She is laid here.
EDCS-25001180.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Urbana, who died at the age of 19, likely erected by her family, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001186.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits of the Dead. Zosime, dutiful, lived 85 years. She is laid here.
EDCS-25001186.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Zosime, who lived to the age of 85, with an unspecified dedicant, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001205.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits of the Dead. Caecilia Felicitas, dutiful, lived 21 years. She is laid here. Viator, imperial slave, assistant of the record-keepers, made this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-25001205.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated to Caecilia Felicitas by her husband Viator, an imperial slave and assistant of the record-keepers, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001206.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Chrestus, imperial guard of the granaries of Utica Augusta, a dutiful man, lived for 80 years.
EDCS-25001206.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a monument to Chrestus, an imperial guard, who died at 80 years old, likely set up by his family or colleagues in the early Imperial period.

EDCS-25001218.translation: [---] Perelius [---] a dutiful man, lived for [---] years; he is buried here.
EDCS-25001218.summary: This fragmentary inscription is a funerary monument dedicated to Perelius, recording his dutiful life and burial place, but lacks details about the dedicant or his exact age.

EDCS-25001224.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Veneria, a dutiful woman, lived for 70 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-25001224.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Veneria, indicating she lived to 70 years old and was buried at this location, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001242.translation: Artoria Harmonia, a dutiful woman, lived for 2 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-25001242.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Artoria Harmonia, a young girl who died at the age of two, likely erected by her family.

EDCS-25001269.translation: Egrilia Romana, daughter of Aulus, a dutiful woman, lived for 20 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-25001269.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Egrilia Romana, daughter of Aulus, who died at the age of 20, likely erected by her family in the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001278.translation: Fulvia Donata, a dutiful woman, lived for [---]
EDCS-25001278.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Fulvia Donata, acknowledging her dutiful life but missing details about her age and burial.

EDCS-25001291.translation: Arsuleia Victrix, a dutiful woman, lived for 38 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-25001291.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Arsuleia Victrix, who lived 38 years, indicating her burial place, likely set up by her family in the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001298.translation: Iulia Quieta, a dutiful woman, lived for 35 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-25001298.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Iulia Quieta, who died at 35 years old and was buried at this location, likely erected by her family.

EDCS-25001306.translation: Modia Grata, freedwoman of Gaius, a dutiful woman, lived for 40 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-25001306.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Modia Grata, a freedwoman of Gaius, who lived for 40 years, marking her burial site, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001313.translation: Quintus Pompeius Naus, a dutiful man, lived for 18 years; he is buried here. Quintus Pompeius Quietus, a dutiful man, lived for 10 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-25001313.summary: This is a joint funerary inscription for two young men, Quintus Pompeius Naus (18) and Quintus Pompeius Quietus (10), likely brothers, erected by their family in the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001328.translation: Marcus Rutilius Liber, pious, lived sixty years.
EDCS-25001328.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Rutilius Liber, recording his name, piety, and age at death.

EDCS-25001351.translation: Valeria Secunda, daughter of Quintus, pious, lived 22 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-25001351.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valeria Secunda, noting her filiation, piety, age at death, and burial location.

EDCS-25001466.text_edition: Caio Memmio Cai filio Quirina Fido Iulio Albio consuli sodali Titio legato Augusti pro praetore provinciae Noricae curatori viae Flaminiae praefecto Miniciae proconsuli provinciae Baeticae legato Augusti legionis VII Claudiae iuridico per Italiam regionis Transpadanae praetori legato pro praetore provinciae Africae aedili Ceriali quaestori provinciae Asiae tribuno laticlavio legionis II Augustae Caius Annius Iulius Secundus [][] amico rarissimo ob eximiam eius erga se benivolentiam sua pecunia posuerunt et dedicaverunt decreto decurionum
EDCS-25001466.translation: To Gaius Memmius Fidus Julius Albius, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, consul, sodalis Titius, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power for the province of Noricum, curator of the Via Flaminia, prefect of Minicia, proconsul of the province of Baetica, legate of Augustus for the Seventh Legion Claudia, jurist throughout Italy for the Transpadane region, praetor, legate with propraetorian power for the province of Africa, aedile Cerialis, quaestor for the province of Asia, military tribune (laticlavius) of the Second Legion Augusta. Gaius Annius Julius Secundus [and others] dedicated this at their own expense and with the decree of the decurions, to a most rare friend, on account of his exceptional benevolence towards them.
EDCS-25001466.summary: This is an honorific inscription, erected by Gaius Annius Julius Secundus and others, commemorating the extensive public and military career of Gaius Memmius Fidus Julius Albius, likely a high-ranking official during the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001540.text_edition: Raniae Flaviae Iulianae Optatae clarissimae feminae matri Flaviorum Flaviani clarissimi pueri et Optatae clarissimae filiae et Flavianillae clarissimae puellae colonia Ammaedarensium sua pecunia fecit
EDCS-25001540.translation: The colony of Ammaedara, at its own expense, made this for Rania Flavia Iuliana Optata, a most illustrious woman, mother of Flavius Flavianus, a most illustrious boy, and Optata, a most illustrious daughter, and Flavianilla, a most illustrious girl.
EDCS-25001540.summary: This is an honorific inscription dedicated by the colony of Ammaedara to Rania Flavia Iuliana Optata, a noblewoman, and her illustrious children, acknowledging her family's status.

EDCS-25001592.translation: Gaius Julius Rogatus, a veteran, pious, lived 80 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001592.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Julius Rogatus, identifying him as a veteran and stating his age and piety.

EDCS-25001596.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Titus Flavius Laetus, freedman of Augustus, a pious clerk, lived 70 years. He is buried here.
