EDCS-25001596.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Flavius Laetus, a freedman of Augustus who served as a clerk, recording his profession, piety, and age at death.

EDCS-25001597.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Titus Flavius Tertius, freedman of Augustus, a pious clerk, lived 70 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001597.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Flavius Tertius, a freedman of Augustus who served as a clerk, recording his profession, piety, and age at death.

EDCS-25001599.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Donatus, freedman of Augustus, assistant to the clerks, pious, lived 50 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001599.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Donatus, a freedman of Augustus and an assistant clerk, noting his profession, piety, and age at death.

EDCS-25001600.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Phaenippus, his brother, made this for Eminens, assistant of Augustus, who lived 30 years, 9 months, and 6 days. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001600.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Phaenippus for his brother Eminens, an assistant of Augustus, specifying his precise age at death.

EDCS-25001601.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Eugramus, homeborn slave of the Emperors, assistant to the clerks, pious, lived 33 years and 5 months. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001601.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Eugramus, a homeborn slave of the Emperors and an assistant clerk, detailing his status, profession, piety, and age at death.

EDCS-25001602.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Pious Iulius Terminalis lived 81 years, 5 months, 4 days. Buried here. Terminalis, his grandson, had this done through Phaenippus, assistant of the imperial record-keepers of our Augustus.
EDCS-25001602.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Iulius Terminalis, set up by his grandson Terminalis, with assistance from Phaenippus, an imperial clerk.

EDCS-25001603.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Primus, imperial slave, assistant of the record-keepers, pious, lived 43 years. Victoria, his pious daughter, lived 8 years. Primigenia, his pious daughter, lived 3 years. Fortunata, imperial freedwoman, made this for her deserving husband and daughters. They are buried here.
EDCS-25001603.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Fortunata, an imperial freedwoman, for her husband Primus, an imperial clerk, and their two young daughters.

EDCS-25001604.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Priscus, slave of our Caesar, assistant of the record-keepers, lived 22 years, 3 months, 7 days. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001604.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Priscus, a young imperial slave who worked as an assistant record-keeper.

EDCS-25001605.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Quietus, imperial slave born in the household, assistant of the record-keepers, pious, lived 43 years, 7 months. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001605.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quietus, an imperial slave, born into the household and serving as an assistant record-keeper.

EDCS-25001606.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Salvius, imperial slave, assistant of the record-keepers, lived 40 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001606.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Salvius, an imperial slave who worked as an assistant record-keeper.

EDCS-25001610.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Africanus, slave of Caesar, assistant of the record-keepers of the military treasury, pious, lived 22 years. His father Africanus made this.
EDCS-25001610.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Africanus, a young imperial slave who served as a clerk in the military treasury, dedicated by his father, also named Africanus.

EDCS-25001615.translation: Attalus, slave of Caesar, scribe, pious, lived 19 years. His pious mother made this.
EDCS-25001615.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Attalus, a young imperial slave who worked as a scribe, dedicated by his mother.

EDCS-25001617.translation: Clarus, slave of our Caesar, scribe, lived 25 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001617.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Clarus, a relatively young imperial slave who worked as a scribe.

EDCS-25001621.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Alexander, slave of our Caesar, notary, pious, lived 40 years. He is buried here. Flavia Atalante made this for her husband from her own money.
EDCS-25001621.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Alexander, an imperial slave working as a notary, erected by his wife Flavia Atalante.

EDCS-25001626.translation: Crescens, imperial slave, formerly from the couriers, pious, lived 45 years. Felicita, his daughter, made this for her pious father. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001626.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Felicita to her father Crescens, an imperial slave who had previously been a courier.

EDCS-25001627.translation: Dionysius, a pious clerk, lived for 50 years. Dionysius, his son, made this out of piety.
EDCS-25001627.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording Dionysius, a clerk, and was erected by his son Dionysius out of filial piety.

EDCS-25001632.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Eusebes, a pious individual, lived for 4 years, 11 months, and 17 days. Hermeros, an Augustan clerk, made this for his subordinate.
EDCS-25001632.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Eusebes, a young individual, erected by Hermeros, an imperial clerk, for his deceased subordinate.

EDCS-25001634.translation: To Flavia Fortunata, a most devoted mother, her son made this. She lived for 60 years. Primigenius, an imperial letter carrier, made this at his own expense. She is buried here.
EDCS-25001634.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Primigenius, an imperial letter carrier, at his own expense to his most devoted mother, Flavia Fortunata.

EDCS-25001635.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Saturninus, an imperial letter carrier, a pious man, lived for 25 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001635.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Saturninus, an imperial letter carrier, who died at the age of 25 and is buried at this spot.

EDCS-25001637.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Publius Aelius Victor mesor agrorum pius vixit annis XXXXVIIII Quartio frater pius fecit hic situs est
EDCS-25001637.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Publius Aelius Victor, a pious land surveyor, lived for 49 years. His pious brother Quartio made this. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001637.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Victor, a land surveyor, erected by his brother Quartio.

EDCS-25001641.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Victor, a pious superintendent of the Emperor's beasts of burden, lived for 60 years. He is buried here.

EDCS-25001642.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Anteros, a pious slave of the Emperor, a footman, lived for 40 years. Flavia Tyche made this for her partner at her own expense. He is buried here.

EDCS-25001643.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Campanus, a pious slave of our Emperor, a footman, lived for 55 years. He is buried here. Flavia made this for her pious partner.

EDCS-25001644.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Chryseros, a slave of the Emperor, a footman, lived for 60 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001644.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Chryseros, a slave and footman of the Emperor, erected in his memory after he lived 60 years.

EDCS-25001649.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Fructus, a pious slave of our Caesar, a footman, lived for 31 years. He is buried here.

EDCS-25001655.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Synhetus, a temple keeper of the Emperor, lived for 95 years. He is buried here.

EDCS-25001778.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Iulia Sissonia, loyal, lived 35 years. She is laid here. Titus Flavius Romanus made this for his most loyal, most sacred, most indulgent wife. He also made this for Fundania Minor, his mother-in-law, who lived 90 years and is laid here.

EDCS-25001779.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Julia Ultrix, a dutiful woman, lived for 28 years. She lies here. Her foster-children made this in recognition of her merits.

EDCS-25001780.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Laches, a slave of our Caesar, a dutiful man, lived for 90 years. He lies here. Martialis, his son-in-law, made this for his deserving relative. May your bones rest well.

EDCS-25001781.translation: Laeta, a slave of Caesar, a dutiful woman, lived for 40 years. Dionysius made this for his dutiful wife. She lies here. May your bones rest well; may the earth be light upon you.

EDCS-25001783.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lebia, a slave of Augustus, a dutiful woman, lived for 26 years. She lies here.

EDCS-25001791.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Primigenius, an imperial slave, to his very young daughter Maxima, also an imperial slave, likely a small tombstone or altar from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001793.publication: CIL 08, 12792 (p 2459) = CLE 01187 = ILTun 00906 = CLEAfrique 00039 = CLEAfr-01, p 130 = AntAfr-1973-133
EDCS-25001793.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Minucia Prima, who lived for 26 years. Nicodromus, a slave of Augustus, made this for his dutiful and well-deserving wife. Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Minucia Prima, who lived for 26 years. Nicodromus, a slave of Augustus, made this for his dutiful and well-deserving wife. My dearest wife, you were snatched away in the prime of your life; twenty-six years was your allotted life. Rome is your origin, but fate decreed you to be Libyan. Now, wretched one, you are led to the Stygian ferry, and sad Lethe swirls in your breast, so that you, pitiable, do not recognize me, your devoted husband. It was Fortune's gift to preserve your modesty, and you could have given both of us to Italy. O good and simple one, you renewed the weeping of many when I no longer have you as a companion in my sight. She lies here. Prima.
EDCS-25001793.summary: This is a detailed funerary inscription, likely a tombstone or epitaph with a poetic eulogy, dedicated by Nicodromus, an imperial slave, to his wife Minucia Prima, revealing personal grief and biographical details typical of the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001794.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Natalis, a dutiful slave of Augustus, lived for 20 years. Heliodorus, a dutiful slave of Augustus, lived for 25 years. Their mother made this. He lies here.

EDCS-25001860.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ulpia Musa, commemorating her life and burial.

EDCS-25001861.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Ulpia Pacata lived piously for 31 years. She is laid here. Acceptus made this for his dear wife.
EDCS-25001861.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Acceptus for his beloved wife, Ulpia Pacata, recording her age and place of burial.

EDCS-25001862.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Ulpia Vitalis lived piously for 4 years, 3 months, 9 days. She is laid here.
EDCS-25001862.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ulpia Vitalis, a young child, detailing her brief life span.

EDCS-25001863.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Volussia Vitalica lived piously for 18 years, 4 months, 5 days. She is laid here.
EDCS-25001863.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Volussia Vitalica, recording her age at death and her burial place.

EDCS-25001864.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Urbica, freedwoman of the Emperor, lived piously for 30 years. She is laid here. Vitalis made this for his most pious wife.
EDCS-25001864.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Vitalis for his most pious wife, Urbica, a freedwoman of the emperor, stating her age and burial.

EDCS-25001865.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Uttedius Felix lived piously for 42 years, 3 months. Uttedia Alexandria made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-25001865.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Uttedia Alexandria to her deserving husband, Uttedius Felix, recording his age at death.

EDCS-25001878.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Iulius Felix, soldier of the First Legion Adiutrix, lived piously for 55 years, 10 months. He is laid here.
EDCS-25001878.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulius Felix, a soldier of Legio I Adiutrix, detailing his military affiliation and age at death.

EDCS-25001880.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Hypnus lived piously for 23 years. He is laid here. Asiaticus, imperial freedman, procurator of the Assuritana region, made this for his pious alumnus who deserved well of him.
EDCS-25001880.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Asiaticus, an imperial freedman and procurator, for his deserving alumnus, Hypnus, stating his age and burial.

EDCS-25001894.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Alexa, imperial slave, assistant to the record-keeper, lived piously for 21 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-25001894.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Alexa, an imperial slave and assistant record-keeper, recording his occupation and age at death.

EDCS-25001902.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Thoas, slave of the Emperor, a very devoted notary, lived for 46 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001902.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Thoas, an imperial slave and notary, erected posthumously by an unknown dedicator, likely in the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001904.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Succesa, devoted slave of Caesar, lived for 36 years. Fortunatus, a herald, made this for his good wife. She is buried here.
EDCS-25001904.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Fortunatus, a herald, to his good wife, Succesa, an imperial slave, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001905.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Campester, slave of the Emperor, an instructor of couriers, devoted, lived for 30 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001905.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Campester, an imperial slave who served as an instructor of couriers, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001922.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Gessia Luscina, devoted, lived for 30 years. She is buried here. Tiberius Claudius Matrillus, a doctor, made this for his devoted wife.
EDCS-25001922.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gessia Luscina, erected by her husband, Tiberius Claudius Matrillus, a doctor, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001930.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Restitutus, devoted, lived for 3 years and 6 months. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001930.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelius Restitutus, a young child, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001933.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Viator, devoted, lived for 36 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001933.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelius Viator, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001942.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Felicula, devoted, lived for 80 years. Felix and Caletyche, her children, made this for their devoted mother. She is buried here.
EDCS-25001942.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelia Felicula, erected by her children, Felix and Caletyche, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001950.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Annius Dicaeus lived for 50 years. Publius Annius Natalis, his freedman, made this for his well-deserving patron.
EDCS-25001950.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Publius Annius Natalis, a freedman, to his well-deserving patron, Publius Annius Dicaeus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001951.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. To Antonus, devoted slave of the Emperor, who lived for 17 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001951.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Antonus, an imperial slave who died young, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001952.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Antonia Haline, devoted, lived for 72 years and 10 months. Publius Aelius Euhodus, freedman of the Emperor, made this for his well-deserving wife, with whom he lived for 40 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-25001952.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Antonia Haline, erected by her husband, Publius Aelius Euhodus, an imperial freedman, commemorating their 40 years of marriage, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25001954.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Arbuscula, our Caesar's dutiful (servant), lived for 53 years. She is laid to rest here.
EDCS-25001954.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Arbuscula, a dutiful servant of the emperor, who died at the age of 53 and was buried here.

EDCS-25001959.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Atimetianus, dutiful, lived for 3 years and 6 months. He is laid to rest here.
EDCS-25001959.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Atimetianus, a young boy who died at the age of 3 years and 6 months and was buried here.

EDCS-25001972.translation: Gaius Caecilius Restutus, dutiful, lived for 19 years. He is laid to rest here.
EDCS-25001972.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Caecilius Restutus, who died at the age of 19 and was buried here.

EDCS-25001974.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Cale, our Caesar's dutiful female slave, lived for 40 years. She is laid to rest here.
EDCS-25001974.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cale, a dutiful female slave of the emperor, who died at the age of 40 and was buried here.

EDCS-25001977.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lucius Cassius Africanus, son of Lucius, dutiful, lived for 30 years. He is laid to rest here.
EDCS-25001977.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Cassius Africanus, who died at the age of 30 and was buried here.

EDCS-25001982.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Clarus, a dutiful slave of Augustus, lived for 40 years. Ianuarius, his son, made this for his most dutiful father. He is laid to rest here.
EDCS-25001982.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Ianuarius to his most dutiful father, Clarus, a slave of the emperor, who died at the age of 40.

EDCS-25001985.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Claudia Nape, dutiful, lived for 86 years. She is laid to rest here.
EDCS-25001985.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Claudia Nape, who died at the venerable age of 86 and was buried here.

EDCS-25001995.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Comisator, our Caesar's dutiful slave, lived for 25 years. He is laid to rest here.
EDCS-25001995.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Comisator, a dutiful slave of the emperor, who died at the age of 25 and was buried here.

EDCS-25002002.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lucius Cusp[---], dutiful, lived for one year and 10 months. He is laid to rest here.
EDCS-25002002.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young Lucius Cusp[---], who died at the age of one year and ten months and was buried here.

EDCS-25002004.translation: To the Divine Shades. Danae lived for 1 year, 6 months, and 20 days. She is laid to rest here.
EDCS-25002004.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Danae, a child who died at the very young age of 1 year, 6 months, and 20 days, and was buried here.

EDCS-25002006.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Dellia Flaccilla, devout, lived for 20 years and 8 months. She lies here.
EDCS-25002006.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the burial of Dellia Flaccilla, dedicated by an unknown individual.

EDCS-25002016.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Eutycha, devout, lived for 15 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25002016.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Eutycha, dedicated by an unknown individual.

EDCS-25002017.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Extricatus, a devout slave of the Emperor, lived for 81 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25002017.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Extricatus, a remarkably long-lived imperial slave, set up by an unknown individual.

EDCS-25002019.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Fabia Iucunda, devout, lived for 34 years, 10 months, and 1 day. Fabius Felix, devout brother, lived for 33 years. They lie here.
EDCS-25002019.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Fabia Iucunda and her brother Fabius Felix, erected by an unknown dedicant.

EDCS-25002024.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Felicia, devout, lived for 50 years. And Badaeus, devout, lived for 60 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25002024.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Felicia and Badaeus, dedicated by an unknown individual.

EDCS-25002032.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Felix, devout slave of the Emperor, lived for 40 years and 3 months. He lies here.
EDCS-25002032.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Felix, an imperial slave, erected by an unknown dedicant.

EDCS-25002033.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Felix, devout slave of our Caesar, lived for 2 years and 20 days. He lies here. Felix, his father, made this for his deserving son.
EDCS-25002033.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Felix, a young imperial slave, dedicated by his father, also named Felix.

EDCS-25002034.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Titus Flavius Atimetus, devout, lived for 78 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25002034.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Flavius Atimetus, dedicated by an unknown individual.

EDCS-25002043.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Flavia Fusca, devout, lived for 40 years. She lies here. Flavius Onesimus made this for his most devout wife and for himself.
EDCS-25002043.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Fusca, dedicated by her husband Flavius Onesimus, who also prepared the monument for his own future burial.

EDCS-25002046.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Fortunatus, a Corinthian imperial slave, devout, lived for 13 years. He lies here. May your bones rest well; may the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-25002046.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Fortunatus, a young imperial slave from Corinth, featuring a common sepulchral blessing.

EDCS-25002050.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Fortunata, freedwoman of Augustus...
EDCS-25002050.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Fortunata, a freedwoman of the emperor, dedicated to the Divine Shades, though the full text is not preserved.

EDCS-25002051.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Furfuleia Tertulla, dutiful, lived 27 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-25002051.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Furfuleia Tertulla, a dutiful woman who died at the age of 27.

EDCS-25002052.translation: To the Divine Shades... Furnia V... lived years...
EDCS-25002052.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Shades for a woman named Furnia V..., whose age at death is partially preserved.

EDCS-25002053.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Galla, freedwoman of Augustus, dutiful, lived 7 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-25002053.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Galla, a dutiful freedwoman of the emperor, who died at the age of 7.

EDCS-25002054.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Getulicus, a home-born slave of Augustus, dutiful, lived 4 years, 8 months, 4 days. He is buried here.
EDCS-25002054.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Getulicus, a dutiful home-born slave of the emperor, who died in early childhood.

EDCS-25002056.translation: To the Divine Shades. Helpis, dutiful, lived five years, three months. She is buried here.
EDCS-25002056.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Helpis, a dutiful individual who died at the age of five years and three months.

EDCS-25002066.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Ismarus, dutiful, lived 25 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25002066.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ismarus, a dutiful man who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-25002067.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Iucundus, slave of our Caesar, dutiful, lived 8 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25002067.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iucundus, a dutiful slave of the emperor (Caesar), who died at the age of 8.

EDCS-25002069.translation: Tiberius Iulius Rufinus, dutiful, lived 27 years, 3 months. He is buried here.
EDCS-25002069.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Tiberius Iulius Rufinus, a dutiful man who died at the age of 27 years and 3 months.

EDCS-25002076.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Iulia Quesita, dutiful, lived 55 years, 11 months, 16 days.
EDCS-25002076.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Quesita, a dutiful woman who died at the age of 55 years, 11 months, and 16 days.

EDCS-25002077.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Julia Tertulla, dutiful, lived for 36 years, 2 months. She lies here.
EDCS-25002077.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julia Tertulla, erected likely by her family, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25002079.translation: Lucius Junius Vitalis lived for 90 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25002079.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription commemorating Lucius Junius Vitalis, likely placed by his family or associates.

EDCS-25002084.translation: Martialis, dutiful, lived for 5 years, 4 months, 12 days. He lies here.
EDCS-25002084.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young boy named Martialis, probably set up by his grieving parents.

EDCS-25002086.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Matila, dutiful, lived for 24 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25002086.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Matila, likely erected by his family, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25002093.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Octavia Lochias lived for 65 years. She lies here. Philomusus, a slave of Augustus and an Augustan, made this for his dutiful mother-in-law.
EDCS-25002093.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Octavia Lochias, erected by her son-in-law, Philomusus, a slave of the Emperor, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25002096.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Philumenus, dutiful, lived for 11 months, 11 days. He lies here.
EDCS-25002096.summary: This is a funerary inscription for the infant Philumenus, likely placed by his parents or guardians.

EDCS-25002098.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Poletice, dutiful, lived for 60 years. She lies here.
EDCS-25002098.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Poletice, likely erected by her family, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25002104.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Primus, a slave of our Caesar, dutiful, lived for 80 years. He lies here. Primigenia, his dutiful fellow-slave, made this for the deserving.
EDCS-25002104.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Primus, a slave of the Emperor, erected by his fellow-slave Primigenia, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25002107.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Primula, a slave of Augustus, dutiful, lived for 9 years. She lies here.
EDCS-25002107.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young imperial slave named Primula, likely erected by her family or fellow slaves, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25002111.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Privata, a slave of Augustus, dutiful, lived for 17 years. She lies here.
EDCS-25002111.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young imperial slave named Privata, likely erected by her family or fellow slaves, dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25002114.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Lucius Pullaienus Proculus, a dutiful man, lived for 65 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-25002114.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Pullaienus Proculus, erected by an unspecified dedicator, likely in the Imperial period.

EDCS-25002115.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Rabiria Spes, a dutiful woman, lived for 37 years. She is laid here. Nicolaus, a freedman of Augustus, made this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-25002115.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Nicolaus, a freedman of Augustus, to his beloved wife Rabiria Spes, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-25002129.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Secunda, a freedwoman of Augustus, a dutiful woman, lived for 2 years. She is laid here.
EDCS-25002129.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Secunda, a freedwoman of Augustus, recording her early death, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25002130.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. [---] Caesaris Augusti, a dutiful woman, lived for 25 years. Securus made this for his wife.
EDCS-25002130.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Securus to his wife, a freedwoman of Caesar Augustus, who died at the age of 25, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-25002132.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Gaius Selicius Victor, a dutiful man, lived for [---]
EDCS-25002132.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Gaius Selicius Victor, recording his name and dutiful nature, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25002133.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Senila, a dutiful woman, lived for one year and eight months. She is laid here.
EDCS-25002133.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Senila, a young girl who died at the age of one year and eight months, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25002134.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. You, whoever you are, pious or unkind, reading this inscription, weep for my age, for I ought not to have left such light or such gods. Why do you say this? For I was obedient to my dear husband, whose character I always timidly guarded, and his chastity and affection. For he deserved it from me, who loved me so chastely. I lived simply, and in his honor, no one could boast or envy me among their husbands. Whoever you are, holy woman, if you can live as chastely, I know you can be dearly loved if you live according to my custom of chastity. For after my death, I leave a memory of many years to my husband. But I give thanks to the gods that, while I lived, he denied nothing to my pleasures, because I myself deserved it. Severa, a slave of Augustus, a dutiful woman, lived for 24 years, 6 months, 11 days. She is laid here. Her husband made this for the deserving woman.
EDCS-25002134.summary: This is an elaborate funerary inscription with a first-person address from Severa, a dutiful slave of Augustus, to her husband, who dedicated the monument to her after her death at age 24, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25002176.text_edition: ano consuli clarissimo viro curatori aquarum et Miniciae et alvei Tiberis praefecto alimentorum XVviro sacris faciundis XIIviro urbis Romae legato Numidiae praetori urbano candidato quaestori allecto in patricias familias triumviro aere argento auro flando feriundo seviro turmae eeqquitum RRomanorum patrono perpetuo Aelius Felix et Fabius Donatulus
EDCS-25002176.translation: To [---] Anus, the most distinguished man, curator of the waters and of the Minicia and the channel of the Tiber, prefect of the alimenta, quindecimvir for performing sacred rites, duodecimvir of the city of Rome, legate of Numidia, urban praetor, elected quaestor, enrolled in the patrician families, triumvir for minting bronze, silver, and gold, sevir of the squadron of Roman equites, perpetual patron. Aelius Felix and Fabius Donatulus.
EDCS-25002176.summary: This is an honorific inscription dedicated by Aelius Felix and Fabius Donatulus to a distinguished senator, Anus, listing his extensive public career and numerous magistracies, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25100291.text_edition: Tesseram pagi Minervi Marcus Grattius Marci filius Papiria magister pagi de sua pecunia dedit
EDCS-25100291.translation: Marcus Grattius, son of Marcus, of the Papirian tribe, master of the 'pagus Minervi', gave this tessera for the 'pagus Minervi' from his own money.
EDCS-25100291.summary: This inscription records that Marcus Grattius, a master of the 'pagus Minervi', donated a tessera (tablet/token) to the 'pagus' from his own funds, likely from the Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-25100292.translation: The roofs of Bassianus' estate, known as Baiae, gleam and shine even more brightly with the splendor of new light. He arranged to build [---] opposite [---] in the name of Sidonius [---] under August [---] // Sidonius
EDCS-25100292.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely an architectural or dedicatory text, celebrating the splendor of Bassianus' estate, possibly built or enhanced by Sidonius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-25200086.translation: Lucius Mutius Faustinus, son of Lucius, of the Arnensian tribe, dutiful, lived 74 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25200086.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Mutius Faustinus, likely a private individual, who lived to 74 years of age.

EDCS-25200095.translation: Of Hadrian Augustus, lord… in the consulship of Camerinus and Niger.
EDCS-25200095.summary: This fragment appears to be part of an official dedication or record, dated to the consulship of Camerinus and Niger, indicating a date of 195 CE during the reign of Hadrian.

EDCS-25200096.translation: Of Imperator Antoninus Augustus… number 751, workshop… in the consulship of Imperator Antoninus and… under the care of Agathus.
EDCS-25200096.summary: This inscription is a fragment, likely from a building or dedication, mentioning Emperor Antoninus and a specific workshop, possibly dated to the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-25200097.translation: In the consulship of Orfitus and Priscus, from the account of Callistus, 61… Certus.
EDCS-25200097.summary: This fragment records an administrative or financial transaction from the consulship of Orfitus and Priscus (possibly 149 CE or 178 CE), involving an account managed by Callistus.

EDCS-25200114.translation: Sextus Veturius, veteran of the Ala Siliana, lived 70 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25200114.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Veturius, a veteran of a cavalry unit called the Ala Siliana, who lived to 70 years.

EDCS-25200115.translation: Cornelius Vitalis, priest of Jupiter, aedile, quaestor, lived 31 years. May the earth be light to you.
EDCS-25200115.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cornelius Vitalis, a priest of Jupiter and holder of municipal offices (aedile and quaestor), who died at the age of 31, with a common funerary wish.

EDCS-25200116.translation: Veturia Martha, daughter of Sextus, priestess of Caelesta, lies here, lived 95 years.
EDCS-25200116.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Veturia Martha, a priestess of Caelesta, who lived to an advanced age of 95.

EDCS-25200121.translation: Marcus Aemilius Primus, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, dutiful, lived [?] years. He lies here.
EDCS-25200121.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Aemilius Primus, a private individual, whose age at death is partly lost.

EDCS-25200122.translation: Gaius Aurelius Memor, son of Gaius (?), son of Aurelius Medicus (?), lived 7 years. Two brothers are buried here.
EDCS-25200122.summary: This is a funerary inscription for two young brothers, one named Gaius Aurelius Memor, who died at the age of 7.

EDCS-25200125.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, likely a funerary monument or dedication, mentioning Quintus Caecilius and his father.

EDCS-25200126.translation: Caninia Sura, pious, lived for [---] years.
EDCS-25200126.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caninia Sura, noting her piety and age, though the exact age and end of the text are missing.

EDCS-25200128.translation: Gaius Cornelius Felix, son of Sextus, of the Quirina tribe, pious, lived for 50 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25200128.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Cornelius Felix, noting his parentage, tribe, piety, age at death, and burial location.

EDCS-25200131.translation: Egnatia Helena, daughter of Marcus, pious, lived for 45 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-25200131.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Egnatia Helena, recording her parentage, piety, age, and burial location.

EDCS-25200134.translation: Gorgonia Fortunata, pious, lived for 80 years. Gaius Aelius Saturninus, pious, lived for [---] years.
EDCS-25200134.summary: This is a double funerary inscription, likely for two individuals, Gorgonia Fortunata and Gaius Aelius Saturninus, recording their piety and ages, though Saturninus's age is incomplete.

EDCS-25200136.translation: Gaius Iulius Felix, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, pious, lived for 9 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25200136.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Iulius Felix, a young boy, detailing his parentage, tribe, piety, age, and burial location.

EDCS-25200140.translation: Iulia Saturnina, wife of Daphnus, freedman of Tiberius Claudius Hilario, lived for 60 years. She is buried here. May your bones rest well; may the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-25200140.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Saturnina, identifying her as the wife of a freedman, stating her age at death, burial location, and including a common funerary wish.

EDCS-25200144.translation: Gaius Petronius Acceptus, pious, lived for 43 years.
EDCS-25200144.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Petronius Acceptus, noting his piety and age at death.

EDCS-25200145.translation: Quarta, daughter of Vetius Ianuarius, lived for 22 years.
EDCS-25200145.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quarta, identifying her parentage and age at death.

EDCS-25200147.translation: Rustica, daughter of Polio, from Iscilis, lived for 60 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-25200147.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Rustica, recording her parentage, place of origin (Iscilis), age, and burial location.

EDCS-25200151.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, with only a few letters preserved, indicating a name starting with 'Servi' and some other text.

EDCS-25200152.translation: To Servilia Venusta, daughter of Servilius Saturninus. She lived for 50 years. She lies here.
EDCS-25200152.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Servilia Venusta, dedicated by her father, Servilius Saturninus, marking her burial place.

EDCS-25200155.translation: Sextus Veturius Extricatus lived for 53 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25200155.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name, age, and burial place of Sextus Veturius Extricatus.

EDCS-25200157.translation: Lucius Vitupius, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, Severus the pious, lived for 67 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25200157.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Vitupius Severus, noting his lineage, tribal affiliation, piety, and age at death.

EDCS-25200165.translation: Mundicia Processa, daughter of Quintus, the pious, lived for 52 years. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-25200165.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Mundicia Processa, mentioning her parentage, piety, and age, concluding with a common funerary wish.

EDCS-25200167.translation: Cornelia Tyrannis, the pious, lived for 14 years.
EDCS-25200167.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Cornelia Tyrannis, indicating her piety and young age at death.

EDCS-25200169.translation: Gaius Octavius Festus, the pious, lived for 18 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25200169.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Octavius Festus, noting his piety, age, and burial place.

EDCS-25200173.translation: Sacred to Mars Augustus. Quintus Furfanius Martialis, son of Quintus, of the Lemonia tribe, took care to have this statue made out of his own money, in accordance with a decree of the decurions, for the honors of his duovirate and Augustan flaminate, brought to the public treasury, in addition to the sum paid for the honor of the quinquennality, and furthermore, games and a double banquet and 10,000 modii of wheat, when it was 10 denarii per modius, from the 2 denarii given to the people in his name by his father Bellicus; likewise, twice the sportulae to the order.
EDCS-25200173.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Mars Augustus by Quintus Furfanius Martialis, who funded a statue and other public benefactions, including games, banquets, and wheat distributions, in return for his civic offices as duovir and Augustan flamen.

EDCS-25200729.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Octavius Sabb[---] [---]timi[---]
EDCS-25200729.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to the Manes, naming Lucius Octavius Sabb[---].

EDCS-25300087.inscription_type: carmina, tituli fabricationis, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-25300087.translation: [---] many used always [---] in the citizens, he happily led, and the majesty of the invincible divine god Mars, made by hand, constitutes the divine power; the native saw a deed similar to himself, with his own face, reformed for the better, it was the exact image of the statue; since the face is more venerable and all the statues thus, religion itself demonstrates such great works, all of which made his camps shine perpetually. Cilonius, the maker, whose love still grows into the number of the great, because this pious man provides him to have a joyful life and a better reputation at the same time; he shows the truth and gladly made a vow in his heart, for he gave a gymnasium and wine to the Martenses, thus not undeservedly, because he paid to offer a gift to the Deity, as he certainly commanded, and to complete the statue with his own money. Thus he published [---]. To Mars Invictus, Cilonius. To Mars Invictus, Cilonius.
EDCS-25300087.summary: This lengthy and highly fragmentary inscription, twice identifying Cilonius and Mars Invictus, appears to be a dedication by Cilonius celebrating the creation of a statue of Mars and various benefactions (a gymnasium, wine) to the Martenses, possibly in gratitude for military success or civic honor.

EDCS-25300092.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Parthicus Arabicus Adiabenicus, invested with tribunician power for the 4th time, emperor for the 8th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul. [---] Alfius Secundus, perpetual flamen, set up two(?) equestrian statues out of 12,000 sesterces, which Lucius Alfius Felix, his father, had promised from his own generosity by doubling the honorary sum for the perpetual flaminate, with the permission of the order.
EDCS-25300092.summary: This inscription records the dedication of two equestrian statues to Emperor Septimius Severus by Alfius Secundus, a perpetual flamen, funded by his father, Lucius Alfius Felix, as a public benefaction related to his perpetual flaminate.

EDCS-25300094.translation: To Tiberius Aprarius Felix, son of Quintus, of the Papirian tribe, an aedilician, perpetual flamen, a good man, a lover and foster-child of his municipality, the people, by collected money, erected this for his incomparable presentation of missiles in honor of his aedileship, and for the great construction of a Septizodium out of his pure generosity. The site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-25300094.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by the people of a municipality to Tiberius Aprarius Felix, recognizing his generosity in public works and spectacles during his aedileship and priesthood.

EDCS-25300332.translation: To Augusta, sacred. Quintus Furfanius Martialis, son of Quintus, of the Lemonian tribe, paid for this from his own money for his honors as duovir and Augustan flamen, the statues being made by decree of the decurions, in addition to the sum counted for the glory of his quinquennialship and further for games and a double banquet and ten thousand modii of wheat, when it was ten denarii per modius, from the two denarii given to the people in his name by Bellicus, his father, and also twice the sportulae to the order.
EDCS-25300332.summary: This inscription records Quintus Furfanius Martialis's funding of statues and public benefactions for the community, including games, banquets, and wheat distributions, in recognition of his magistracies and priesthood.

EDCS-25300350.translation: Her husband and spouse willingly and gladly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-25300350.summary: This inscription records a vow made and fulfilled by a husband and wife, possibly on behalf of a named woman.

EDCS-25300419.text_edition: Quinto Caecilio Quinti filio Arnensi Marcello Dentiliano Xviro stlitibus iudicandis tribuno militum legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis quaestori provinciae Africae aedili curuli candidato divi Hadriani praetori candidato eiusdem legato provinciae Cretae Cyrenarum legato provinciae Hispaniae proconsuli provinciae Cretae Cyrenarum legato legionis XII fulminatae legato Augusti pro praetore provinciae Galliae Aquitanicae coloniae patrono Thibiucenses aere conlato
EDCS-25300419.translation: To Quintus Caecilius Marcellus Dentilianus, son of Quintus, of the Arnensian tribe, decemvir for judging lawsuits, military tribune of the Eleventh Legion Claudia Pia Fidelis, quaestor of the province of Africa, curule aedile, candidate of the deified Hadrian, praetor, candidate of the same Hadrian, legate of the province of Crete and Cyrenaica, legate of the province of Hispania, proconsul of the province of Crete and Cyrenaica, legate of the Twelfth Legion Fulminata, Augustan legate pro praetore of the province of Gallia Aquitanica, patron of the colony, the people of Thibiucenses erected this by collected money.
EDCS-25300419.summary: This inscription, erected by the citizens of Thibiuca, commemorates the distinguished public career of Quintus Caecilius Marcellus Dentilianus, listing his numerous political and military offices.

EDCS-25401760.translation: To Aurelius Antoninus, son of Augustus, of both orders.
EDCS-25401760.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Aurelius Antoninus, identified as the son of an emperor and belonging to both equestrian and senatorial orders, likely referring to Caracalla as heir apparent.

EDCS-25501680.translation: By the authority of Emperor Vespasian Caesar Augustus, Father of the Fatherland, the boundaries of the new and old province were delimited where the royal ditch was, by Rutilius Gallicus, consul.
EDCS-25501680.summary: This inscription records the re-demarcation of provincial boundaries under the authority of Emperor Vespasian by the consul Rutilius Gallicus, likely related to land administration or expansion.

EDCS-25501733.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Antonius Rufus Honorati filius Tromentina Veiis Arnensis Thignicae genius veritatis pius vixit annis hic situs est ossa tibi bene quiescant terra tibi levis sit  Οἵη περ φύλλων γενεή, τοίη δὲ καὶ ἀνδρῶν
EDCS-25501733.translation: Sacred to the Manes Gods. Marcus Antonius Rufus, son of Honoratus, of the Tromentina tribe from Veii, Arnensis from Thignica, a genius of truth, pious, lived for years. He lies here. May your bones rest well, may the earth be light upon you. "As is the generation of leaves, so is that of men."
EDCS-25501733.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Antonius Rufus, acknowledging his piety and character, including a Greek poetic quote about the transience of life.

EDCS-25600116.translation: Sacred to the Manes Gods. Gaius Bruttius Exoratus, a veteran of the Third Augustan Legion, lived 90 years. Helvia, his wife, made this. He lies here.
EDCS-25600116.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Bruttius Exoratus, a veteran of the Legio III Augusta, erected by his wife Helvia after he lived to the age of 90.

EDCS-25600117.translation: Lucius Caelius Legitimus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, in the century of Fundanius Severus, lived 40 years, served 17 years. He lies here. Julia Rustica, his wife, erected this.
EDCS-25600117.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Caelius Legitimus, a soldier of the Legio III Augusta, erected by his wife Julia Rustica, noting his age and years of service.

EDCS-25600118.translation: Tiberius Claudius Manduccus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, lived for [---] years.
EDCS-25600118.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Tiberius Claudius Manduccus, a soldier of the Legio III Augusta.

EDCS-25600119.translation: Publius Durmius Firmus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, lived for 22 years and served for 4 years. Publius Durmius Firmus the veteran, his father, established this monument for his son.
EDCS-25600119.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of a young soldier from the Legio III Augusta, erected by his father, a veteran of the same name, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-25600720.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Augusti filius Augustus tribunicia potestate XVI Asprenas consul proconsul VII vir epulonum viam ex castris hibernis Tacapes muniendam curavit legio III Augusta CI
EDCS-25600720.translation: Imperator Caesar Augustus, son of Augustus, holding tribunician power for the sixteenth time, consul, proconsul, septemvir of the Epulones, in the seventh year of his proconsulship (?), ordered the road from the winter camp of Tacapes to be fortified. The Third Augustan Legion (built this).
EDCS-25600720.summary: This inscription records the construction or fortification of a road from a military camp at Tacapes by the Legio III Augusta, ordered by Emperor Augustus, likely dating to 12-11 BC based on his tribunician power count.

EDCS-25600771.translation: Eros, everything through you. Amandus, Frunitus.
EDCS-25600771.summary: This brief inscription, likely a dedication, expresses gratitude or devotion to Eros by Amandus and Frunitus, possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-25600853.translation: To the memory of Tiberius Claudius Iulianus, all the curiales of the municipality of Cincara, incited by the generosity of his mother, contributed from their own funds to establish this bronze statue. The place was given by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-25600853.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the curiales of the municipality of Cincara to Tiberius Claudius Iulianus, funding a statue with their own money, possibly dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-25600918.translation: From the Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius. Number 606. From the Regia workshop. Orfitus and Priscus being consuls. FC.
EDCS-25600918.summary: This is a brick stamp or similar inscription, indicating its origin from the Regia workshop and dating it to the consulship of Orfitus and Priscus, which corresponds to 149 AD, during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-25600919.translation: From the Emperor Antoninus Augustus, our lord. Number 343. From the Regia workshop. Gallicanus and Veter(e) being consuls. PEC.
EDCS-25600919.summary: This is a brick stamp or similar inscription, indicating its origin from the Regia workshop and dating it to the consulship of Gallicanus and Vetere, which corresponds to 150 AD, during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-25600920.translation: From the Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius, our lord. Number [undetermined]. From the Agrippa workshop. Gallicanus and Vetere being consuls. PEC.
EDCS-25600920.summary: This is a brick stamp or similar inscription, indicating its origin from the Agrippa workshop and dating it to the consulship of Gallicanus and Vetere, which corresponds to 150 AD, during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-25600921.translation: From the Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius, our lord. Number 517. From the Agrippa workshop. Gallicanus and Vetere being consuls. PEC.
EDCS-25600921.summary: This is a brick stamp or similar inscription, indicating its origin from the Agrippa workshop and dating it to the consulship of Gallicanus and Vetere, which corresponds to 150 AD, during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-25600924.translation: From the Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius, our lord. Number [undetermined]. From the Iunia workshop. Barbarus and Regulus being consuls. PEC.
EDCS-25600924.summary: This is a brick stamp or similar inscription, indicating its origin from the Iunia workshop and dating it to the consulship of Barbarus and Regulus, which corresponds to 157 AD, during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-25600930.translation: Number 53. From the new Aureliana workshop. Anullinus II and Fronto being consuls. Cut by Athenodorus, procurator.
EDCS-25600930.summary: This is a brick stamp or similar inscription from the new Aureliana workshop, indicating the supervision of Athenodorus, procurator, and dating it to the consulship of Anullinus (II) and Fronto, which corresponds to 199 AD.

EDCS-25600953.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Agrius Zopatus, a loyal veteran, lived for 70 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25600953.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Agrius Zopatus, a veteran, erected by an unknown dedicant, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-25600955.text_edition: Lucius Flaminius Decimifilius Arnensis miles legionis III Augustae centuria Iuli Longi dilecto lectus ab Marco Silano militavit annis XIX in praesidio ut esset in salto Philomulsiano ab hostem sic in pugna occissus sic vixit pie annis XL hic situs est
EDCS-25600955.translation: Lucius Flaminius Decimifilius, of the Arnensis tribe, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, in the century of Iulius Longus, chosen and enrolled by Marcus Silanus, served for 19 years in the garrison, so that he might be in the Philomulsian forest against the enemy. Thus he was killed in battle. Thus he lived loyally for 40 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25600955.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Flaminius Decimifilius, a soldier of Legio III Augusta, detailing his service and death in battle, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-25600959.translation: Lucius Silicius Optatus lived for 50 years. Intercepted on his journey, the veterans residing in Simitthu made this from their own money for him.
EDCS-25600959.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Silicius Optatus, stating he was intercepted on his journey, and erected by veterans in Simitthu using their own funds, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-25600969.translation: Lucius Atinius Primus, loyal, lived for 68 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25600969.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Atinius Primus, erected by an unknown dedicant, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-25600970.translation: Aviania Muntana, daughter of Lucius, lived for 50 years.
EDCS-25600970.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aviania Muntana, daughter of Lucius, erected by an unknown dedicant, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-25600972.translation: Publius Cluventius Felix lived for 106 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25600972.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Cluventius Felix, noting his exceptionally long life, erected by an unknown dedicant, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-25600974.translation: Gaius Cornelius, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, Primus, loyal, lived for 60 years.
EDCS-25600974.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Cornelius Primus, specifying his tribe, erected by an unknown dedicant, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-25600975.translation: Sextus Cornelius Reperitanus, loyal, lived for 50 years.
EDCS-25600975.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Cornelius Reperitanus, erected by an unknown dedicant, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-25600977.translation: Marcus Cossutius Adiutor, loyal, lived for 75 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25600977.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Cossutius Adiutor, erected by an unknown dedicant, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-25600978.translation: Gaius Cossutius, son of Gaius, Primus, loyal, lived for 9 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25600978.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Cossutius Primus, a young boy, erected by an unknown dedicant, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-25600981.translation: Gnaeus Domitius, son of Gnaeus, of the Quirina tribe, Optatus, pious, snatched away from his parents by fate. He lived 18 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25600981.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gnaeus Domitius Optatus, a young man who died at the age of 18.

EDCS-25600991.translation: Lucius Manlius, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, Rogatus, pious. He lived 55 years. He lies here.
EDCS-25600991.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Manlius Rogatus, who lived to be 55 years old.

EDCS-25600993.translation: Munatia [---] Quarta, pious, lived 65 years. She lies here.
EDCS-25600993.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Munatia Quarta, who died at the age of 65.

EDCS-25600994.translation: Mustia, daughter of Marcus, Faustina, pious. She lived 15 years. [---]
EDCS-25600994.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Mustia Faustina, a young woman who died at 15.

EDCS-25600996.translation: Papiria, daughter of Lucius, Quinta, pious. She lived [---]
EDCS-25600996.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Papiria Quinta.

EDCS-25600998.translation: Plotia, daughter of Gaius, Lapa. She lived 70 years. She lies here.
EDCS-25600998.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Plotia Lapa, who died at the age of 70.

EDCS-25601003.translation: Histonia, daughter of Publius, Probata, pious. She lived 77 years. She lies here.
EDCS-25601003.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Histonia Probata, who died at the age of 77.

EDCS-25601007.translation: Rubria Honorata, pious, lived 36 years. Titus Flavius Postimus [dedicates this] to his dearest wife.
EDCS-25601007.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Titus Flavius Postimus to his beloved wife, Rubria Honorata, who died at 36.

EDCS-25601014.translation: Vipsania Quinta, pious, lived 32 years. She lies here.
EDCS-25601014.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Vipsania Quinta, who died at the age of 32.

EDCS-25601228.translation: To the Deified Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus and Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus Armeniacus, the pagus and civitas of Thuggensis [dedicated this] by decree of the decurions with public money.
EDCS-25601228.summary: This is a public dedication from the pagus and civitas of Thuggensis, erected by decree of the decurions with public funds, in honor of the deified emperor Antoninus Pius, father of Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus (dated to after 164 AD, following their Armenian triumphs).

EDCS-25601235.translation: To Faustina Augusta, daughter of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus, son of the divine Antoninus Pius. The district and community of Thugga (set this up) by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-25601235.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Faustina, daughter of Emperor Marcus Aurelius and wife of Lucius Verus, erected by the pagus and civitas of Thugga with public funds, likely in the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-25601253.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Invictus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of his country, proconsul. The Republic of the colony of Thugga (set this up) by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-25601253.summary: This inscription is a public dedication by the colony of Thugga to Emperor Maximian, celebrating his titles and achievements, dating to approximately 288 CE.

EDCS-25601269.translation: To Appia Alexandria, most distinguished woman, and to Pompeia Appia Cincia Agathoclia.
EDCS-25601269.summary: This fragment of an inscription is a dedication to two women, Appia Alexandria, a woman of senatorial rank, and Pompeia Appia Cincia Agathoclia, likely honoring them as individuals or as part of a family monument.

EDCS-25601289.text_edition: Lucio Instanio Papiria Commodo Asicio Adiutori splendidissimae coloniae Thuggae duumviralicio aedilicio  libentissime adque abstinentissime ad sacras aures sine onere rei publicae
EDCS-25601289.translation: To Lucius Instanius Papiria Commodus Asicius Adiutor, for the most splendid colony of Thugga, having honorably served as duumvir and aedile, most willingly and honestly, reaching the sacred ears (of the emperor) without burden to the state.
EDCS-25601289.summary: This inscription honors Lucius Instanius Papiria Commodus Asicius Adiutor for his distinguished and uncorrupt public service as duumvir and aedile in the colony of Thugga, highlighting his integrity and effectiveness.

EDCS-25700352.translation: The Emperors Caesars Gaius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, imperator for the 5th time, and Gaius Iulius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, Princeps Iuventutis, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, built the road from Carthage.
EDCS-25700352.summary: This inscription records the construction of a road from Carthage by the emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus Caesar, celebrating their military victories and dating to approximately 237 CE.

EDCS-25700358.translation: Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time, paved the road from Carthage to Theveste through Legio III Augusta, under Publius Metilius Secundus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, (at milestone) 85.
EDCS-25700358.summary: This milestone records the construction of a road from Carthage to Theveste by Emperor Hadrian, carried out by Legio III Augusta under the legate Publius Metilius Secundus, dating to 123 CE.

EDCS-25700389.text_edition: Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII consul III viam a Carthagine Thevestem milia passuum CXCIcentum nonaginta unum miliaDCCXXXXseptingentos quadraginta stravit Publio Metilio Secundo legato Augusti pro praetore consule designato per legionem III Augustam
EDCS-25700389.translation: The Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time, paved the road from Carthage to Theveste for 191 miles and 740 paces, under Publius Metilius Secundus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, consul designate, through Legio III Augusta.
EDCS-25700389.summary: This inscription records a major road construction project from Carthage to Theveste, undertaken by Emperor Hadrian with Legio III Augusta under the supervision of legate Publius Metilius Secundus, dating to 123 CE.

EDCS-25700391.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare Vespasiano Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate VII imperatore XVII consule VII qui primusitionem flumin aperuit Imperatore Tito Vespasiano Augusti filio imperatore XI pontifice tribunicia potestate V consule V Caesare Augusti filio Domitiano consule IIII pontifice Quinto Egnatio Cato legato Augusti pro praetore legio III Augusta
EDCS-25700391.translation: In the consulship of Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, imperator for the 17th time, consul for the 7th time, who first opened the river channel; in the consulship of Emperor Titus Vespasian, son of Augustus, imperator for the 11th time, pontifex, holding tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 5th time; and in the consulship of Caesar Domitian, son of Augustus, consul for the 4th time, pontifex; under Quintus Egnatius Cato, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, Legio III Augusta (acted).
EDCS-25700391.summary: This inscription details a public work, likely road construction or repair, carried out by Legio III Augusta under legate Quintus Egnatius Cato, during the co-rule of Emperors Vespasian, Titus, and Domitian, dating to 76 CE.

EDCS-25900101.text_edition: Caius Iulius Quinti filius Felix Aurunculeianus aedilis ob honorem aedilitatis signum Marsyae quod ex HS IICCCCduobus milibus quadringentis nummum cum legitima summa taxaverat adiecta amplius pecunia posuit et dedicavit decreto decurionum idemque primus ludos dedit
EDCS-25900101.translation: Gaius Iulius Felix Aurunculeianus, son of Quintus, as aedile, dedicated the statue of Marsyas, which he had valued at 2,400 sesterces with the legitimate sum, adding more money, by decree of the decurions; and he was the first to give games.
EDCS-25900101.summary: This inscription records Gaius Iulius Felix Aurunculeianus's dedication of a Marsyas statue and his sponsorship of public games as an aedile, demonstrating his munificence to the community of Thugga.

EDCS-25900233.text_edition: At deas Caelestes argenteas fabricandas II ex HS  milibus nummum quae Aburnius Avillius Felix testamento suo ab heredibus suis praestari voluit rei publicae dederunt templum deae Caelestis Augustae, ex pollicitatione Gabini Rufi patris ob honorem flamonii perpetui itemque Iuliae Gallitae matris ex HS LX milibus nummum coeptum est inlatis quoque rei publicae Thuggensium ante diem dedicationis HS XXX milibus nummum die dedicationis rei publicae numeratis ex testamento Avilliae  filiae Venustae ex quorum reditu sportulae et ludi praestentur Quintus Gabinius Rufus Felix Beatianus multiplicata a se pecunia ob honorem parentum suorum HS  milibus nummum ad ornamentum patriae suae sua liberalitate constitutis perfecit excoluit et cum statuis ceterisque solo privato dedicavit ludis editis datis sportulis et epulo et gymnasio
EDCS-25900233.translation: Furthermore, (they) gave to the Republic two silver statues of the Heavenly Goddesses, from 1,000 sesterces, which Aburnius Avillius Felix wished to be provided by his heirs in his will. The temple of the Augustan Heavenly Goddess, begun from the promise of Gabinius Rufus, his father, in honor of his perpetual flaminate, and likewise from Julia Gallita, his mother, from 60,000 sesterces, has been completed. Also, before the day of dedication, 30,000 sesterces were contributed to the Republic of Thugga, and on the day of dedication, money was counted out to the Republic from the will of Avillia Venusta, his daughter, from whose income distributions and games are to be provided. Quintus Gabinius Rufus Felix Beatianus, having multiplied the money himself, with 1,000 sesterces established for the embellishment of his homeland by his own generosity, completed and adorned it with statues and other features from private land, and dedicated it with games, distributions, a banquet, and a gymnasium.
EDCS-25900233.summary: This extensive inscription details multiple benefactions to the community of Thugga, including the dedication of silver statues to Caelestis, the construction and adornment of a temple to Caelestis Augusta by the Gabinii Rufi family, and the establishment of funds for public games and distributions, showcasing the family's substantial generosity and civic engagement.

EDCS-26000920.publication: CIL 08, 15519 = CIL 08, 26475 = Dougga 00068 = ILTun 01393 = LBIRNA 00027 = Aounallah-2016, p 121
EDCS-26000920.translation: Sacred. Lucius Iulius Crassus, son of Lucius, of the Cornelia tribe, adorned with aedile rank, military tribune of the 21st Rapax Legion in Germania, prefect of engineers, duovir, augur, duovir quinquennalis designate, patron of the district, dedicated this. Gaius Pomponius Restitutus, son of Lucius, saw to its construction at his own expense.
EDCS-26000920.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Lucius Iulius Crassus, a man of significant military and civic career, with Gaius Pomponius Restitutus funding its construction, likely in the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-26001074.translation: Sacred to Salus Augusta. Gaius Pacuvius Felix, son of Gaius, made, gave, and dedicated this at his own expense on behalf of himself and his wife, Tullia Primula.
EDCS-26001074.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the goddess Salus Augusta by Gaius Pacuvius Felix, acting for himself and his wife Tullia Primula, and funded by their own money, likely for a shrine or altar.

EDCS-26001076.translation: To Imperator Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power, father of his country, proconsul, the Republic of the colony Mariana Augusta Alexandriana Uchitanorum Maiorum, devoted to his divinity and majesty, by decree of the decurions, with public money, [erected this].
EDCS-26001076.summary: This inscription is an honorary dedication by the local council of the colony of Uchitanorum Maiorum to Emperor Aurelian, celebrating his titles and virtues, and funded by public money, dating to his reign (AD 270-275).

EDCS-26001077.translation: To the lord of triumph, liberty, and our restorer through his invincible labors, for the private and public welfare, Lucius Flavius Valerius Constantinus, forever always Augustus, the Republic of the colony of Uchitanorum Maiorum, devoted to his divinity and majesty eternally, [erected this].
EDCS-26001077.summary: This inscription is an honorary monument dedicated by the community of Uchitanorum Maiorum to Emperor Constantine the Great, praising him as a bringer of triumph and liberty, and dating to his reign (AD 306-337).

EDCS-26001089.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Sextus Manlius Fortunatus pius vixit annos LXXI hic situs est
EDCS-26001089.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Sextus Manlius Fortunatus, pious, lived 71 years. He is laid to rest here.
EDCS-26001089.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Manlius Fortunatus, noting his age and piety, and marking his burial place.

EDCS-26201125.translation: Doliare work from the estates of Domitia Lucilla [wife of Flavius Probus], in the consulship of Pontianus and Atilianus.
EDCS-26201125.summary: This inscription is a brick stamp indicating that the brickwork was produced on the estates of Domitia Lucilla during the consulship of Pontianus and Atilianus, dating it to AD 261.

EDCS-26300053.translation: In these estates of Marcus Hortensius Gaudens and his sons Gaudentius, Restitutus, province 239.
EDCS-26300053.summary: This inscription appears to be a boundary marker or property tag, indicating that the land belongs to Marcus Hortensius Gaudens and his sons, and possibly referring to a specific provincial survey or tax district, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26400669.translation: Gaius Sulpicius Lupercus lived 49 years. He is laid to rest here.
EDCS-26400669.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Gaius Sulpicius Lupercus, stating his age and place of burial.

EDCS-26500696.text_edition: Tiberius Caesar divi Augusti filius Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XXXI consul III Caius Vibius Marsus pro consul III dedicavit
EDCS-26500696.translation: Tiberius Caesar, son of divine Augustus, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 31st time, consul for the third time. Gaius Vibius Marsus, proconsul for the third time, dedicated this.
EDCS-26500696.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument or public work by the proconsul Gaius Vibius Marsus, honoring Emperor Tiberius and listing his imperial titles, thus dating it to AD 29 or 30.

EDCS-26500882.text_edition: Caius Marius Cai filius aedilis signum Aequitatis sacrum de sua pequnia sic ex decreto decurionum faciundum curavit
EDCS-26500882.translation: Gaius Marius, son of Gaius, aedile, oversaw the construction of this sacred statue of Aequitas at his own expense, in accordance with a decree of the decurions.
EDCS-26500882.summary: This inscription records that the aedile Gaius Marius funded and oversaw the erection of a sacred statue of Aequitas (Equity) as mandated by the town council.

EDCS-26500886.summary: This fragmentary inscription is a dedication by Sextius to Aurelius Antoninus.

EDCS-26500906.text_edition: D equo publico ab Imperatore Caesare Hadriano Augusto flamen perpetuus ob liberalitatem HS CCCLmilium Syllae posuit decreto decurionum pecunia publica
EDCS-26500906.translation: To the public horse granted by Imperator Caesar Hadrianus Augustus, the perpetual flamen, in recognition of the generosity of 350,000 sesterces, placed this for Sylla by decree of the decurions with public money.
EDCS-26500906.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to a public horse by a perpetual flamen, funded by public money and decreed by decurions, in honor of the Emperor Hadrian's generosity to Sylla, likely dating to the Hadrianic period (117-138 CE).

EDCS-26500953.translation: Emperor Caesar [---] Annius Clodianus Albanus Muricus.
EDCS-26500953.summary: This fragmentary inscription mentions an Emperor Caesar and a person named Annius Clodianus Albanus Muricus, possibly indicating a dedication or public record involving these individuals.

EDCS-26500956.summary: This is a genitive inscription, likely indicating ownership or a dedication made in honor or memory of Quintus Granius Marcianinus.

EDCS-26500957.summary: This fragmentary inscription mentions Quintus Granius Pelagius in the genitive case, suggesting it might be part of a dedication or a funerary monument for him.

EDCS-26500960.summary: This very fragmentary inscription includes the name Felicissimus and the abbreviated letters V M P P, which are commonly found in funerary or dedicatory contexts, possibly indicating a dedication to or by Felicissimus.

EDCS-26500979.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription referring to an unnamed 'most honorable man', likely part of a funerary monument or a public dedication.

EDCS-26500983.summary: This inscription indicates the duoviral status of Memmius, suggesting he held a high municipal office, likely found on a public monument or a personal monument commemorating his civic achievements.

EDCS-26500985.translation: Of Marcus Cucullus, the most honorable man.
EDCS-26500985.summary: This genitive inscription likely refers to Marcus Cucullus, a 'most honorable man', suggesting a dedication or funerary monument for him.

EDCS-26500986.text_edition: Luci M L Dogasi honestissimi viri
EDCS-26500986.translation: Of Lucius M. L. Dogasius, the most honorable man.
EDCS-26500986.summary: This genitive inscription refers to Lucius M. L. Dogasius, a 'most honorable man', likely indicating a dedication or funerary monument for him.

EDCS-26801057.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-26801057.translation: For the health of the Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gallienus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, Germanicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, hailed as imperator for the 12th time, consul for the 6th time, father of his country, proconsul, and Cornelia Salonina Augusta... the curator of the public works built a portico for the forum, when the Emperor Augustus was establishing the colony. According to the plan, for such a ceremony, 50,000 sesterces were contributed, which also as a sportula (gift) to Thugga, through the indulgence of the most sacred Emperor, he offered the benevolence of his favor and generosity. [...] Papiria Felix Iulianus, Roman knight, perpetual flamen, of duumviral rank, from the total amount of the perpetual flamenate, contributed, after the contributions were made, sportulae and a banquet for the decurions, with 50,000 sesterces contributed to the public funds, and on the day of dedication, he gave a banquet and presented theatrical games, and to the entire populace...
EDCS-26801057.summary: This lengthy inscription records a substantial public benefaction, including the construction of a portico and distribution of funds and entertainment to the public and officials, dedicated for the health of Emperor Gallienus and Empress Salonina by an equestrian and perpetual flamen in Thugga (modern Dougga, Tunisia), likely dating to 265 AD based on the imperial titulature.

EDCS-26801190.text_edition: M Fydel et F Thesylgum pater et mater M Metusanis qui eis hec sic memoriam fecit discussi ratiocinio ad ea erogatum est sumptos mercedes in numo follis m milia praeter cibarias operantibus feliciter legant et vissitent filios et nepotes meos et tales faciant
EDCS-26801190.translation: Marcus Fydel and F. Thesylgum, father and mother of M. Metusanis, who made this memorial for them. Having examined the accounts, the expenses for it were paid out in a sum of a thousand folles, besides food for the workers. May their sons and grandsons read and visit happily and do likewise.
EDCS-26801190.summary: This inscription records a private memorial erected by M. Metusanis for his parents, M. Fydel and F. Thesylgum, detailing the cost of its construction and wishing for future generations to visit it and follow suit, probably dating to the 4th century AD due to the mention of 'folles'.

EDCS-27600038.translation: To our Lord, Emperor Constantine the Great, unconquered, pious, fortunate, Augustus, born for the good of the Republic. To our Lord, Magnus Decentius Caesar, for the 8th time.
EDCS-27600038.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine the Great and to Magnus Decentius Caesar, likely dating to 350-352 AD, based on Decentius's title.

EDCS-27600045.translation: To our Lord Constantine the Great, Victor, always Augustus, born for the good of the Republic, for the 10th time.
EDCS-27600045.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine the Great, celebrating his victories and rule for the good of the Republic, likely dating to after 324 AD when he became sole emperor.

EDCS-27600050.translation: To Flavius Constantius, most noble and most fortunate Caesar, born for the good of the Republic.
EDCS-27600050.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Flavius Constantius, holding the title of Caesar, and likely refers to Constantius I or Constantius II, probably dating to the early-to-mid 4th century AD.

EDCS-27600091.translation: To our Lord Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantius, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered, Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 14th time, consul for the 6th time, father of his country, proconsul, and...
EDCS-27600091.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantius (likely Constantius I), detailing his full imperial titulature, including his 14th tribunician power and 6th consulship, which dates it specifically to 308 AD.

EDCS-27600095.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, greatest Brit{t}annicus, greatest Germanicus, holding tribunician power for the 19th time, consul for the 3rd time, restored [this].
EDCS-27600095.summary: This inscription records a restoration by Emperor Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus), indicated by his titles of 'greatest Britannicus' and 'greatest Germanicus', with his 19th tribunician power and 3rd consulship dating the action to 216 AD.

EDCS-27600114.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, grandson of the divine Gordian, son of the sister of the divine Gordian, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, consul, proconsul, father of his country.
EDCS-27600114.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Gordian III, specifying his lineage as the grandson and nephew of the deified Gordians, which confirms his reign from 238 to 244 AD.
