EDCS-27600141.translation: Emperor Caesar, grandson of the divine Nerva, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time, paved the road from Carthage to Theveste through the Third Augustan Legion, with Publius Metilius Secundus as legate of Augustus pro praetore.
EDCS-27600141.summary: This inscription records the construction of a road from Carthage to Theveste by Emperor Hadrian, carried out by Legio III Augusta under the command of Publius Metilius Secundus, with Hadrian's 7th tribunician power and 3rd consulship dating the work to 123 AD.

EDCS-27600148.text_edition: Imperator Caesar divi Nervae nepos divi Traiani Parthici filius Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII consul III viam a Karthagine Thevestem stravit per legionem III Augustam Publio Metilio Secundo legato Augusti pro praetore
EDCS-27600148.translation: Emperor Caesar, grandson of the divine Nerva, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time, paved the road from Carthage to Theveste through the Third Augustan Legion, with Publius Metilius Secundus as legate of Augustus pro praetore.
EDCS-27600148.summary: This inscription records the paving of a road from Carthage to Theveste by Emperor Hadrian, executed by the Third Augustan Legion under Governor Publius Metilius Secundus, dating to 123 AD based on Hadrian's 7th tribunician power and 3rd consulship.

EDCS-27600149.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, with tribunician power...
EDCS-27600149.summary: This inscription records the official titles and honors of Emperor Caracalla, likely from a milestone or dedication, datable to his reign (198-217 CE).

EDCS-27600150.translation: The Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Decius Traianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland, 85 [miles].
EDCS-27600150.summary: This inscription is from a milestone, recording the full titles of Emperor Decius and the distance of 85 miles, likely from a specific point along a Roman road, datable to his reign (249-251 CE).

EDCS-27600151.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Decius Traianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland, 86 [miles].
EDCS-27600151.summary: This inscription is from a milestone, identifying the Emperor Decius with his imperial titles and indicating a distance of 86 miles, datable to his reign (249-251 CE).

EDCS-27600232.translation: To the Great and Undefeated Emperor Caesar Valerius Diocletianus, Undefeated, Pious, Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the second time, consul for the second time, proconsul for the second time; and to [[Valerius Maximianus]] Pious, Felix Augustus, with tribunician power for the second time, consul, proconsul.
EDCS-27600232.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the co-emperors Diocletian and Maximian, listing their imperial titles and iterations of offices, dating to the early years of the First Tetrarchy (c. 287 CE).

EDCS-27800149.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filio divi Commodi fratri divi Antonini Pii nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VI imperatori XI consuli II proconsuli optimo maximoque principi patri Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti principis iuventutis et Publi Septimi Getae nobilissimi Caesaris pagus Thibaritanus decreto decurionum pecunia publica
EDCS-27800149.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus Adiabenicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the sixth time, acclaimed Imperator for the eleventh time, consul for the second time, proconsul, the best and greatest princeps, father of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, princeps iuventutis, and of Publius Septimius Geta, most noble Caesar: The Thibaritan district, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-27800149.summary: This inscription is a public dedication from the district of Thibaritanus to Emperor Septimius Severus and his sons Caracalla and Geta, outlining Severus's extensive lineage, imperial titles, and the honors of his heirs, funded by local officials, datable to around 198 CE.

EDCS-27800403.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XV imperatori II consuli IIII patri patriae Lepcitani publice
EDCS-27800403.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the fifteenth time, acclaimed Imperator for the second time, consul for the fourth time, father of the fatherland: The people of Lepcis publicly [dedicated this].
EDCS-27800403.summary: This inscription is a public dedication from the citizens of Lepcis to Emperor Antoninus Pius, detailing his imperial titles and offices, datable to 151-152 CE based on his fifteenth tribunician power.

EDCS-27900251.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Castus, son of Catagus, a dutiful man, lived for 85 years.
EDCS-27900251.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a memorial to Castus, son of Catagus, who lived to be 85 years old.

EDCS-27900253.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Gaius Corm...
EDCS-27900253.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription, dedicating a memorial to Gaius, whose full name is incomplete.

EDCS-27900255.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Domit...
EDCS-27900255.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription, dedicating a memorial to an individual named Domit..., whose full name is incomplete.

EDCS-27900270.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Sallustia Felicissima, a dutiful woman, lived for 45 years. She lies here.
EDCS-27900270.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a memorial to Sallustia Felicissima, noting that she lived for 45 years and is interred at this location.

EDCS-28000291.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Anconius Felix, pious, lived 29 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-28000291.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Anconius Felix, likely set up by family or friends, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100060.text_edition: Quintus Titleis Cai filius Sarapi Isidi dedit
EDCS-30100060.translation: Quintus Titleis, son of Gaius, dedicated this to Serapis and Isis.
EDCS-30100060.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Quintus Titleis, son of Gaius, to the gods Serapis and Isis, likely for a religious offering or monument.

EDCS-30100061.text_edition: Claudius Syrapiacus votum solvit  Claudius Syrapiacus votum solvit
EDCS-30100061.translation: Claudius Syrapiacus fulfilled his vow. Claudius Syrapiacus fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-30100061.summary: This inscription records Claudius Syrapiacus fulfilling a vow, suggesting a votive offering or act of gratitude to a deity.

EDCS-30100065.text_edition: Dignae meritae ac obsequentissimae coniugi Felicitati Gaudentius fecit in pace
EDCS-30100065.translation: Gaudentius made this for his worthy, deserving, and most obedient wife Felicitas, in peace.
EDCS-30100065.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Gaudentius for his wife Felicitas, expressing his affection and her virtues, likely from a Christian context given 'in pace'.

EDCS-30100066.text_edition: Dulcissimo compari Iulio Ianuario qui vicsit sic cum suis annis XXXII plus minus in pace requievit
EDCS-30100066.translation: To the sweetest partner, Iulius Ianuarius, who lived with his family about 32 years. He rested in peace.
EDCS-30100066.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulius Ianuarius, set up by an affectionate partner, indicating his age and peaceful passing, possibly with Christian connotations.

EDCS-30100070.text_edition: Minervae Augustae  Quintus Aurelius Florentius et Aurelia Afrodite cum suis  ἀγάλματα
EDCS-30100070.translation: To Minerva Augusta. Quintus Aurelius Florentius and Aurelia Afrodite with their family (dedicated these) statues.
EDCS-30100070.summary: This inscription records the dedication of statues to Minerva Augusta by Quintus Aurelius Florentius and Aurelia Afrodite, along with their family, likely as a votive offering.

EDCS-30100101.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Gaius Gelasius vixit annos LXXII
EDCS-30100101.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Gaius Gelasius lived 72 years.
EDCS-30100101.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Gelasius, stating his age at death, typical of a tombstone or burial marker.

EDCS-30100102.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Caerellius Sextilius pius vixit annos XXII Faustinus pater
EDCS-30100102.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Caerellius Sextilius, pious, lived 22 years. Faustinus, his father.
EDCS-30100102.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caerellius Sextilius, erected by his father Faustinus, noting the deceased's piety and age at death.

EDCS-30100103.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Fausti Acerotis fecit sibi et coniugi suae se vivente annis LXX sibi et coniugi suae annis LX
EDCS-30100103.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Faustus Aceros made this for himself and his wife while he was alive, he lived 70 years, his wife 60 years.
EDCS-30100103.summary: This is a funerary inscription commissioned by Faustus Aceros for himself and his wife during his lifetime, indicating their respective ages.

EDCS-30100104.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Caius Minucius Fla  Dis Manibus sacrum Minucia Iulia
EDCS-30100104.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Gaius Minucius Fla[...]. Sacred to the Divine Shades. Minucia Iulia.
EDCS-30100104.summary: This appears to be a double funerary inscription, likely from a shared tombstone or monument, for Gaius Minucius and Minucia Iulia, perhaps a couple or relatives.

EDCS-30100105.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Namfamo vixit annos XXXVI
EDCS-30100105.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Namfamo lived for 36 years.
EDCS-30100105.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a tomb to Namfamo, recording their age at death.

EDCS-30100106.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Munnia Saturnina vixit annis XXXX Lucius Caelius Praestans filius curavit
EDCS-30100106.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Munnia Saturnina lived for 40 years. Her son, Lucius Caelius Praestans, took care of this.
EDCS-30100106.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Munnia Saturnina, indicating her age and that her son, Lucius Caelius Praestans, oversaw its creation.

EDCS-30100107.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Gaius Cannius Aris vixit annis LXXV hic situs est
EDCS-30100107.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Gaius Cannius Aris lived for 75 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-30100107.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Cannius Aris, stating his age and place of burial.

EDCS-30100108.text_edition: AL cula vixit  itus   uxori fecit hic sita est
EDCS-30100108.translation: [---]cula lived [---] made this for his wife; she is buried here.
EDCS-30100108.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for an unnamed wife, made by her husband, noting her burial place.

EDCS-30100109.summary: This inscription records that Gaius Allius Maialicus was the sculptor of the monument.

EDCS-30100110.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus Dacicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III imperator V et Caius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar princeps iuventutis Germanicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus Dacicus maximus pontes vetustate conlabsos sic et iter longa incuria corruptum restituerunt et pro sua infatigabili providentia pervium commeantibus reddiderunt  IIVX
EDCS-30100110.translation: Imperator Caesar Gaius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus Germanicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus Dacicus maximus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the third time, acclaimed imperator five times, and Gaius Iulius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, Germanicus maximus Sarmaticus maximus Dacicus maximus, restored bridges collapsed by old age, as well as the road corrupted by long neglect, and, by their tireless foresight, made it passable for travelers.
EDCS-30100110.summary: This inscription commemorates the restoration of old bridges and a damaged road by the Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus, likely dating to 237/238 AD based on their titulature.

EDCS-30100111.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Publius Licinius Egnatius Gallienus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III consul IIII  Domino nostro Flavio Theudossius sic Augusto IIII
EDCS-30100111.translation: Imperator Caesar Publius Licinius Egnatius Gallienus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the third time, consul for the fourth time. To our Lord Flavius Theodosius Augustus, consul for the fourth time.
EDCS-30100111.summary: This inscription appears to acknowledge the Emperor Gallienus and also mentions Flavius Theodosius Augustus, indicating a complex or possibly anachronistic dedication, given the consular dates.

EDCS-30100113.text_edition: Caio Iunio Faustino Placido Postumiano clarissimo viro cons(uli) adlecto inter comites Auggustorum nnostrorum sacerdoti Flaviali Titiali legato Auggustorum pro praetore provinciae Mysiae sic inferioris legato Auggustorum pro praetore provinciae Belgicae legato Auggustorum pro praetore provinciae Lusetaniae! legato Auggustorum legionis primae adiutricis piae fidelis iuridico per Aemiliam et Etruriam et Tusciam praetori candidato legato provinciae Africae dioeceseos Karthaginiensis tribuno plebis candidato quaestori provinciae Africae decemviro stlitibus iudicandis patrono perpetuo
EDCS-30100113.translation: To Gaius Iunius Faustinus Placidus Postumianus, most distinguished man, adlected consul, among our Augusti's companions, Flavian and Titian priest, legate of the Augusti with propraetorian power for the province of Lower Moesia, legate of the Augusti with propraetorian power for the province of Belgica, legate of the Augusti with propraetorian power for the province of Lusitania, legate of the Augusti of the First Legion Adiutrix Pia Fidelis, judicial officer for Aemilia and Etruria and Tuscia, praetor candidate, legate of the province of Africa, diocese of Carthage, plebeian tribune candidate, quaestor of the province of Africa, decemvir for judging lawsuits, perpetual patron.
EDCS-30100113.summary: This inscription is a lengthy career dedication honoring Gaius Iunius Faustinus Placidus Postumianus, listing his extensive civil, military, and religious offices, demonstrating his high social standing and service to the Roman state.

EDCS-30100114.text_edition: Baeatissimo saeculo Cuttilulenses  Macrini Sossiani legati clarissimi viri
EDCS-30100114.translation: In the most blessed age, the Cuttilulenses. Of Macrinus Sossianus, most distinguished legate.
EDCS-30100114.summary: This inscription from the Cuttilulenses honors Macrinus Sossianus, a distinguished legate, likely acknowledging his beneficence or presence during a prosperous era.

EDCS-30100120.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Saturninus verna hic situs est qui vixit annos XXVI dies III Aurelii Fortunatus Prima filio dulcissimo Zabullus Primulus Albanus fratri
EDCS-30100120.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Saturninus, a home-born slave, is buried here; he lived for 26 years and 3 days. Aurelii Fortunatus and Prima, to their sweetest son. Zabullus, Primulus, Albanus, to their brother.
EDCS-30100120.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Saturninus, a young home-born slave, dedicated by his parents, Aurelii Fortunatus and Prima, and his brothers, Zabullus, Primulus, and Albanus.

EDCS-30100136.text_edition: Parthici     di Auspici Felicis  cultu ornavit et
EDCS-30100136.translation: He adorned it with the worship of Parthicus, the auspicious and fortunate god, and...
EDCS-30100136.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the ornamentation of a sacred space or monument dedicated to a deity named Parthicus, likely by an individual or group.

EDCS-30100141.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Marcus Oppius Felix pius vixit annos XXXIII hic situs est
EDCS-30100141.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Marcus Oppius Felix, a dutiful man, lived for 33 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-30100141.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Oppius Felix, dedicated by a dutiful person, noting his age and burial place.

EDCS-30100142.text_edition: Sexti filius Arnensi Fortunatus pius vixit annos V sit tibi terra levis ossa tibi bene quiescant hic situs est
EDCS-30100142.translation: Fortunatus, son of Sextus, of the Arnensian tribe, a dutiful boy, lived for 5 years. May the earth be light upon you, may your bones rest well. He is buried here.
EDCS-30100142.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young boy named Fortunatus, son of Sextus, of the Arnensian tribe, who lived for five years, with a plea for his peaceful rest.

EDCS-30100143.text_edition: Genti Valeriae aeternae ddominorum nnostrorum Imperatoris Caesaris Cai Valeri Diocletiani Pii Felicis Invicti Augusti et Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Valeri Maximiani Pii Felicis Inuicti Augusti et Flavi Valeri Constanti et Galeri Valeri Maximini fortissimorum felicissimorumque Caesarum templum cum omni cultu plebs municipii Mariani Thibaritani devota numini maiestatique ipsorum et s eorum cumulata participantibus secum civibus suis votivae devotionis magno exemplo incohatum perfecit et perfectum ac dedicatum consecravit [ex auctoritate] [[[Luci Aeli Helvi Dionysi &
EDCS-30100143.translation: To the eternal Valeria gens of our lords, the Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, and the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, and Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximinus, the most brave and most fortunate Caesars: the populace of the municipality of Mariana Thibaris, devoted to their divinity and majesty, and that of them, along with their fellow citizens participating with them, completed a temple with all its cult, begun as a great example of votive devotion, and consecrated it when completed and dedicated, [by the authority] of Lucius Aelius Helvius Dionysius...
EDCS-30100143.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a temple by the populace of Mariana Thibaris to the divine majesty of the emperors Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Maximinus, undertaken by shared civic devotion.

EDCS-30100149.text_edition: Memoriae Syluuli sacerdotis inter lapsos vixit annos XXV in fide
EDCS-30100149.translation: To the memory of Sylvulus, priest among the lapsed, he lived 25 years in faith.
EDCS-30100149.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sylvulus, a priest who lived for 25 years and remained faithful, possibly indicating his adherence to a non-state religion during a time of persecution.

EDCS-30501140.text_edition: Abienii eximiae integritatis viro ac mire bonitatis exemplo Postumio Rufio Festo amplissimo proconsuli clarissimo viro vice sacr[a iudicanti &
EDCS-30501140.translation: To Abienius, a man of exceptional integrity and a marvelously good example, Postumius Rufius Festus, most esteemed proconsul, most distinguished man, judging in sacred capacity...
EDCS-30501140.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Abienius, a man of great integrity, and Postumius Rufius Festus, a distinguished proconsul and judge, likely commemorating their public service or virtues.

EDCS-30501141.text_edition: Marco Tettio Gallico Clodiano equiti Romano flamini perpetuo et Tettiae Pomponiae Cosconianae filiae eorum Iucundus servus actor
EDCS-30501141.translation: To Marcus Tettius Gallicus Clodianus, Roman knight, perpetual flamen, and to Tettia Pomponia Cosconiana, their daughter. Iucundus, slave-administrator.
EDCS-30501141.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Iucundus, a slave-administrator, to Marcus Tettius Gallicus Clodianus, a Roman knight and perpetual priest, and to his daughter Tettia Pomponia Cosconiana.

EDCS-30501142.text_edition: ae Marci Tetti Gallici Clodiani equitis Romani flaminis perpetui et Marco Tettio Gallicano Clodiano filio eorum Iucundus servus actor
EDCS-30501142.translation: To Marcus Tettius Gallicus Clodianus, Roman knight, perpetual flamen, and to Marcus Tettius Gallicanus Clodianus, their son. Iucundus, slave-administrator.
EDCS-30501142.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Iucundus, a slave-administrator, to Marcus Tettius Gallicus Clodianus, a Roman knight and perpetual priest, and to his son Marcus Tettius Gallicanus Clodianus.

EDCS-30501164.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Caius Aurelius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus et Imperator Caesar Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus et Flavius Valerius Constantius et Galerius Valerius Maximianus nobbilissimi Caessares a Tipasa milia passuum IIII
EDCS-30501164.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, and Emperor Caesar Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, and Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, the most noble Caesars, from Tipasa, 4 miles.
EDCS-30501164.summary: This inscription is a milestone or boundary marker, indicating a distance of 4 miles from Tipasa, and acknowledging the reigning tetrarchs: Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Maximinus, dating it to the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-33500319.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-33500319.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto divi Veri fratri Armeniaco Parthico Medico Germanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXVII imperatori VI cons(uli) III patri patriae proconsuli pagus Uchitanorum Maiorum aedem vetustate corruptam ex auctoritate Iuni Sabiniani proconsulis clarissimi viri petente procuratore transtulit et aquam induxit itemque fontem fecit curatore ato magistro pagi
EDCS-33500319.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, brother of the divine Verus, conqueror of Armenia, Parthia, Media, and Germania, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 27th time, acclaimed imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of his country, proconsul: the pagus Uchitanorum Maiorum, by the authority of Iunius Sabinianus, most distinguished proconsul, at the request of the procurator, transferred a temple corrupted by age and introduced water, and likewise made a fountain, with the curator and master of the pagus responsible.
EDCS-33500319.summary: This inscription records that the pagus Uchitanorum Maiorum, by authority of the proconsul and at the procurator's request, restored an old temple and installed a water supply and fountain, dedicating these works to Emperor Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus), identifiable by his titles and dating to around 214/215 CE.

EDCS-33500451.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pertinaci Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II imperatori IIII consuli II proconsuli patri patriae civitas Limisa decreto decurionum pecunia publica fecit anno Publi Corneli Anullini proconsulis clarissimi viri
EDCS-33500451.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the second time, acclaimed Imperator for the fourth time, consul for the second time, proconsul, father of his country: the city of Limisa, by decree of the decurions, made this with public money in the year of Publius Cornelius Anullinus, the most distinguished proconsul.
EDCS-33500451.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by the city of Limisa to Emperor Septimius Severus, funded by public money, during the proconsulship of Publius Cornelius Anullinus.

EDCS-33500455.text_edition: Palladi  Lucio Iunio Publi filio Papiria Proculo Feliciano flamini Augusti perpetuo municipii Limisensium cui cum ordo ob eximia eius merita publice statuam de sportulis ponendam censuisset honore muneris oblati contentus sua pecunia posuit locus datus decreto decurionum
EDCS-33500455.translation: To Palladius, son of Publius, of the Papiria tribe, Lucius Iunius Proculus Felicianus, perpetual Augustan priest of the municipality of Limisenses, to whom, when the order of decurions had decreed a statue to be erected publicly from contributions for his outstanding merits, he, content with the honor of the office offered, erected it at his own expense. The location was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-33500455.summary: This inscription commemorates a statue erected by Lucius Iunius Proculus Felicianus at his own expense, for his merits as an Augustan priest of Limisa, on a spot granted by decurial decree, after the decurions had initially proposed a public subscription for it.

EDCS-34000170.text_edition: Isi et Serapi Caius Novius Cano votum solvit libens merito  Γάϊος Νόουιος Κάνων ἀνέθηκε̣ν
EDCS-34000170.translation: To Isis and Serapis, Gaius Novius Cano willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow. Gaios Nouios Kanon dedicated this.
EDCS-34000170.summary: This bilingual (Latin and Greek) inscription records Gaius Novius Cano's fulfillment of a vow to Isis and Serapis.

EDCS-34000171.text_edition: Isidi Augustae sacrum Publius Aelius Privatus et Cocceia Bassa uxor eius aram ex voto libentes animo posuerunt et dedicaverunt
EDCS-34000171.translation: Sacred to Augusta Isis. Publius Aelius Privatus and his wife Cocceia Bassa, with willing hearts, placed and dedicated this altar in accordance with a vow.
EDCS-34000171.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar to Isis Augusta by Publius Aelius Privatus and his wife Cocceia Bassa, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-34100184.text_edition: Frenalius Venator Exsuperus Augur Volens Sidereus sic Atonitus Hieronica sic Chrysippe XS  Sidereus sic Ignaeus Turinus Mavortius Rapidus Arminius Inpulsator Castalius Gelos Piropus EugeIneIus#Eugeneus#EUGINIS Animator Blandus Sidonius Omnipotius Aquila Licinus Amazonius Imber  καρουραχχθα βραχχθαθ ηθαειθουμα νεσφομηι μελα ηιεουηεμη εσταβαηι  EIxcito#Excito#IXCITO te daemon qui hic conversans trado tibi hos equos ut deteneas illos et inplicentur nec se movere possint  καβρακκρακκρου  ρακκραρα ιρακ βραχθαβραχθαχθαη riκσονυθν
EDCS-34100184.translation: Frenalius, Venator, Exsuperus, Augur, Volens, Sidereus, likewise Atonitus, Hieronica, likewise Chrysippe. Sidereus, likewise Ignaeus, Turinus, Mavortius, Rapidus, Arminius, Inpulsator, Castalius, Gelos, Piropus, Eugeneus, Animator, Blandus, Sidonius, Omnipotius, Aquila, Licinus, Amazonius, Imber. [Greek magical words] I summon you, demon, who resides here. I hand over to you these horses, that you may detain them and entangle them, and that they may not be able to move. [Greek magical words]
EDCS-34100184.summary: This inscription is a lead curse tablet (defixio) invoking a demon to stop racehorses, listing names of charioteers and horses, and incorporating magical incantations in Latin and Greek.

EDCS-34200064.summary: This very brief inscription appears to be a simple list of three personal names, likely indicating individuals involved in some context, though the monument's nature is unclear.

EDCS-34200080.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo tribunicia potestate VII imperatori IX consuli II patri patriae Quintus Lollius Quinti filius Quirina Saturninus equo publico exornatus IIvir quinquennalis praeter alia in rem publicam et erga cives beneficia ob honorem quinquennalitatis inlatis rei publicae HS VI milibus sedem quoque domino nostro Severo Augusto designato ab ordine loco sua pecunia posuit idemque dedicavit
EDCS-34200080.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, with tribunician power for the seventh time, acclaimed Imperator for the ninth time, consul for the second time, father of his country: Quintus Lollius Saturninus, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, adorned with the public horse, duumvir quinquennalis, besides other benefits to the state and to the citizens, for the honor of the quinquennal magistracy, having contributed six thousand sesterces to the state, also placed this seat, designated by the order of decurions for our lord Severus Augustus, at his own expense and dedicated it.
EDCS-34200080.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a seat for Emperor Septimius Severus by Quintus Lollius Saturninus, a duumvir quinquennalis, who funded it himself and contributed a sum to the state in honor of his magistracy.

EDCS-34200081.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Pio principi iuventutis Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filio divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti praeterquam quod  [] statuam togatam posuit item rei publicae summam duumviratus legitimam intulit Marcus Agrius Ulpius Primanus IIvir ex reliquiis et liberalitate pollicitus basem posuit
EDCS-34200081.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius, prince of youth, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, grandson of divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of divine Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of divine Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of divine Trajan Parthicus and divine Nerva: besides having placed a togate statue, Marcus Agrius Ulpius Primanus, duumvir, also contributed the legitimate sum of the duumvirate to the state, and promised from the remnants and his generosity, placed the base.
EDCS-34200081.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue base to Emperor Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius) by Marcus Agrius Ulpius Primanus, a duumvir, who also made a financial contribution to the state.

EDCS-34200082.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Pio principi iuventutis Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filio divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti sedemL cum statua togata  et filius eius Quintus Domitius Luci filius Quirina Pudens IIvir  liberalitate pollicitus sua pecunia summam quoque honorariam IIviratus
EDCS-34200082.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius, prince of youth, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, grandson of divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of divine Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of divine Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of divine Trajan Parthicus and divine Nerva: a seat with a togate statue, and his son Quintus Domitius Pudens, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, duumvir, by his generosity promised and at his own expense also the honorary sum of the duumvirate.
EDCS-34200082.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a seat with a togate statue to Emperor Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius) by Quintus Domitius Pudens, a duumvir, who also contributed his duumviral honorary sum at his own expense.

EDCS-34200083.text_edition: Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Nervae Traiani Caesaris Augusti Germanici Dacici consulis VI imperatoris XIIII Lucius Acilius Strabo Clodius Nummus legatus Augusti pro praetore inter Musulamios et Siccenses
EDCS-34200083.translation: By the authority of Emperor Nerva Trajan Caesar Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, consul for the sixth time, acclaimed Imperator for the fourteenth time: Lucius Acilius Strabo Clodius Nummus, legate of Augustus with propraetorian power, between the Musulamii and the Siccenses.
EDCS-34200083.summary: This inscription records a boundary demarcation or official act carried out by Lucius Acilius Strabo Clodius Nummus, the imperial legate, under the authority of Emperor Trajan, specifying a location between the Musulamii and Siccenses tribes.

EDCS-34600048.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-34600048.text_edition: Ex indulgentia domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris divi Severi Pii nepotis divi Magni Antonini fili Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Pii Felicis Augusti pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate VIIII consulis III patris patriae proconsulis colonia Alexandriana Augusta Uchi maius sub eius nomine auspicioque deducta per Caesonium Lucillum clarissimum virum partes proconsulis PONTM vices administrantem monumentum ad aeternum testimonium reciperatae libertatis erexit pecunia publica
EDCS-34600048.translation: By the indulgence of our lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, grandson of the deified Severus Pius, son of the deified Magnus Antoninus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the ninth time, consul for the third time, father of his country, proconsul, the colony of Alexandriana Augusta Uchi Maius, founded under his name and auspices by Caesonianus Lucillus, a most distinguished man administering the duties of proconsul, erected this monument with public money as an eternal testimony of its recovered liberty.
EDCS-34600048.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by the colony of Alexandriana Augusta Uchi Maius, with public funds, in gratitude for the recovery of its liberty, granted by Emperor Severus Alexander, whose titles and lineage are extensively listed, and dedicated by Caesonianus Lucillus, acting proconsul.

EDCS-34800167.translation: On the 17th day before the Kalends of June, total number 59. Of these, 1 scribe, 1 optio, 21 quintanarii, 3 stationarii, 5 assigned to the bath water, 1 to the rods, 1 to Boinag (?), 7 sick. The rest will punch. On the 19th... [3] bath... Sick: Aurelius Carinas, Iulius...
EDCS-34800167.summary: This inscription is a daily record, likely from a military unit or a similar organized group, detailing the number of personnel, their assignments (e.g., scribes, bath duty, guards, sick), and some names of the sick, dated to the 17th day before the Kalends of June.

EDCS-34800170.translation: On the 16th day before the Kalends of July, total number 86. New recruits number 64, including 3 cavalrymen. 22 quintanarii, 8 at the bath, 1 from the watchtowers, 1 at the commander's office, 1 at the camel station, 1 optio. Also, Rufus 1. 4 sick. 28 excused. The remaining 26 are on public duties. The bath accepts...
EDCS-34800170.summary: This inscription is a daily roll call or duty roster, likely military, detailing the total number of personnel, a large number of new recruits, their specific assignments (e.g., bath duty, watchtowers, camel station), and the number of sick and excused individuals, dated to the 16th day before the Kalends of July.

EDCS-34800173.translation: On the 12th day before the Kalends of August, total number 44. Of these: 1 scribe, 1 optio, 1 'proculcator,' 7 at the bath water, 5 stationarii, 1 miller, 1 baker, 1 at the prison. Sick: Paccius Maximus 1, with 3 assistants. The rest will punch, 22. The bath accepts 16 and 1 assistant. The oven accepts 5.
EDCS-34800173.summary: This inscription is a daily duty roster, likely for a group of workers or soldiers, detailing their assignments (e.g., scribe, bath duty, milling, baking, prison guard), the sick, and specific numbers assigned to various tasks and facilities like the bath and oven, dated to the 12th day before the Kalends of August.

EDCS-34800177.translation: On the 11th day before the Kalends of October, total number 58. Among these: 9 cavalrymen, 1 optio, 14 quintanarii, 6 at the bath water, 1 from the watchtowers, 1 at the commander's office, 1 sent to Esuba, 1 smith. Sick: 3. Iulius Ianuarius, Iulius (a sesquiplicarius), Caecilius Rogatus. The remaining 21 will punch. The bath accepts 16. The oven 5.
EDCS-34800177.summary: This inscription is a daily record of personnel and assignments, likely from a military unit, detailing the total number of individuals, cavalrymen, specific duties (e.g., bath water, watchtowers, smith), individuals sent on special missions, and the sick, with a clear breakdown for the bath and oven, dated to the 11th day before the Kalends of October.

EDCS-34800182.translation: On the 11th day before the Kalends of November, total number 45. Among these: 1 sesquiplicarius, Aurelius Celestinus; [3] cavalrymen, Caecilius Rogatus; 1 optio, Aurelius Quirinus; quintanarii [3]; at the bath [23]; from the watchtowers [3]; at...
EDCS-34800182.summary: This inscription is a fragment of a daily roster, likely military, providing a count of personnel, specific roles (e.g., sesquiplicarius, cavalry, optio), and their assignments, including the bath and watchtowers, dated to the 11th day before the Kalends of November.

EDCS-34800189.translation: ...total number 44... Of these: 1 scribe, 1 optio, 1 'proculcator'... at the bath 3, 13 quintanarii, 4 stationarii. Sick: 2. Paccius Maximus, Iulius Secundarius 2. The remaining 19 will punch. The bath accepts 12, the oven 7.
EDCS-34800189.summary: This inscription is a fragmented daily roster, likely from a military or institutional context, detailing the number of personnel, their specific roles and assignments, the sick, and the distribution of tasks for the bath and oven.

EDCS-34800190.translation: ...15 quintanarii, 6 at the bath water, 3 stationarii, 1 at Esuba, 1 at the treasury. Sick: 2. Pacius Maximus, Pompeus Datius. The remaining 15 will punch. [The bath...] [The] oven...
EDCS-34800190.summary: This inscription is a fragmented daily roster, likely military, detailing specific assignments for personnel (e.g., bath duty, guards, treasury), individuals sent to Esuba, the sick with their names, and a partial breakdown for the bath and oven.

EDCS-34800192.translation: ...2 stationarii, 1 sent with the Garamantes, 1 custodian. Sick: 4. Farbius..., Aurelius Felix, Iulius Priscus, Iulius Optatus. The remaining 24 will punch...
EDCS-34800192.summary: This inscription is a fragmented daily roster, likely military, detailing specific assignments such as guards and a custodian, a person sent with the Garamantes, the number of sick individuals, and their names.

EDCS-34800194.translation: ...at the commander's office 1, 1 smith. Sick: 2. Aurelius Celestinus, Caecilius Rogatus. The remaining 16 will punch... The bath accepts [3]. The oven...
EDCS-34800194.summary: This inscription is a fragmented daily roster, likely military, detailing specific assignments like commander's office duty and smith, the sick individuals with their names, and a partial breakdown for the bath and oven.

EDCS-34800195.translation: Sick: 1. [—] Caecilius Rogatus. The remaining will prick. 16. The bath receives 12. The oven 4.
EDCS-34800195.summary: This inscription appears to be a military record or duty roster, detailing personnel status and allocations to a bath and oven.

EDCS-34800196.translation: [—] Optio: 1. Quintanarii: 15. To the bath water: 6. From the watchtowers: 1. To the standards: 1. To the praepositus: 1. To the rods: 1. Sick: 2. Cornelius Annibal, Aurelius Celestinus. The remaining will prick.
EDCS-34800196.summary: This inscription records a detailed allocation of military personnel to various duties and positions, including sick leave, and lists specific individuals like Cornelius Annibal and Aurelius Celestinus.

EDCS-34800197.translation: [—] Sick: 3. Cornelius Annibal, Aurelius Celestinus, Caecilius Rogatus. The remaining will prick 18. [—] The bath: 14. [—] The oven: 4.
EDCS-34800197.summary: This inscription, likely a military record, lists sick individuals, notes the number of others on duty, and records allocations to a bath and an oven.

EDCS-34800198.translation: To the praepositus: 1. From the bath: 1. Sick: 3. Cornelius Annibal, Aurelius Celestinus.
EDCS-34800198.summary: This inscription appears to be a military duty roster, assigning personnel to specific roles and recording sick individuals, including Cornelius Annibal and Aurelius Celestinus.

EDCS-34800199.translation: [—] Aurelius Donatus [—]. The remaining will prick 10 [—]. The bath receives 9. The oven 2.
EDCS-34800199.summary: This inscription records personnel details, mentioning Aurelius Donatus, and allocations related to a bath and an oven, possibly part of a military or organizational roster.

EDCS-34800200.translation: [—] Sent for bath wood: 1. Guard: 1. Sick: 4. Iulius Ianuarius [—], Caecilius [—].
EDCS-34800200.summary: This inscription, likely a duty roster, records specific tasks and mentions individuals, including Iulius Ianuarius and Caecilius, who are noted as sick.

EDCS-34800204.translation: [—] Aurelius Restutus, Iulius Donatus, Aurelius Felicianus, Terentius Rogatus, Caecilius Rogatus.
EDCS-34800204.summary: This inscription lists the names of several individuals, likely recording a group of people, possibly for duty, a census, or some other collective purpose.

EDCS-34800206.translation: [—] To the camels [—]. Sick: 1. Aurelius Celestinus. The remaining will prick 31. The bath receives 21. The oven 10.
EDCS-34800206.summary: This inscription appears to be a military or organizational record, detailing assignments, including to camels, noting a sick individual Aurelius Celestinus, and recording allocations for a bath and an oven.

EDCS-34800207.translation: [—] The bath receives 17. The oven 4. Sick: Aurelius Carinas, Iulius Maximus, Iulius Victorinus, Iulius sesquiplicarius. [—] Number [—]. Quintanarii: 19 [—].
EDCS-34800207.summary: This inscription records personnel details, specifically mentioning sick individuals and their ranks, and allocations to a bath and an oven, likely as part of a military roster.

EDCS-34800212.summary: This inscription preserves only the partial name of an individual, Caecilius Fortunatus, and provides no further context for its purpose.

EDCS-34800214.translation: Musanus... Restutus... the remaining ones will refute.
EDCS-34800214.summary: This is a fragment of a Latin inscription, possibly a record or roster, mentioning individuals named Musanus and Restutus and an action of refutation.

EDCS-34800217.summary: This is a fragmented inscription listing parts of names, likely of individuals named Aurelius, Iulius, and Caecilius, possibly from a roster or monument dedication.

EDCS-34800227.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, likely a list of names, mentioning Aurelius, Chrisogonus, and Locrianus.

EDCS-34800228.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, possibly a list of names or locations, mentioning Bubeius and Ipalacen.

EDCS-34800230.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, possibly a military or administrative record, mentioning Cornelius and Felsinus with an associated number, likely from a roster.

EDCS-34800231.translation: to the passage... and we went out... Malchus and Iulius Felix... deserted, having left bundles... Celius Fortunatus, Iulius Dona[tus], Sulpicius Donatus... Pompeius Datius and they returned... at the fifth hour of the night. Sulpicius Donatus... was sent with letters... Iulius Rufinus, cavalryman.
EDCS-34800231.summary: This is a detailed record, likely military, describing a journey, desertions, and subsequent returns of several individuals, including soldiers and cavalrymen, who were sent with letters, possibly dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-34800232.translation: Optatus... Aemilius Aemilianus, Cornelius Annibal, and according to your letters, Iulius Rufinus made an advance payment. Macargius was sent on the aforementioned day. Licinius Fortunatus and Paccius Maximus, Iulius Ianuarius, Iulius Censorinus, Aurelius Marinus were sent to Hyeru[zeria] on the aforementioned day. Iulius Rogatianus, Iulius Bonilla.
EDCS-34800232.summary: This inscription is an administrative or military record detailing various individuals being sent on missions and financial transactions, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-34800233.translation: came with tickets... and bundles... Iulius Baltunus... in... half... Titus Buzuris, Co... Aurelius Felicianus.
EDCS-34800233.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, possibly an administrative record, mentioning arrivals with tickets and bundles, and individuals named Iulius Baltunus, Titus Buzuris, and Aurelius Felicianus.

EDCS-34800235.translation: On the Nones of November, entered... lovers leading four donkeys and two Egyptians carrying letters from you and Gtasazeiheme Opter, a runaway slave.
EDCS-34800235.summary: This inscription records an event on the Nones of November involving people, possibly 'lovers,' leading animals and two Egyptians carrying letters, mentioning a runaway slave, likely an administrative or private record.

EDCS-34800236.translation: On the aforementioned day, entered... carrying three mules and four donkeys with barley. On the aforementioned day, came... Iulius Ianuarius, Octavius Fortunatus, Horatianus.
EDCS-34800236.summary: This is an administrative or military record detailing the entry of animals carrying barley and the arrival of individuals named Iulius Ianuarius, Octavius Fortunatus, and Horatianus on a specific day.

EDCS-34800240.translation: To Octavius Festus, my decurion and superintendent, Aemilius Aemilianus, soldier, sends greetings. I have sent to you, master, through the camel-drivers of Iddibal, thirteen and a half measures of barley, which amounts to ninety modii. On the twelfth day before the Kalends of February, in the year of the future consuls, after Fuscus and Bassus were consuls.
EDCS-34800240.summary: This inscription is a letter from a soldier, Aemilius Aemilianus, to his superior, Octavius Festus, reporting the dispatch of ninety modii of barley via camel-drivers, dated to the twelfth day before the Kalends of February, in the year after Fuscus and Bassus's consulship (likely 296 CE).

EDCS-34800241.translation: To Octavius Festus, my decurion and superintendent, Aemilius Aemilianus, soldier, sends greetings. I have sent to you, master, through the camel-drivers of Iassucthan, thirteen and a half measures of barley, which amounts to one hundred and eight modii. On the twelfth day before the Kalends of February, in the year of the future consuls, after Fuscus and Bassus were consuls.
EDCS-34800241.summary: This inscription is a letter from a soldier, Aemilius Aemilianus, to his superior, Octavius Festus, reporting the dispatch of one hundred and eight modii of barley via camel-drivers, dated to the twelfth day before the Kalends of February, in the year after Fuscus and Bassus's consulship (likely 296 CE).

EDCS-34800242.translation: To Octavius Festus, my decurion and superintendent, Aemilius Aemilianus, soldier, sends greetings. I have sent to you, master, through the camel-drivers of Iaremaban, twenty measures of barley, which amounts to sixty modii. On the [missing] day before the Kalends of February, in the year of the future consuls, after Fuscus and Bassus were consuls.
EDCS-34800242.summary: This inscription is a letter from a soldier, Aemilius Aemilianus, to his superior, Octavius Festus, reporting the dispatch of sixty modii of barley via camel-drivers, dated to the Kalends of February, in the year after Fuscus and Bassus's consulship (likely 296 CE).

EDCS-34800243.translation: Greetings... I have sent to you, master... Fezinis, son of S[...], seven measures of barley... three [...], consuls...
EDCS-34800243.summary: This fragmentary inscription is likely a dispatch or report detailing the transfer of seven measures of barley, possibly mentioning a recipient named Fezinis and referencing consuls.

EDCS-34800244.translation: To the decurion and superintendent, for Aurelius Donatus, soldier, you will receive from Glareus, two and a half measures of barley, making thirty modii, for the use of the soldiers staying at Golas. On the third day before the Kalends of August, when Aemilianus and Bassus were consuls.
EDCS-34800244.summary: This inscription records an order for the distribution of thirty modii of barley to soldiers stationed at Golas, to be received by Aurelius Donatus from Glareus, dated to July 30, 296 CE.

EDCS-34800245.translation: To Octavius Festus... greetings... soldier... when Aemilianus and Bassus were consuls.
EDCS-34800245.summary: This highly fragmentary inscription is likely a letter or report addressed to Octavius Festus, mentioning a soldier and dated to the consulship of Aemilianus and Bassus (296 CE).

EDCS-34800249.translation: To Aurelius Secundus, decurion, greetings. I have sent to you, master, also through your own servants, two and a half measures of barley. Let them sign the receipt for me.
EDCS-34800249.summary: This inscription is a letter or dispatch to Aurelius Secundus, a decurion, confirming the sending of two and a half measures of barley and requesting a signed receipt.

EDCS-34800250.translation: I sent... six measures of barley... received on the ninth day before the Kalends of [missing].
EDCS-34800250.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to be a record of a dispatch, noting the sending of six measures of barley and its receipt nine days before the Kalends of an unspecified month.

EDCS-34800252.translation: Emeritus and Poppidius... greetings. Know that two ships... came from different directions... at Lepcis, and another two... and a half in burden... did not meet them... receive the cares and... in the seventh year...
EDCS-34800252.summary: This fragmentary inscription is likely a report or communication concerning the movement of ships and their cargo, mentioning vessels arriving from different directions at Lepcis, and possibly indicating a problem with their intended meeting, dated to a seventh year (of an emperor or indiction cycle).

EDCS-34800258.translation: Pomponius Silvanus, decurion, to Julius Varius, knight of the Eighth Cohort Fida, sends greetings. Inquire about the tesserae in the place called Seceda. I sent tesserae to Varius and Julius Januarius at Seceda. On the eighth day before the Kalends of May. Received on the fifth day before the Kalends of May by Panus, the grain-official.
EDCS-34800258.summary: This inscription is a set of administrative dispatches from Decurion Pomponius Silvanus to Julius Varius, a knight, concerning tesserae (identifying tokens or passes) sent to Seceda, with a clear record of dispatch and receipt on April 24th and April 27th, respectively.

EDCS-34800260.translation: ...arrived... Aurelius Celestinus... on the aforementioned day, he arrived... Optantus... a tessera from... to the barley... by Volumnius... may you be well... Gallus... because... refugees...
EDCS-34800260.summary: This is a fragment of a military or administrative document, possibly recording arrivals, a tessera (tablet/pass), and a mention of refugees, dated vaguely by context to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-34800263.translation: ...asses... consuls... To Catulus, acting, Aemilius Flamininus, chief, I want you to know, master, that the refugee Aban, a barbarian, reached my men; I sent him to you on the third Ides of February (February 11th) by M[---].
EDCS-34800263.summary: This is a fragment of a letter, likely military or administrative, from Aemilius Flamininus to Catulus, informing him of the transfer of a refugee named Aban on February 11th.

EDCS-34800264.summary: This is a small fragment of a text, likely an administrative or military record, mentioning individuals named Abban and Donatus, with Abban potentially holding an acting role.

EDCS-34800265.translation: To my praepositus Julius... I sent back to the district... to Aelius, a horseman, with two letters, Donatus... Julius Maximus arrived... on the ninth Ides of Ma[...](March/May), at the first hour... well...
EDCS-34800265.summary: This is a fragment of a letter or official report, likely from a subordinate to his praepositus (commander/overseer) Julius, regarding the return of something or someone to a district and the arrival of Julius Maximus on a specific date and time.

EDCS-34800266.translation: ...you should know that the deserters Amnon and Meded... came... I sent them to you with my letters; I hope you are well...
EDCS-34800266.summary: This is a fragment of a letter, likely military, reporting the arrival of two deserters, Amnon and Meded, and their subsequent transfer with accompanying letters.

EDCS-34800269.translation: Terentius... and a decurion... of them... very... first...
EDCS-34800269.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning Terentius and a decurion, likely part of a dedication or record of public service.

EDCS-34800271.translation: to his... Pomponius Felix, on the Ides of November, I hope you are well.
EDCS-34800271.summary: This is a fragment of a letter, possibly a personal communication or an official dispatch, from Pomponius Felix, wishing the recipient well on the Ides of November.

EDCS-34800272.translation: To Luria Felici... I received letters... life through whom... whom...
EDCS-34800272.summary: This is a fragmented letter or document, likely addressed to Luria Felici, acknowledging the receipt of letters and vaguely mentioning 'life through whom'.

EDCS-34800275.translation: Augurinus to Hurtatus, his brother, greetings! I inform you, brother... first... thresholds... me at the first hour... there is another pal...
EDCS-34800275.summary: This is a fragment of a letter from Augurinus to his brother Hurtatus, containing a greeting and mentioning events or observations at the 'first hour'.

EDCS-34800278.translation: German... sixteen tesserae, of which you had six empty... sign...
EDCS-34800278.summary: This is a fragment of an administrative or inventory record, possibly concerning a distribution or accounting of sixteen tesserae, six of which were empty.

EDCS-34800280.translation: Iulius [---] / Vabius S[---] / Aelius Rogat[us ---] / Aurelius Urb[---] / Iulius Saturn[inus ---] / Iulius Ianuarius [---]
EDCS-34800280.summary: This is a fragment listing multiple names, likely part of a list of individuals such as soldiers, officials, or members of a collegium.

EDCS-34800296.translation: The seventh day before the Kalends of [---] / one and a half [---] / he restores [---] / Lucius? Cornel[ius ---] / [---] / [---]
EDCS-34800296.summary: This fragmented inscription records a date and mentions an act of restoration by a Lucius Cornelius, possibly concerning a building or monument.

EDCS-34800299.summary: This is a very small and incomplete fragment of an inscription, preserving only parts of a few lines, including the name Iulius.

EDCS-34800302.summary: This is a small, fragmented inscription, possibly a funerary text, mentioning a person named Lupina.

EDCS-34800305.summary: This is a highly fragmented inscription, likely part of a dedication or record, mentioning 'Martia' and 'Romano'.

EDCS-34800306.translation: [---]go / [---] so much / Dido / on the back
EDCS-34800306.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, possibly from a funerary monument, mentioning a name Dido and an instruction related to the back of the object.

EDCS-34800309.translation: [---]r [---]os [---]aren[---] / [---]m us S[---]LA[---]OM[---] / [---]iusero Lepidio three in number [---] / [---]S[---]REN[---]MACO[---] / [---]se where I greet you and propitiate [---] / [---]re I am with this pressing [---] / [---]tura II from Durense [---] / [---] because of opinions [---] / [---]mar[---] to arrive by right [---] / [---]n in place of barbarians, about five in number [---] / [---] forty more or less [---] / [---] are greater, the Garamantes [---] / [---] and [---] and Masa[---] / [---]at when [he/she/it] intervened [---] / [---] where are Bocus Seb[---] / [---]ias all eight [---] / [---] the masters commanded [---] / [---]robe[---] to come and [---] / [---]m to carry V[---] pa[---] / [---] / [---] my lord [---]
EDCS-34800309.summary: This lengthy and heavily fragmented inscription appears to be an official letter or report, discussing military movements, negotiations with barbarian tribes (Garamantes, Masa[esules]), and administrative orders, possibly dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-34800310.translation: [---] to Atilius Iobenalis, decurion in charge of [---] / [---]nius Saturninus, decurion, greetings [---] / [---]P[---]AT pr(a)e[---]M[---] / [---] pr(a)ef() A[---]E[---]A[---] / [---]NI[---]AME[---] / [---]T[---] whom [---] work [---] / [---] I do not want you to present yourself without [---] / I sent through Can[---] / [---]de I beg, brother [---] / how much [---]
EDCS-34800310.summary: This fragmented inscription is a letter of correspondence, likely between two decurions, with one, Saturninus, sending greetings and instructions to Atilius Iobenalis, possibly regarding official duties.

EDCS-34900999.text_edition: Imperatore Domitiano Caesare Augusto Germanico XIIII consule  iuventus civitatis Mactaritanae cultores Martis Augusti solo publico basilicam et horrea II pecunia sua faciendum curaverunt curatoribus Saturnino Arisim filio et Fortunato Lulhim filio magistri Saturninus Leptha filius Aris Chunin filius  Rogatus Addunis filius Fortunatus Arsaces filius Silvanus Mutthumbalis filius Balsillec Zrumae filius Nanes Thusabiae filius Baricbal Iasuctani filius Primus Victoris filius Gudul Mazbalis filius Saturninus Fortunati filius Cittin Selidiunis filius Ninus Iassicbalis filius Baric Sappir filius Mitthun Samonis filius Aris Arisim filius Fortunatus Papi filius Sfichinnius Mercatoris filius Optatus Laeti filius Mitthun Mutthumbalis filius Baliathon ilian filius Victor Balsamonis filius Felix Fortis filius Aris Zruman filius Mustu Musthumbalis filius Rogatus Fausti filius Bassus Maximi filius Faustus Sexti filius Ninus Baricbalis filius Mitthumbal Rogati filius Crescens Baricis libertus Titus Iaraucan filius Gudul Thilbran filius Birzic Maximi filius Maximus Optati filius Rogatus ri filius Publius Fortis filius Ialival Bassi filius Maximus Iurathai filius Mustu Zrumai filius Macarsa Quinti filius Fortunatus A--- f(ilius) Rogatus Tertullae filius Rogatus Largi Africanus Ardeumai filius Satur Birissio filius Datus Thiai filius Rogatus Faustae filius Messor Tertullae filius Satur Aris filius NovMuthumbalis filius Baricbal Fidae filius Ninus Adarbalis filius Aris Cantoris filius Vassai Rogatae filius Fortunatus Oricionis filius Sfichenius Mitthunis Vindex Bassi Birzic Zrumai filius Ciri Bassi filius Martialis Acchana filius Lucrio Sexti filius Martialis Fav filius Cinsais filius Cai Chachonis filius Saturninus Zrumanis Baliathon Tafibra
EDCS-34900999.translation: When Domitian Caesar Augustus Germanicus was consul for the fourteenth time, the youth of the Mactaritanian city, worshippers of Mars Augustus, had a basilica and two granaries built at their own expense on public land, under the curators Saturninus, son of Aris, and Fortunatus, son of Lulhim. The masters were Saturninus, son of Leptha; Aris, son of Chunin; Rogatus, son of Addunis; Fortunatus, son of Arsaces; Silvanus, son of Mutthumbalis; Balsillec, son of Zruma; Nanes, son of Thusabia; Baricbal, son of Iasuctan; Primus, son of Victor; Gudul, son of Mazbalis; Saturninus, son of Fortunatus; Cittin, son of Selidiun; Ninus, son of Iassicbal; Baric, son of Sappir; Mitthun, son of Samo; Aris, son of Aris; Fortunatus, son of Papi; Sfichinnius, son of Mercator; Optatus, son of Laetus; Mitthun, son of Mutthumbalis; Baliathon, son of Ilian; Victor, son of Balsamo; Felix, son of Fortis; Aris, son of Zruman; Mustu, son of Musthumbalis; Rogatus, son of Faustus; Bassus, son of Maximus; Faustus, son of Sextus; Ninus, son of Baricbalis; Mitthumbal, son of Rogatus; Crescens, freedman of Baric; Titus, son of Iaraucan; Gudul, son of Thilbran; Birzic, son of Maximus; Maximus, son of Optatus; Rogatus, son of R[---]; Publius, son of Fortis; Ialival, son of Bassus; Maximus, son of Iurathus; Mustu, son of Zruma; Macarsa, son of Quintus; Fortunatus, son of A[---]; Rogatus, son of Tertulla; Rogatus Largus, son of Africanus Ardeumai; Satur, son of Birissio; Datus, son of Thia; Rogatus, son of Fausta; Messor, son of Tertulla; Satur, son of Aris; NovMuthumbalis, son of [---]; Baricbal, son of Fida; Ninus, son of Adarbal; Aris, son of Cantor; Vassai, son of Rogata; Fortunatus, son of Oricio; Sfichenius, son of Mitthun; Vindex, son of Bassus; Birzic, son of Zruma; Ciri, son of Bassus; Martialis, son of Acchana; Lucrio, son of Sextus; Martialis, son of Fav[---]; Cinsais, son of Cai Chacho; Saturninus, son of Zruman; Baliathon, son of Tafibra.
EDCS-34900999.summary: This monumental inscription from the city of Mactaris (modern Maktar, Tunisia), dated to 90 CE during Domitian's 14th consulship, records the construction of a basilica and two granaries on public land, financed by the local youth (iuventus) who worshipped Mars Augustus, with a detailed list of curators and masters responsible for the project.

EDCS-35001070.translation: The 13th day before the Kalends of June, number [---] / among these, one sesquiplicarius / six horsemen / one optio / ten (?) quintanarii / to the bath water [---] / to the camels [---] / to the praepositus one / one smith / one sick person / Caecilius Rogatus / the rest will be re-punched [---] / bath eight [---] / furnace five [---]
EDCS-35001070.summary: This inscription is a detailed military record or roster, dated to May, listing various personnel roles including a sesquiplicarius, cavalry, an optio, quintanarii, and specialists like a smith, along with assignments (bath water, camels), and noting one sick person and a certain Caecilius Rogatus.

EDCS-36400004.text_edition: Hic Vibius Felix si tempora longa fuissent Felix epulis cognomine fortem neverim nam septem simul et viginti viximus annos nunc quoniam Parcae rumpunt breve fila sorores patris mente pia pariter karaque sepultus hoc situs in tumulo felix in mortem quiesco
EDCS-36400004.translation: Here I, Vibius Felix, if my life had been long, would have been called Felix for my feasts, strong by name, for we lived together for twenty-seven years. Now, since the Fates, sisters, cut short the brief threads, buried by my father's pious and equally dear will, I rest here in this tomb, Felix in death.
EDCS-36400004.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Vibius Felix, dedicated by his father, recording his age at death and expressing sorrow for his short life.

EDCS-36400005.text_edition: Hic sita est Kasta Magni rarissima coniunx quae vixit semper parce et sine fraude beata pro dolore pia est misera brevi tempore fati sancta pudicitia vixit denis quater annis o dulcis coniunx uita mihi dulcior ipsa concordes linquens generum tuumque maritum hanc aram Magnus posuit karissimae Kastae
EDCS-36400005.translation: Here lies Casta, wife of Magnus, most rare, who always lived frugally and without deceit, blessed. Pious in her sorrow, wretched by the short time of fate, holy in her modesty, she lived for four decades of years. O sweet wife, you who were sweeter than life itself to me, leaving behind your son-in-law and your husband, Magnus placed this altar for his dearest Casta.
EDCS-36400005.summary: This is a funerary altar dedicated by Magnus to his beloved wife, Casta, praising her virtues and lamenting her premature death at forty years old.

EDCS-36400006.text_edition: ius Luci filius Arnensi Fortunatus vixit annis IV hic situs est RN teneris sic puer iacet sub pondere magno annis uixi IV dulcissumus sic parentibus in V annum ascendens hic animam posui cui puero mater mama posuisse viderus sic huic puero lapidem supremum redidit ipsa
EDCS-36400006.translation: Lucius' son, Fortunatus, of the Arnensian tribe, lived for 4 years. He lies here. So a tender boy lies under a great weight. I lived for 4 years, most sweet to my parents. Ascending into my 5th year, I placed my soul here. To this boy, his mother, his 'mama,' seems to have placed it. So she herself rendered this ultimate stone to this boy.
EDCS-36400006.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young boy named Fortunatus, son of Lucius, who died at the age of four, dedicated by his mother.

EDCS-36400008.text_edition: Hic sita sedibus tot tollens o Claudia co o utinam t R frui d  SAV
EDCS-36400008.translation: Here lies, O Claudia, raising so many from their seats... O, if only to enjoy...
EDCS-36400008.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for a woman named Claudia, whose full story or epithet is lost dues to the damage.

EDCS-36400011.text_edition: tas hominum mos est bene vivere et post obitum nomen habere cara pia coniux Vaninna cum Valio Victorico sancto marito periit V LVM  NOSA
EDCS-36400011.translation: It is the custom of men to live well and to have a name after death. My dear, pious wife Vaninna, with her holy husband Valerius Victoricus, died...
EDCS-36400011.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for a pious wife named Vaninna, who died alongside her husband, Valerius Victoricus.

EDCS-36400015.text_edition: Aquiliae Quinti filiae Flaccillae c(larissimae)? f(eminae) Quintus Silius Luci filius Arnensi Maurus amicus ob merita quod beneficio liberalitate eius paternis necessitatibus exsolutus dignitatis suae conservatus est decreto decurionum
EDCS-36400015.translation: To Aquilia Flaccilla, daughter of Quintus, a most distinguished woman. Quintus Silius Maurus, son of Lucius, of the Arnensian tribe, her friend, in recognition of her merits, because by her generosity and beneficence he was relieved from paternal necessities and his own dignity was preserved, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-36400015.summary: This is a honorary inscription dedicated by Quintus Silius Maurus to Aquilia Flaccilla, likely a prominent woman, in gratitude for her financial assistance, erected by decree of the local council.

EDCS-43400822.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Flavia Domitia, indicating a Christian context with the phrase 'in pace'.

EDCS-44200073.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, greatest Parthicus, greatest Britannicus, greatest Germanicus, in his twentieth tribunician power, consul for the fourth time, father of his country, restored this.
EDCS-44200073.summary: This is an imperial inscription, likely a milestone or building dedication, recording a restoration project undertaken by Emperor Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius) around 217 AD, based on his titulature.

EDCS-44200346.translation: The Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, greatest Germanicus, greatest Sarmaticus, greatest Dacicus, pontifex maximus, in his third tribunician power, acclaimed imperator five times, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, princeps iuventutis, greatest Germanicus, greatest Sarmaticus, greatest Dacicus, restored this road from Carthage to the borders of the province of Numidia, which had been corrupted and dilapidated by long neglect.
EDCS-44200346.summary: This is a milestone or road dedication recording the restoration of a road from Carthage to Numidia by Emperor Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus Caesar around 237 AD.

EDCS-44400164.translation: The Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus, son of the deified Nerva, Germanicus...
EDCS-44400164.summary: This is a fragmentary imperial inscription, likely a milestone, dedicated to Emperor Trajan (Nerva Traianus Augustus), dating from the early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-44400337.translation: The Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, in his third year of tribunician power, acclaimed imperator five times, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, princeps iuventutis, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, restored the road from Carthage to the borders of the province of Numidia, which had been corrupted and dilapidated by long neglect. The 66th milestone.
EDCS-44400337.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road from Carthage to Numidia by Emperor Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus Caesar, likely dating to 237/238 AD, and marks the 66th milestone.

EDCS-44400338.translation: To the eternal Emperor Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix, Invincible Augustus. The 70th milestone.
EDCS-44400338.summary: This inscription is a milestone dedicated to Emperor Aurelian, dating to around 270-275 AD, and marks the 70th mile.

EDCS-44400351.translation: The Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, in his third year of tribunician power, acclaimed imperator five times, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, princeps iuventutis, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, restored the bridges that had collapsed due to age and the road that had been corrupted by long neglect, and by their untiring foresight, made it passable for travelers. The 30th milestone.
EDCS-44400351.summary: This inscription records the restoration of bridges and a road by Emperor Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus Caesar, likely dating to 237/238 AD, and marks the 30th milestone.

EDCS-44500115.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power, Consul for the second time, Father of the Fatherland, father of our lord Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gallienus Pius Felix.
EDCS-44500115.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Valerian, recognizing his titles and his relationship as father to Emperor Gallienus, likely dating to 253-260 AD.

EDCS-44900148.translation: To our lord Flavius Claudius Constantinus the Younger, most noble Caesar. The 53rd milestone.
EDCS-44900148.summary: This inscription is a milestone dedicated to Constans (Flavius Claudius Constantinus), as 'most noble Caesar,' likely dating to around 333-337 AD, and marks the 53rd mile.

EDCS-44900218.translation: For the good of the Republic, born our lord Flavius Constantinus, always Augustus.
EDCS-44900218.summary: This inscription is a dedication honoring Emperor Constantine I, likely dating to after 307 AD, emphasizing his birth for the public good and his perpetual status as Augustus.

EDCS-45600121.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, in his nineteenth year of tribunician power, acclaimed imperator three times, Consul for the fourth time, Father of the Fatherland, restored this. The 81st milestone.
EDCS-45600121.summary: This inscription is a milestone recording the restoration work by Emperor Caracalla, dating to around 216 AD, and marks the 81st mile.

EDCS-45900018.translation: To our lord Emperor Caesar Flavius Constantinus Maximus, Pius, Felix, Victor and Triumphant, always Augustus, born for the good of the Republic, and to Flavius Claudius Constantinus, most noble Caesars.
EDCS-45900018.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine I, emphasizing his numerous titles and virtues, and also to his son Constantius II, as a 'most noble Caesar', likely dating to the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-46900323.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Caius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus Germanicus maximus tribunicia potestate IIII consul III imperator II pater patriae proconsul et Quintus Herennius Etruscus Messius Decius nobilissimus Caesar et Caius Valentius Hostilianus Messius Quintus nobilissimus Caesar et Herennia Etruscilla Augusta coniunx Augusti nostri  milia res
EDCS-46900323.translation: The Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, in his fourth year of tribunician power, Consul for the third time, acclaimed imperator two times, Father of the Fatherland, proconsul, and Quintus Herennius Etruscus Messius Decius, most noble Caesar, and Gaius Valens Hostilianus Messius Quintus, most noble Caesar, and Herennia Etruscilla Augusta, wife of our Augustus… miles.
EDCS-46900323.summary: This inscription, likely a milestone, records the restoration or construction activity attributed to Emperor Decius, his sons Herennius Etruscus and Hostilian, and his wife Herennia Etruscilla, likely dating to 251 AD.

EDCS-47400210.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Vindicia Victorina lived 70 years, 3 months, 12 days. Her dutiful son-in-law set this up. Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Julia Maxima lived 30 years, 7 months. Julius Maximus, her dutiful father, set this up for his daughter. She is buried here. Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Aemilius Tertullus lived 7 years, [?] months. He is buried here. His father set this up. Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Aemilius Saturninus lived 3 years, 2 months. He is buried here. His father set this up.
EDCS-47400210.summary: This is a collection of four separate funerary inscriptions on a single monument, commemorating Vindicia Victorina (set up by her son-in-law), Julia Maxima (set up by her father), and two young boys, Aemilius Tertullus and Aemilius Saturninus (both set up by their father), likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-47400288.translation: To the Imperator Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power, father of the country, consul. To the Imperator Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veldumnianus Volusianus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power, father of the country.
EDCS-47400288.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusian, likely set up by a public body or individual, dating to their co-reign (c. 251-253 AD).

EDCS-47400346.summary: This fragment likely belonged to a public monument, possibly a statue base or an arch, celebrating the victory of an emperor named Constantine (likely Constantine I, early 4th century AD).

EDCS-48600014.translation: To Marcus Flavius Valerius Constantius, most noble Caesar, the municipality of Iulium Aurelium Mustitanum, devoted to his divinity and majesty, [dedicated this].
EDCS-48600014.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the municipality of Iulium Aurelium Mustitanum to Constantius Chlorus, here styled 'nobilissimus Caesar', dating to the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-51500074.translation: Marcus Vibius Crescens, most distinguished man.
EDCS-51500074.summary: This is a brief inscription identifying Marcus Vibius Crescens as a 'clarissimus vir', likely a senator, and could be from a tomb, statue base, or personal object.

EDCS-51500075.translation: Caste, from Marcus Vibius Crescens, garum.
EDCS-51500075.summary: This inscription is a stamp from a garum (fish sauce) container, indicating its origin from Marcus Vibius Crescens and perhaps a quality or type named 'Caste'.

EDCS-51500076.summary: This is a simple ownership or maker's mark, indicating that an object (likely a container or product) belonged to or was produced by Marcus Vibius Crescens.

EDCS-51600002.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription for a woman named Indulgentia, expressing her faithfulness and repose in peace.

EDCS-51600003.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription for Julia Monna, indicating her peaceful rest.

EDCS-51800021.translation: To the Imperator Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Decius Traianus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power...
EDCS-51800021.summary: This inscription is a dedication, likely from a statue base or milestone, to the emperor Decius, dating to his reign (c. 249-251 AD).

EDCS-51900490.translation: Marcus Aufidius, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, Rufinus, pious, lived three years. He lies here.
EDCS-51900490.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Aufidius Rufinus, a young boy who lived for three years, set up by his family.

EDCS-51900492.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Publius Anicius Baric lived for 115 years; he returned his debt to nature.
EDCS-51900492.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Publius Anicius Baric at the age of 115, dedicated by an unspecified individual or group.

EDCS-51900496.translation: Lucius Gargilius Paetus, son of Lucius, of the Arna tribe, devoted, lived 70 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-51900496.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Gargilius Paetus, noting his devotion and age at death.

EDCS-51900500.translation: Tettia Prima, daughter of Lucius, devoted, lived 23 years. She is buried here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-51900500.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Tettia Prima, highlighting her youth and wishing her peaceful rest.

EDCS-53200657.translation: Marcus? Nummius?, most distinguished man, consul, proconsul.
EDCS-53200657.summary: This fragmentary inscription commemorates a distinguished man, possibly Marcus Nummius, who held the high offices of consul and proconsul.

EDCS-56500212.translation: To Gaius Marius Fidus, son of Sextus, of the Quirina tribe, quaestor, prefect for administering justice, duumvir, perpetual flamen. The people of Emerita placed this, using the money they had contributed for his biga, because of his merits and the generosity he promised annually and perpetually for banquets, and by his will, the equestrian statues for his sons and the pedestrian statue for himself.
EDCS-56500212.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue to Gaius Marius Fidus by the people of Emerita, recognizing his public offices and generosity, and also mentions statues for his sons.

EDCS-58200010.publication: CLEAfr-01, p 74 = CLENuovo p 85 = CLEAfr-02, 00018 = AE 2014, 01519
EDCS-58200010.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Flavius Rogatianus junior lived 16 years, 10 months, 23 days. Lucius Rogatianus [set this up] for his most obedient son. After the sweet Muses, hostile to cruel fate, after the groves and springs, the woods and rivers of Maro (Virgil), this seat holds me, surrounded by a shady grove. I am not troubled that I was quickly snatched to the shades below, but that my camp and flourishing studies, as a soldier, became a deserter by the deadly law of the Fates, after my death and the ultimate initial crime. I do not cast these words with an eternally resounding Camena (Muse).
EDCS-58200010.summary: This is a poignant funerary inscription from Lucius Rogatianus for his young son, Lucius Flavius Rogatianus junior, expressing sorrow over his premature death and unfinished life as a 'soldier' of studies.

EDCS-58200016.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Fulvius Fortunatianus, devoted, lived 65 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-58200016.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Fulvius Fortunatianus, noting his devotion and age at death.
