EDCS-65600348.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Varius Delphicus lived 48 years. Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Pompeia Calla lived 94 years. A son placed this for his parents. Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Varia Prisca lived 65 years. Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Septiminius Aprilis lived 53 years.
EDCS-65600348.summary: This is a funerary monument for four individuals, Varius Delphicus, Pompeia Calla, Varia Prisca, and Septiminius Aprilis, with one section explicitly stating that a son erected it for his parents, Varius Delphicus and Pompeia Calla.

EDCS-65600349.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Vereius Sebosus lived 60 years. Vereius Felix placed this for his son. He lies here.
EDCS-65600349.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Vereius Felix for his son, Publius Vereius Sebosus, who lived to be 60 years old and is buried at the site.

EDCS-65600350.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Victorina lived 7 years, 7 months. She lies here. Devoted parents made this for their daughter. [Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed.] IRE[...] lived 4 years, 5 months, 15 days. She lies here. Devoted parents made this for their daughter.
EDCS-65600350.summary: This funerary monument, split into two parts, was erected by devoted parents for their two young daughters, Victorina and another unnamed girl, who died at early ages.

EDCS-65600351.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Saturus lived 35 years, 10 months. He lies here. Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Vinnius Felix lived 7 years, 8 months, 20 days. He lies here. His mother placed this for her son.
EDCS-65600351.summary: This monument commemorates two individuals, Saturus and the young Vinnius Felix, with the latter's epitaph explicitly stating it was erected by his mother.

EDCS-65600352.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Surus Vinius Honoratus lived 33 years, 11 months, 6 days. Helvia Fortunata placed this for her dearest husband. He lies here.
EDCS-65600352.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Gaius Surus Vinius Honoratus at the age of 33, erected by his wife, Helvia Fortunata, for her beloved husband.

EDCS-65600353.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Vinnia Bocchis lived 16 years. She lies here. Vinnia Urbana, a most devoted mother, placed this for her most devoted daughter.
EDCS-65600353.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Vinnia Urbana, a devoted mother, for her equally devoted daughter, Vinnia Bocchis, who died at the age of 16.

EDCS-65600354.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Volussena Salsula lived 85 years. She lies here.
EDCS-65600354.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Volussena Salsula, who lived to the age of 85 and is buried at this location.

EDCS-65600357.translation: [...] She lies here. [...]ninus and Maximianus placed this for their devoted mother.
EDCS-65600357.summary: This fragment of a funerary inscription records that two sons, whose names appear to be [---]ninus and Maximianus, erected it for their deceased mother.

EDCS-69000081.translation: Lucius lies here, as does his devoted wife Julia, who lived with equal age, spirit, and love. For their harmony in life delighted the citizens. Their marriage ended with Julia's death, for she was devoted first, and Lucius himself followed later. May their ashes rest with flowers, and may mother earth cover their bones lightly.
EDCS-69000081.summary: This is a metrical funerary inscription commemorating Lucius and his devoted wife Julia, highlighting their harmonious life and their deaths, with Julia preceding Lucius.

EDCS-69100244.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus.
EDCS-69100244.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Flavius Valerius Constantinus (Constantine the Great), likely dating to his reign (early 4th century CE).

EDCS-69200158.translation: For the health of Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, father of his country, consul, and Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, wife of our Augustus, and of the entire divine house, the citizens of the municipality of Aelia Hadriana Thambaiensis dedicated the Gordian Baths on the 16th day before the Kalends of January, during the consulship of Arrianus and Papus.
EDCS-69200158.summary: This inscription records the dedication of the Gordian Baths by the citizens of Aelia Hadriana Thambaiensis in honor of Emperor Gordian III, his wife Sabinia Tranquillina, and the imperial family, dated to December 17, 243 CE.

EDCS-69200159.translation: To good memory. Of Donatus Felix, vicar, who lived 40 years.
EDCS-69200159.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Donatus Felix, identified as a 'vicarius' (deputy or administrator), who died at the age of 40.

EDCS-69200160.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Surula, a slave, lived one year and six months.
EDCS-69200160.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes for Surula, identified as a slave, who died in infancy at one year and six months old.

EDCS-69200161.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Fortis lived 87 years.
EDCS-69200161.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes for Fortis, who lived to the advanced age of 87.

EDCS-69200166.translation: To the Divine Manes. Faustus Rogatus lived 81 years. His bones are here for himself.
EDCS-69200166.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes for Faustus Rogatus, who lived 81 years, indicating his burial place.

EDCS-69200485.translation: To Gnaeus Blaesius Atticus, son of Gnaeus, of the Galeria tribe, a veteran. He served 22 years in the military, lived 65 years. He is buried here. Attius Sedatus, Publius Masurius Evictus, Titus Masurius Optatus, his heirs, had this made.
EDCS-69200485.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gnaeus Blaesius Atticus, a veteran who served 22 years and died at 65, erected by his heirs.

EDCS-69200486.translation: Hirtilia Sabina's freedwoman, here, and Lelia, lived 52 years, she is buried here. Quintus Euhodius Marcellus, soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, from the century of P. I. Hospes and Ienticosus, placed this.
EDCS-69200486.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Hirtilia Sabina, a freedwoman, who lived 52 years and is buried here, placed by Quintus Euhodius Marcellus, a soldier of Legio III Augusta, and others.

EDCS-69200487.translation: Lucius Valerius Pastor, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, a veteran, lived 110 years. He is buried here. Lucius Valerius Callistus, his son, placed this.
EDCS-69200487.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Valerius Pastor, a veteran who lived an exceptionally long life of 110 years, erected by his son Lucius Valerius Callistus.

EDCS-69200488.translation: [---]nius [P]roculus, soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, he served in Legio XXII Alexandria for 6 years and in the Third Augustan [Legion ---]. He lived 45 years. [He was] killed. [He is] buried here.
EDCS-69200488.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a soldier named [---]nius Proculus, who served in two legions (Legio XXII Alexandria and Legio III Augusta), lived 45 years, and was killed, marking his burial place.

EDCS-69200499.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Saturninus, son of Cabdollo, a dutiful man, lived 85 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-69200499.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Saturninus, son of Cabdollo, who lived to a very old age, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-69200503.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Augustus, for the health and well-being of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus (?).
EDCS-69200503.summary: This fragment of a dedication, likely from an altar or statue base, invokes Jupiter Augustus for the well-being of the emperor Trajan Hadrian, dating it to the reign of Hadrian (117-138 CE).

EDCS-69200505.translation: Titus Statilius Taurus, imperator for the second time.
EDCS-69200505.summary: This is a commemorative inscription for Titus Statilius Taurus, acknowledging his military command and his second acclamation as imperator, likely from the Augustan or early Imperial period.

EDCS-69200512.translation: [...] most noble Caesar Augustus, prince of youth, and Marcia Octacilia Severa Augusta, mother of Caesar, mother of the camps, of the senate, and of the fatherland. 80.
EDCS-69200512.summary: This is a fragment of a monumental inscription, possibly from an honorific statue base, dedicated to a most noble Caesar and Marcia Octacilia Severa Augusta, likely dating to the 3rd century CE due to the titles.

EDCS-69200516.translation: Mucia Mevia Redemta, daughter of Lucius, a dutiful girl, lived 14 years. She was buried here.
EDCS-69200516.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young girl named Mucia Mevia Redemta, noting her parentage and age at death, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-69200518.translation: [...] to Marcus Aurelius Probus, Invincible, Pious, Fortunate Augustus.
EDCS-69200518.summary: This is a fragment of an honorific inscription, likely from a statue base, dedicated to the emperor Marcus Aurelius Probus, dating it to his reign (276-282 CE).

EDCS-69200520.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, grandson of the divine Marcus Antonius Gordianus, nephew of the divine Antonius Gordianus, the most brave, most fortunate, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul. 76.
EDCS-69200520.summary: This is a monumental honorific inscription dedicated to Emperor Gordian III, detailing his full titulature and family connections, dating it to his reign (238-244 CE).

EDCS-69200531.translation: Valentius lived in peace for approximately 80 years.
EDCS-69200531.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription for Valentius, indicating he lived a long life, likely from the late Imperial period.

EDCS-69200539.translation: [To the Eternal] Emperor Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, Invincible, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, our lord. 82.
EDCS-69200539.summary: This is a fragment of an honorific inscription dedicated to the Emperor Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, acknowledging his imperial titles, dating it to his reign (270-275 CE).

EDCS-69200540.translation: [...] 84. To our lord Flavius Claudius Constantinus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-69200540.summary: This is a fragment of an honorific inscription dedicated to Flavius Claudius Constantinus as 'nobilissimus Caesar', likely dating to the early 4th century CE during the Tetrarchy or later.

EDCS-69200550.translation: Pompeia Secundula, pious, lived 26 years; she lies here. Nonia Bonosa, daughter of Faustus, pious, lived 28 years; she lies here. Areia Secunda, daughter of Laetus, pious, lived 55 years; she lies here.
EDCS-69200550.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lives and burial places of three women: Pompeia Secundula, Nonia Bonosa, and Areia Secunda, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-69200551.summary: This is a brief Christian funerary inscription for a woman named Onorata, indicating her peaceful passing, likely from late antiquity.

EDCS-69200555.translation: To Gaius Flavius Galerius Constantius and Gaius Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesars.
EDCS-69200555.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Caesars Constantius Chlorus and Galerius Maximianus, acknowledging their status as most noble, dating to the Tetrarchic period (late 3rd/early 4th century CE).

EDCS-69200567.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Julia Regula, also known as Stolata, lived 63 years and 6 months; she lies here.
EDCS-69200567.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julia Regula (or Stolata), detailing her age and marking her burial place, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-70000127.translation: To our lord Licinianus Licinius, most noble Caesar...
EDCS-70000127.summary: This fragmentary inscription is a dedication to Licinius II as a most noble Caesar, dating to the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-70200497.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian, unconquered Augustus, pious, felicitous, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the sixth time, consul for the third time, father of his country, proconsul; and to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian, unconquered... consul...
EDCS-70200497.summary: This is a honorific inscription dedicated to the co-emperors Diocletian and Maximian, listing their imperial titles and offices, dating to the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-70200503.translation: To the Emperors, our lords, Gaius Valerius Diocletian and Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian and...
EDCS-70200503.summary: This fragmentary inscription is a dedication to the emperors Diocletian and Maximian, likely from the Tetrarchic period (late 3rd/early 4th century CE).

EDCS-70200504.translation: ...Aurelius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesars.
EDCS-70200504.summary: This fragmentary inscription is a dedication to Constantius Chlorus and Galerius Maximianus as most noble Caesars, dating to the Tetrarchic period (late 3rd/early 4th century CE).

EDCS-70700100.translation: Brother Minor, a secret... knowing... send with him how... denarii... I ask that they be sent to me because I hope... any from abroad... and order... for me... of oil... and ask...
EDCS-70700100.summary: This is a fragmentary private letter, likely inscribed, concerning money, oil, and other personal requests, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-70700104.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly related to a census or inventory given the mention of 'six thousand,' dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71100054.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Here lies Burpurius, once born of noble blood, exceptional in all piety. He had recently completed sixty-six years of life, but now he obtains eternal rest in a peaceful abode, as pleasing to the gods above as to the spirits below. He is laid to rest here.
EDCS-71100054.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Burpurius, praising his noble lineage and piety, and stating he died at the age of sixty-six.

EDCS-71200007.summary: This is a brief Christian funerary inscription for Marcellina, emphasizing her peaceful rest.

EDCS-71200402.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Optatilia Fadilla, pious, lived for 11 years. She is laid to rest here.
EDCS-71200402.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Optatilia Fadilla, a pious girl who died at the age of eleven.

EDCS-71200604.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-71200604.translation: For the health of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius and his children, Julia Paula Laenatiana, on account of the honor of her perpetual flaminate, built a temple to Minerva on private land [---] and for the dedication she gave donations to the district and the community [---] and to the decurions, and a gymnasium and a banquet to the entire populace. The curators were Asicius Adiutor and Marcus Terentius [---].
EDCS-71200604.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a temple to Minerva by Julia Paula Laenatiana in honor of her perpetual flaminate, along with public benefactions, for the health of Emperor Antoninus Pius and his family, likely dating to the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-71200605.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-71200605.translation: For the health of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius and his children, Julia Paula Laenatiana, on account of the honor of her perpetual flaminate, built a temple to Minerva on private land [---] and for the dedication she gave donations to the district and the community [---] and to the decurions, and a gymnasium and a banquet to the entire populace. The curators were Asicius Adiutor and Marcus Terentius Gell[---].
EDCS-71200605.summary: This inscription, largely a duplicate of the previous one, records Julia Paula Laenatiana's construction of a temple to Minerva and her associated public benefactions for the health of Emperor Antoninus Pius and his children, overseen by curators Asicius Adiutor and Marcus Terentius Gell[ius].

EDCS-71300302.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Aelia Victoria lived for 40 years, cared for by Aelius Fortunatus, her father.
EDCS-71300302.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelia Victoria, who lived 40 years, dedicated by her father Aelius Fortunatus.

EDCS-71300303.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Aulus Cassius Sentius lived for 50 years.
EDCS-71300303.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Aulus Cassius Sentius, who lived for 50 years.

EDCS-71300306.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Valgia Ingenua, most faithful, lived for 60 years.
EDCS-71300306.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valgia Ingenua, described as most faithful, who lived for 60 years.

EDCS-71300307.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Apollonius, son of Vindex, lived for 28 years.
EDCS-71300307.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Apollonius, son of Vindex, who lived for 28 years.

EDCS-71300308.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Donata lived for 22 years. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-71300308.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Donata, who lived for 22 years, wishing her bones eternal rest.

EDCS-71300309.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Fortunata lived for 2 years.
EDCS-71300309.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Fortunata, who died at the age of two, likely erected by her parents or guardians in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300310.translation: To the Divine Shades. In memory of Gaia Scubtis, who lived for 2[+] years.
EDCS-71300310.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Gaia Scubtis, whose exact age at death is partially lost, erected in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300311.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Iovinus lived for 10 months and 7 days.
EDCS-71300311.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iovinus, who died in infancy, likely set up by his family during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300312.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. The dutiful Saturninus lived for 47 years. His son Nabor [made this].
EDCS-71300312.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Nabor to his dutiful father, Saturninus, who lived to be 47, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300315.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Aelia Gemella lived for 60 years.
EDCS-71300315.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelia Gemella, who died at the age of 60, erected in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300316.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Cilia Fanent lived for 20 years.
EDCS-71300316.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cilia Fanent, who died at the age of 20, likely erected by her family in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300317.translation: To the Divine Shades. Fuscha Audnia lived for 80 years, most dutiful/sweetest.
EDCS-71300317.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Fuscha Audnia, who lived to be eighty, likely set up by her family, with an affectionate epithet, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300318.translation: Gaius Sempronius Auricinus lived for 45 years. Good sons do these things for their parents.
EDCS-71300318.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Sempronius Auricinus, who died at 45, placed by his good sons in dedication to their parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300319.translation: Quintus Menius Saturninus lived for 85 years and 1 day.
EDCS-71300319.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Menius Saturninus, who lived a long life of 85 years and one day, likely set up by his family in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300320.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Iulius Italicus lived for 22 years, and his daughter Maia lived for 1 month.
EDCS-71300320.summary: This is a joint funerary inscription commemorating Iulius Italicus, who died at 22, and his infant daughter Maia, who lived for only one month, erected in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71300323.translation: [May you] see, Diotes, [may you] be able to see many things.
EDCS-71300323.summary: This is a brief, personal inscription, possibly a wish or an epitaph, dedicated to someone named Diotes.

EDCS-71300351.translation: Let it be very fully made known to you by my will that the dispositions contained in the inscriptions, and that the sacred command cannot be made known to them, you must with greater care [write] the prices of the games and the permitted sixtieth part. If he was found bound, for investigation, to the proper tablets, likewise the Saturnalia.
EDCS-71300351.summary: This lengthy and fragmented inscription appears to be a legal or administrative text, possibly a rescript or edict concerning regulations for games, financial matters (the 'sixtieth part'), and judicial processes, likely issued by a Roman authority.

EDCS-72000079.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici Parthici Maximi Britanici Maximi filius divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepos divi Antonini Pii pronepos divi Hadriani abnepos divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae abnepos Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Britanico maximo Germanico maximo pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis XVIIII imperatori III consuli IIII patri patriae proconsuli miliarius II
EDCS-72000079.translation: For Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the deified Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva; Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, in his 19th year of tribunician power, acclaimed imperator for the third time, consul for the fourth time, father of the country, proconsul; milestone 2.
EDCS-72000079.summary: This is a Roman milestone dating to the reign of Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus), commemorating his titles and achievements and marking the second mile from a specific point.

EDCS-72000082.text_edition: Dacicus maximus pontes vetustate dilabsos iter longa iniuria corruptum restituerunt et pro sua infaticabili providentia perviam comeantibus reddiderunt miliarium VIIII
EDCS-72000082.translation: The greatest Dacicus, they restored bridges collapsed by old age and a road ruined by long neglect, and by their tireless foresight, they made it passable again for travelers. Milestone 9.
EDCS-72000082.summary: This inscription is a Roman milestone, likely from the time of a victorious emperor or governors (perhaps Trajan or Hadrian, given the 'Dacicus' epithet), recording the restoration of bridges and a road, marking the ninth mile.

EDCS-72000088.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus miliarium numero L
EDCS-72000088.translation: For Imperator Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, milestone number 50.
EDCS-72000088.summary: This is a Roman milestone dedicated to the emperor Gordian III (Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus), marking the fiftieth mile from a specific point.

EDCS-72000093.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari divi Septimi Severi Pii Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi Brittanici maximi filio divi Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Brittanico maximo Germanico maximo pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis XVIIII imperatori III consuli IIII patri patriae proconsuli miliarium
EDCS-72000093.translation: For Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the deified Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva; Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, in his 19th year of tribunician power, acclaimed imperator for the third time, consul for the fourth time, father of the country, proconsul; milestone.
EDCS-72000093.summary: This is a Roman milestone dating to the reign of Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus), listing his full imperial titulature and acknowledging his ancestry, similar to EDCS-72000079 but without a specific mile number.

EDCS-72000103.text_edition: Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Brittanico maximo Germanico maximo pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis XIX imperatori  consuli IIII patri patriae proconsuli  numero XLVIIII
EDCS-72000103.translation: For Pius Felix Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, in his 19th year of tribunician power, imperator [for the third time], consul for the fourth time, father of the country, proconsul. Number 49.
EDCS-72000103.summary: This is a fragment of a Roman milestone, likely dedicated to Emperor Caracalla (given the titles and tribunician power), marking the 49th mile from a specific point.

EDCS-72400115.translation: For Marcus Aelius Aurelius Verus Caesar, son of the Pious Augustus, holding tribunician power, consul for the second time, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-72400115.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Marcus Aelius Aurelius Verus Caesar (Lucius Verus), likely during his co-regency with Marcus Aurelius, funded by public money and decreed by local officials.

EDCS-72500070.translation: Cornelia Fortunata, daughter of Spurius, priestess of the Augustan cult, pious, lived 45 years. She lies here. May your bones rest well. May the earth be light to you.
EDCS-72500070.summary: This is a Roman funerary inscription for Cornelia Fortunata, a priestess of the imperial cult, who died at 45 years old, expressing common funerary wishes.

EDCS-72600340.summary: This is a very brief and fragmented inscription, likely a name label or part of a larger dedication, identifying Marcus Novius.

EDCS-72700002.translation: To the Preserver of our Augusti and Caesar. Egnatuleius Crescens, most distinguished man, legate of Numidia.
EDCS-72700002.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the 'Preserver' (Conservator) of the emperors and a Caesar, erected by Egnatuleius Crescens, a vir clarissimus and legate of Numidia, likely dating to the early third century AD.

EDCS-72700005.translation: The Emperor Caesar Gaius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the third time, acclaimed imperator seven times, [and Gaius] Iulius Verus Maximinus...
EDCS-72700005.summary: This inscription is part of an honorific dedication, likely a milestone, to Emperor Maximinus Thrax, detailing his titles, military victories, and magistracies, probably from around 237-238 AD.

EDCS-72700006.translation: ...holding tribunician power for the third time, acclaimed imperator five times, and Gaius Iulius Verus Maximinus, most noble Caesar, princeps iuventutis, Germanicus Maximus, Sarmaticus Maximus, Dacicus Maximus, restored the road from Carthage to the borders of the province of Numidia, which had been ruined and dilapidated by long neglect.
EDCS-72700006.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road from Carthage to the Numidian border by Emperor Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus Caesar, detailing their imperial titles and dating to around 237-238 AD.

EDCS-72700007.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, grandson of the divine Gordianus... [---] 69.
EDCS-72700007.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary honorific dedication to Emperor Gordian III, identifying him as the grandson of the deified Gordian I or II, and includes a numeral likely indicating a milestone distance, dating to around 238-244 AD.

EDCS-72700008.translation: To our Lord, the Emperor Constantius Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-72700008.summary: This inscription is a dedicatory text to Emperor Constantius, likely Constantius II, acknowledging him with standard imperial epithets, probably from the mid-fourth century AD.

EDCS-72700010.translation: To Caesar Marcus Aurelius Probus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, consul for the fourth time, father of the country.
EDCS-72700010.summary: This inscription is an honorific dedication to Emperor Probus, listing his imperial titles and consular tenure, dating to around 281 AD.

EDCS-72700046.translation: The Emperor Caesar, grandson of the divine Nerva, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the seventh time, consul for the third time...
EDCS-72700046.summary: This inscription is a honorific or monumental dedication to Emperor Hadrian, detailing his lineage and imperial titles, dating to around 123 AD.

EDCS-72700081.translation: To Faustina Augusta, daughter of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country...
EDCS-72700081.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Faustina Augusta, likely Faustina the Younger, identifying her as the daughter of Emperor Antoninus Pius, dating to the mid-second century AD.

EDCS-72700083.translation: The Third Augustan Legion marked out (the boundaries), with Gaius Vibius Marsus as proconsul for the third time. First quinternarius: 70 to the right of the decumanus, 30 beyond the cardo 350.
EDCS-72700083.summary: This inscription records a centuriation or boundary demarcation performed by Legio III Augusta under the proconsulship of Gaius Vibius Marsus, providing specific measurements for land division, likely dating to the first or second century AD.

EDCS-72700085.translation: Pompeianus, faithful, lived in peace for 8 years, buried on the day before the Ides of May, in the 9th indiction.
EDCS-72700085.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Pompeianus, recording his age at death and the date of his burial according to the Roman calendar and indiction cycle, dating to the Late Antique period (likely 5th-6th century AD).

EDCS-72700086.translation: Pompeianus, faithful, lived in peace for 1 year, 6 months. He rests on the 3rd day before the Ides of December, in the 2nd indiction.
EDCS-72700086.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Pompeianus, recording his age at death and the date of his burial (December 11th), likely from the late Roman period given the indiction date.

EDCS-72700087.translation: Pompeianus, faithful, lived in peace for 45 years [and 3 months?]. He was laid to rest on the day before the Nones of May, in the [first?] indiction [of ...].
EDCS-72700087.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Pompeianus, detailing his age and the date of his burial (May 6th), with the specific indiction year largely lost.

EDCS-72700097.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Aurelius Restutus lived for 35 years.
EDCS-72700097.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Lucius Aurelius Restutus, recording his name and age at death.

EDCS-72700098.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Marcus Iunius Rogatianus lived for 3 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-72700098.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Iunius Rogatianus, a child who died at the age of three, indicating his burial place.

EDCS-72700099.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. [Avielius] Maximus lived for 18 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-72700099.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for [Avielius] Maximus, stating he lived for 18 years and is buried at this spot.

EDCS-72700100.translation: Sextus Marius Pudens, son of Titus, of the Pollia tribe, lived for 21 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-72700100.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Marius Pudens, identifying him by name, filiation, tribe, and age at death, and marking his burial place.

EDCS-72700101.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Donatus, son of Siddinis, lived for 10 years. He is laid and buried here.
EDCS-72700101.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Donatus, the son of Siddinis, recording that he lived to be 10 years old and is buried at this location.

EDCS-72700102.translation: Hotmilcot Arinis, wife of Labio, lived for 71 years. She is laid here.
EDCS-72700102.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Hotmilcot Arinis, identifying her as Labio's wife and stating that she lived for 71 years and is buried here.

EDCS-72700103.translation: Munda, son of Aechanaes, lived for 75 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-72700103.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Munda, identified as the son of Aechanaes, stating he lived for 75 years and is buried at this spot.

EDCS-72700104.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Sattaras, son of Alauchus, lived for 61 years. He is laid here.
EDCS-72700104.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sattaras, son of Alauchus, recording his name, filiation, and age at death, and indicating his burial place.

EDCS-72700105.translation: Amot, the pious daughter of Rusticus and Asmunis Maca, lived 26 years.
EDCS-72700105.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Amot, dedicated by or on behalf of her parents, recording her age at death, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-72700106.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Coloap, the pious son of Fronto, lived 83 years. He lies here.
EDCS-72700106.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Coloap, son of Fronto, noting his piety and age at death, and indicating his burial place, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-72700107.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Tulius Rogatus lived 68 years. He lies here.
EDCS-72700107.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Tulius Rogatus, recording his name and age at death, and indicating his burial place, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-72700108.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Valeria Lathinia lived 51 years. She lies here.
EDCS-72700108.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valeria Lathinia, stating her name, age at death, and place of burial, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-72700109.translation: Publius Aufidius Saturninus, a veteran, had two columns made at his own expense.
EDCS-72700109.summary: This inscription records that Publius Aufidius Saturninus, a veteran, financed the creation of two columns, indicating a public or private construction project during the imperial period.

EDCS-72700110.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Nonius Lucius lived 75 years.
EDCS-72700110.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Nonius Lucius, recording his name and age at death, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-72700111.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Nonia Aurelia lived 82 years.
EDCS-72700111.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Nonia Aurelia, noting her name and age at death, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-72700112.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Modia Victoria, pious, lived 31 years. She lies here.
EDCS-72700112.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Modia Victoria, highlighting her piety and age at death, and indicating her burial location, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-72700113.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Baricbal Prima Serruma, pious, lived 40 years.
EDCS-72700113.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Baricbal Prima Serruma, recording their piety and age at death, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-72700116.translation: To Domitia Aurelia Faustina, daughter of Aurelius Caesar, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-72700116.summary: This inscription commemorates Domitia Aurelia Faustina, likely a member of the imperial family, indicating a public dedication funded by the decurions (local council) with public money, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-73900281.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Felix, son of Macrinus Menotis, lived piously for 73 years. Likewise Maior, daughter of Extricatus, lived piously for 68 years.
EDCS-73900281.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Felix, son of Macrinus Menotis, and Maior, daughter of Extricatus, who lived to advanced ages, likely set up by their family.

EDCS-73900282.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Aulus Egnatius Mulinus lived piously for 45 years. He is laid to rest here.
EDCS-73900282.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aulus Egnatius Mulinus, who died at the age of 45, likely erected by his family or friends.

EDCS-73900284.translation: From the workshop of Modius Julianus.
EDCS-73900284.summary: This short inscription indicates that an object or monument was produced in the workshop of Modius Julianus, likely a craftsman or manufacturer.

EDCS-73900285.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Augustus. To Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, acclaimed Imperator, consul for the 3rd time, father of his country, son of the divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the divine Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the divine Nerva. Gaetulicus, for the honor of a duplicated flamoniate, promised 2,000 sesterces, then amplified this sum to 4,000 sesterces for the work, and further for the decurionatus, he built a temple with a marble statue, marble columns, ornaments, and all its embellishments, and the steps of the podium. He also promised and paid for an apophysis of alder wood instead of two columns and on the day of the feast and dedication, he gave handouts to the decurions for the dedication, and a gymnasium and games to all the people.
EDCS-73900285.summary: This elaborate inscription records the dedication of a temple with a marble statue and columns to Jupiter Augustus by Gaetulicus, in honor of Emperor Commodus (circa 180-185 AD), as well as Gaetulicus's generous gifts to the community for his various public offices.

EDCS-73900431.translation: To our most peaceful Emperor Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, the Undefeated, Pious, Fortunate Augustus. 94 miles.
EDCS-73900431.summary: This is a milestone, likely erected during the reign of Emperor Aurelian (270-275 AD), indicating a distance of 94 miles and honoring the emperor.

EDCS-74000013.translation: To the Emperors Caesars Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and [[Publius]] Septimius [[Geta]] Caesar Augustus, and to Julia Augusta, mother of the camps, a cistern.
EDCS-74000013.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a cistern to the imperial family of Septimius Severus, Caracalla, Geta (whose name was later erased as part of a damnatio memoriae), and Julia Domna, likely dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-74100259.translation: Sacred to Mars Augustus, for the well-being of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Adiabenicus. The curia Martis built and dedicated this shrine from the ground up and a statue with their own money, on granted land.
EDCS-74100259.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a shrine and a statue to Mars Augustus by the 'curia Martis' for the well-being of Emperor Septimius Severus (circa 193-211 AD).

EDCS-74200160.translation: Faunus, Oricimuicius, Leontius, Spina, [---]ecta, Pinnatus, Auspicator, Eventus.
EDCS-74200160.summary: This inscription lists a series of names, possibly indicating individuals involved in a specific group, a dedication, or perhaps gladiators or participants in some event.

EDCS-74700031.translation: By the authority of Quintus Junius Rusticus, Prefect of the City, most distinguished man. Ten pounds.
EDCS-74700031.summary: This inscription indicates an action carried out under the authority of Quintus Junius Rusticus, the Prefect of the City, and notes a weight or value of ten pounds, likely for a public work or regulation (circa mid-2nd century AD).

EDCS-74700522.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, grandson of the divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the divine Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, and great-great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan and the divine Nerva. To Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus Germanicus.
EDCS-74700522.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Caracalla, listing his extensive imperial titles and his prestigious lineage through adoptive and blood relations to past divine emperors, likely dating to his sole reign (circa 212-217 AD).

EDCS-74700526.translation: To Philippus, with tribunician power, consul-designate for the third time, father of the country, proconsul; and to Marcus Iulius Philippus, most noble Caesar Augustus, prince of youth; and to Marcia Otacilia Severa Augusta, mother of Caesar Augustus and of the camps and of the senate and of the country. 78.
EDCS-74700526.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Philip the Arab, his son Marcus Iulius Philippus, and his wife Marcia Otacilia Severa, likely dating to the later part of Philip's reign (around 247-249 CE) given the mention of his third consulship.

EDCS-75200151.translation: ... as James the Apostle said: 'Pray for one another'.
EDCS-75200151.summary: This fragmentary inscription contains a quote from the Epistle of James, indicating a Christian context, possibly from a funerary monument or a church setting.

EDCS-75300322.translation: To our Lords the Emperors Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, Pious, Fortunate, Undefeated Augusti, consuls for the fifth time.
EDCS-75300322.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the co-emperors Constantius Chlorus and Galerius, commemorating their fifth consulship and thereby dating the monument to 300 CE.

EDCS-75300323.translation: Of Valerius Pudens Marsillianus, most distinguished man, A.F.T.H.
EDCS-75300323.summary: This fragmentary inscription mentions Valerius Pudens Marsillianus, a 'clarissimus vir' (a man of senatorial rank), likely part of a dedicatory or funerary monument.

EDCS-75300325.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, with tribunician power for the 18th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, restored [this].
EDCS-75300325.summary: This inscription records a restoration project undertaken by Emperor Caracalla, indicating a date of 215 CE based on his tribunician power and consulships.

EDCS-75300331.translation: To the Emperors Caesars Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Invictus Augustus, for the 4th time, and Marcus Aurelius...
EDCS-75300331.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperors Diocletian and Marcus Aurelius (likely Maximian), noting Diocletian's fourth consulship, placing it around 290 CE.

EDCS-75300332.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, with tribunician power for the 19th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, established [this]. 2.
EDCS-75300332.summary: This inscription records an establishment (or construction) by Emperor Caracalla, dating to 216 CE based on his 19th tribunician power and 4th consulship.

EDCS-75300335.translation: To our Lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Pious, Fortunate, Undefeated Augustus, born for the good of the Republic. 10.
EDCS-75300335.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine the Great, praising him as 'born for the good of the Republic', likely dating to the period of his sole or co-rule.

EDCS-75300336.translation: To our Lords the Emperors Gaius Valerius Diocletianus, Pious, Fortunate, Most Undefeated Augusti, consuls...
EDCS-75300336.summary: This fragmentary inscription is a dedication to the co-emperors Diocletian and Maximian, referring to them as 'most undefeated Augusti' and mentioning their consulships.

EDCS-75300337.translation: To our Lord Licinius Licinianus, Pious, Fortunate Augustus.
EDCS-75300337.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Licinius, a contemporary and rival of Constantine the Great, likely dating from his reign (308-324 CE).

EDCS-75300338.translation: To Flavius Valerius Crispus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-75300338.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Flavius Valerius Crispus, a Roman Caesar, likely dating to the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-75300339.translation: To our Lord Flavius Julius Constantius, most noble and flourishing Caesar, born for the good of the republic.
EDCS-75300339.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Constantius II, celebrating him as a Caesar born for the good of the state, likely dating to the first half of the 4th century CE.

EDCS-75300341.translation: To the propitious god Neptune, they built a temple at their own expense, under the direction of [---] Tertius, master.
EDCS-75300341.summary: This inscription records the construction of a temple to the god Neptune, built at the expense of a group, under the supervision of a master named Tertius.

EDCS-75300350.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, grandson of the deified Gordian, son of the deified Gordian's sister, Pius, Felix, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus...
EDCS-75300350.summary: This inscription identifies the Roman Emperor Gordian III, detailing his lineage and imperial titles, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-75300352.summary: This inscription simply lists the name Tiberius Claudius, likely part of a larger monument or a simple identification.

EDCS-75300353.translation: To Aulus Gabinius Dato, son of Aulus, of the Arnensis tribe, flamen of the deified Titus, inducted into the five decuriae by Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, patron of the pagus and the civitas; the pagus and civitas Thuggensis erected this at their own expense.
EDCS-75300353.summary: This inscription is an honorary dedication by the pagus and civitas of Thuggensis to Aulus Gabinius Dato, a local notable and priest of the deified Titus, who was patron of the community and adlected into the decuriae by Emperor Hadrian, likely dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-75300356.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Titus Flavius Venustus, freedman of Emperor Domitian, who lived 80 years without torment. Chrysanthus, imperial dispensator, made this for his well-deserving friend.
EDCS-75300356.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Titus Flavius Venustus, a freedman of Emperor Domitian, set up by his friend Chrysanthus, an imperial dispensator, likely dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-75300359.translation: The Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus, Pius, Felix, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, in his second tribunician power, father of his country, consul designate for the second time, proconsul, and Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veldumnianus Volusianus...
EDCS-75300359.summary: This inscription honors the Roman Emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus, detailing their imperial titles, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-75300361.translation: To our Lord Flavius Valerius Constantine, Pius, Felix, always victorious Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, in his 15th tribunician power, 6th consulship, 14th imperatorial acclamation, father of his country, proconsul; the order of the municipality of Ziccili erected this through its own generosity, with Marcus Lutatius Luzanus, son of Marcus, curator of the state, overseeing and dedicating it.
EDCS-75300361.summary: This inscription is a dedication from the municipality of Ziccili to Emperor Constantine the Great, celebrating his many titles and achievements, erected under the curatorship of Marcus Lutatius Luzanus, likely dating to the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-75400168.translation: To the Prince of Youth, Flavius Valerius Constantius, most noble Caesar; the colonia Aelia Hadriana Augusta Bullensium Regiorum, devoted to his divinity and majesty, [erected this].
EDCS-75400168.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the Roman colony Aelia Hadriana Augusta Bullensium Regiorum to Flavius Valerius Constantius, the Prince of Youth and noble Caesar, likely Constantius II, from the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-75700194.translation: Titia, daughter of [---], Namphame, pious, lived 60 years. She is buried here. May your bones rest well; may the earth be light for you.
EDCS-75700194.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titia Namphame, a pious woman who lived 60 years, likely from the Imperial period based on formulaic blessings.

EDCS-75700196.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning Gaius Cornelius Fuscus and potentially a co-dedicant, Aemilius or Aemilia, likely part of a larger dedication or funerary monument.

EDCS-75700197.translation: Gaius Cesennius Secundus, pious, lived 75 years. [---]
EDCS-75700197.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Cesennius Secundus, a pious man who lived 75 years, suggesting a private tomb or monument.

EDCS-75700199.translation: Gaius Rasinius Faustus, veteran, pious, lived L[---] years. He is buried here.
EDCS-75700199.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Rasinius Faustus, a pious veteran who lived over 50 years, marking his burial place.

EDCS-75700200.translation: [---] Manlius, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, Receptus, aedile, duumvir, perpetual flamen, lived [---] years.
EDCS-75700200.summary: This is a funerary inscription or public monument for Manlius Receptus, who held a series of important municipal and religious offices (aedile, duumvir, perpetual flamen), indicating his prominent status in the community.

EDCS-75700201.translation: Cornelia Fausta, daughter of Sextus, pious, lived 26 years.
EDCS-75700201.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cornelia Fausta, a pious woman who lived 26 years, likely a private memorial from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75700202.summary: This is a fragmented inscription naming Titus Veturius, likely part of a larger dedication or funerary monument.

EDCS-75700203.translation: Capito, son of Cossutus, lived [---] years.
EDCS-75700203.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription for Capito, son of Cossutus, indicating a personal memorial.

EDCS-75700205.translation: May your bones rest well. Iulia Rosa, daughter of Gaius, pious, lived 100 years. She is buried here. May the earth be light for you.
EDCS-75700205.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Rosa, a pious woman who lived an impressive 100 years, with standard burial blessings, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-75700206.translation: Sextus Pompeius, son of Sextus, of the Quirina tribe, R[---], lived 75 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-75700206.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Pompeius, who lived 75 years and belonged to the Quirina tribe, marking his burial place.

EDCS-75700207.translation: Gargilia Zara, dutiful, lived 35 years. She lies here.
EDCS-75700207.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gargilia Zara, a woman who lived 35 years.

EDCS-75700208.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gaius Pontius, son of [---], Umbrianus, a veteran of the First [---], served [---] years, lived [---] years. He lies here.
EDCS-75700208.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes for Gaius Pontius Umbrianus, a veteran, likely from the 1st or I Legion, who died at an unspecified age after serving an unspecified number of years.

EDCS-75900077.text_edition: Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate XVI consule III et Imperatore Caesare Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto tribunicia potestate II consule II aedem Apollinis pecunia publica facta Quinto Voconio Saxa Fido proconsule Marcus Ummidius Annianus Quadratianus legatus fecit idemque dedicavit
EDCS-75900077.translation: When Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus was Pontifex Maximus, in his 16th tribunician power, consul for the third time, and Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus was in his 2nd tribunician power, consul for the second time, the temple of Apollo was built with public money. Quintus Voconius Saxa Fidus being proconsul, Marcus Ummidius Annianus Quadratianus, the legate, made and dedicated the same.
EDCS-75900077.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a temple to Apollo, funded by public money, by the legate Marcus Ummidius Annianus Quadratianus during the joint reign of Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, specifically between December 10, 161 AD, and December 9, 162 AD.

EDCS-76300055.translation: To Caecilius Secundus, son of Secundus, honored with the public horse, prefect of the first cohort of Thracian Roman citizens, perpetual flamen. Marcus Caecilius Honoratus [made this] for his dearest brother. The place was given by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-76300055.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Marcus Caecilius Honoratus to his dearest brother, Caecilius Secundus, who held various public and religious offices, including prefect of a Thracian cohort and perpetual flamen, with the burial plot granted by decree of the decurions.

EDCS-77400004.translation: Publius Annius Fuscus Masurius, son of Lucius Annius Constans, lived 15 years.
EDCS-77400004.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Annius Fuscus Masurius, who died at the age of 15.

EDCS-77400005.translation: Gaius Selius Felix made this for himself and his family.
EDCS-77400005.summary: This inscription records that Gaius Selius Felix constructed a monument for himself and his family.

EDCS-77400006.summary: This inscription records the names Publius Fa[---] and Publius Faustinus Rufus, likely from a funerary or dedicatory context.

EDCS-77400008.summary: This inscription simply records the name of Flavia Aponia Saloninula, likely from a funerary or dedicatory monument.

EDCS-77400017.translation: Sabbatiolus, also known as Iuventinus, placed in peace.
EDCS-77400017.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription for Sabbatiolus, also known as Iuventinus, indicating his burial.

EDCS-77700153.translation: Publius Popillius Neo, freedman of Publius, silversmith, lies here.
EDCS-77700153.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Popillius Neo, a freedman and silversmith, indicating his place of burial.

EDCS-78300373.translation: Victor, son of Nabor, fulfilled his vow willingly and with a cheerful mind, having undertaken it.
EDCS-78300373.summary: This inscription records Victor, son of Nabor, fulfilling a vow, likely to a deity.

EDCS-78300376.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. [...]adius lived for 25 years.
EDCS-78300376.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a person named [...]adius, who lived for 25 years.

EDCS-78300379.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. [...] Veturius Speratus placed this statue for Fabricia Donatilla, his most beloved wife, because of her merits. She lived for 27 years.
EDCS-78300379.summary: This is a funerary inscription and monument dedicated by Veturius Speratus to his beloved wife, Fabricia Donatilla, who lived for 27 years.

EDCS-78300380.translation: Sacred to Mercury Sobrius Augustus. For the well-being of Hadrian Augustus, Ianuarius Merulae willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-78300380.summary: This inscription records Ianuarius Merulae fulfilling a vow to Mercury Sobrius Augustus for the health of Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-78300383.translation: Sacred to Neptune and the Augustan Nymphs. The city of Thabborensis, with the sufetes Secundus, son of Zdrubal, and Verus, son of Marcellus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-78300383.summary: This inscription records the city of Thabborensis, led by two sufetes, dedicating a monument to Neptune and the Augustan Nymphs in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-78300391.summary: This is a fragment of a dedication, likely to the Emperor Severus.

EDCS-78300394.translation: Lucius Gavius Felix and Valeria Isaru had this made at their own expense for the honor of the perpetual flamonate in the Roman district.
EDCS-78300394.summary: This inscription records Lucius Gavius Felix and Valeria Isaru funding the construction of something to honor the perpetual flamonate in the Roman district.

EDCS-78300395.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Corfidia Tertia lived for 110 years; she lies here. Felicius and Fortunatus placed this for their devoted mother.
EDCS-78300395.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Felicius and Fortunatus to their devoted mother, Corfidia Tertia, who lived to the age of 110.
